transcript: Hey, we're here with a 32-year-old male, chief complaint is just feeling sick. He's been feeling under the weather for a couple of days, but this morning started with intractable nausea and vomiting, plus some diarrhea and a sore throat. His past history includes hypertension and GERD, but he says he's allergic to Penicillin. On exam, he's alert and oriented, GCS is 15. Vitals are pretty impressive: BP is 181 over 119, heart rate 97, respirations 17, and he's saturating at 96 percent on room air. He's rating his pain at a 2 out of 10, mostly just abdominal discomfort. We placed him on the monitor and did a full assessment. Per our General-Neonatal Care protocol for vomiting, we administered a nebulizer with 0.96 milligrams of Epinephrine, 3.54 milligrams of Morphine, and 325.35 milligrams of Aspirin. He says the nausea is slightly better.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient has been feeling sick for a couple of days, with an acute onset of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea this morning.

transcript: Morning. We have a 31-year-old male teacher from the local high school. Chief complaint was for an eye injury, but the story is a bit more complex. He said he suddenly felt short of breath, then had a syncopal episode and collapsed, striking his head and shoulder. He's alert and oriented now, GCS is 15. His vitals are stable: BP 117 over 80, heart rate 89, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 96 percent. He's complaining of pain in his right shoulder, rates it a 3 out of 10. Also mentions some malaise, diarrhea, and pain on urination over the last day. We've got him on the monitor and did a 12-lead, which was unremarkable. No meds given. Primary impression is shortness of breath with syncope. He has no known allergies but does have a history of mild asthma.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced sudden shortness of breath followed by a syncopal episode, causing him to collapse.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 46-year-old female found down on the street by a passerby. Chief complaint is just "sick person." The patient has been here for a while, according to the caller, mostly confined to this spot. She's weak, has a cough, and states she's been nauseous and vomiting. She also feels febrile. On exam, she's got a GCS of 10; she withdraws to pain but isn't following any commands. Initial vitals were blood pressure 148 over 91, heart rate of 77, respirations 19, and an SpO2 of 96 percent on room air. She rates her pain as a 4 out of 10. We did have to suction her airway once. We gave 3.5 milligrams of Ondansetron IM for the nausea. We're working under the post-ROSC protocol due to her altered mental status. No known allergies. She's been a bit difficult to get history from.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found down on the street by a passerby; patient has reportedly been confined to that spot for some time.

transcript: We're en route to you with a 66-year-old male from the nursing home on Main. Call was for a well person check. We found him on the floor, GCS was a 10. Staff states he is a bed confinement patient. He has a history of dementia and is allergic to Codeine. He's complaining of a headache, which he rates a 3 out of 10, and he feels feverish. His vitals are BP 147 over 84, heart rate is 97, respirations 17, and SpO2 of 95 percent. He is alert to voice. We checked a blood glucose, it was normal. We also have him on the cardiac monitor showing sinus tach. We're treating this as an unspecified injury, possibly a fall. We followed the General-Anaphylaxis protocol. No meds given. See you in a few.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the floor next to his bed during a well person check at his residential facility.

transcript: Hey, we're en route with a 64-year-old female from a high school office. Call was for a diabetic problem. Staff says she's been feeling weak and fatigued for two days, with some vomiting. Today she became very disoriented. We found her hyperventilating. She has a known history of Type 2 Diabetes. She's alert but confused, GCS is a 10. She's complaining of generalized pain, about a 4 out of 10. Vitals are BP 151 over 97, heart rate 112, respirations are 21, and SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. We've just done a basic evaluation procedure and have her on a 3-lead monitor with continuous assessments. She has no known allergies. We'll be there in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient has felt weak, fatigued, and has been vomiting for two days. Became acutely disoriented at work today, prompting coworkers to call for help.

transcript: Hey team, we've got a 60-year-old male, chief complaint of a traumatic injury after a fall. We picked him up at a restaurant downtown. He was out to dinner and developed sudden abdominal pain, fever, and then diarrhea. He got up to use the restroom, felt dizzy, and passed out, hitting his head. On our arrival, his GCS was an 8. Vitals are uh, BP 122 over 81, heart rate 98, respirations are 20, and he’s saturating at 95 percent on room air. He's alert but very confused. Pain scale is a zero, he's denying any pain right now. Because of the restaurant setting, we initially considered an allergic reaction and followed the anaphylaxis protocol, giving 2.74 milligrams of Albuterol/Ipratropium via neb. We also got a blood glucose, it was normal, and we did a 12-lead which shows sinus tach. He has a history of COPD and hypertension. Allergies to penicillin.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed sudden abdominal pain, fever, and diarrhea while at a restaurant, then had a syncopal episode and fell, hitting his head.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 50-year-old male found on the side of the road by a bystander. The initial call was for diabetic problems, but he's just generally altered. He couldn't tell us what happened. His GCS on our arrival was a 14. He's complaining of some unspecified shoulder pain and abdominal pain, zero out of ten right now, but he's also got a bit of an active nosebleed. Vitals are a little soft, BP is 95 over 58, heart rate is 89, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. He's alert. Per his medical alert bracelet, he has a history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He takes Metformin and Lisinopril. No known allergies. We put him on the cardiac monitor. Given the vague complaints, we followed our chest pain protocol and administered 375.15 milligrams of Aspirin via IM. We established IV access and are just monitoring him now.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by a bystander on the side of the road with an altered mental status and was unable to explain what happened.

transcript: We're en route with a 73-year-old male for chest pain, picked up from a trade/service area. On arrival he was complaining of 6 out of 10 chest pain, was noticeably disoriented, and felt warm to the touch, consistent with a fever. He's alert but confused, GCS is 13. Vitals are BP 136 over 76, heart rate 62, respirations 15, and SpO2 96 percent on room air. It was a bit wild on scene—he briefly went unresponsive and pulseless. We immediately started mechanically assisted chest compressions, got ROSC back in less than a minute. We performed a 12-lead EKG after that. Our primary impression is shortness of breath with a secondary of cardiac arrhythmia. We're on the General-Sepsis protocol. He has a history of CHF and is allergic to iodine. No meds given by us.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient complaining of chest pain, fever, and disorientation experienced a brief pulseless arrest on scene requiring mechanical compressions before ROSC was achieved.

transcript: Alright, we have a 78-year-old female we picked up from a department store. The initial call was for a sick person. We found her significantly altered with a GCS of 12, complaining of shortness of breath and a headache. She also mentioned some chest pain before we arrived. She has a history of diabetes and hypertension. Her vitals are a bit concerning: BP is 172 over 107, heart rate is 106, respirations are 20, and her SpO2 is 100 percent on room air. She denies any pain right now. We put her on a 3-lead, which just showed sinus tach. We checked her blood sugar, it was a little low, so we gave her 53.18 milligrams of oral glucose, which she tolerated well. Her mental status has slightly improved since then.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient began feeling sick while at a department store, experiencing shortness of breath, headache, and chest pain, leading to a 911 call.

transcript: We have a 34-year-old female found on a street corner, dispatched as an unconscious person. Bystanders state she had a tonic-clonic seizure. She's post-ictal now, GCS is a 12. She is alert but very confused. She's been vomiting and is nauseous, also some hyperventilation. She has a history of epilepsy and asthma. She's allergic to sulfa drugs. Vitals are BP 132 over 90, heart rate 89, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 100 percent. She's complaining of a generalized pain, about a five out of ten. We've just been doing continuous physical assessment. Following our failed airway protocol for post-ictal bronchospasm, we administered 2.52 milligrams of an Albuterol Ipratropium solution sublingually. She's been stable since then. We should be at your doors in about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a witnessed tonic-clonic seizure on a public street. EMS found her in a post-ictal state upon arrival.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 34-year-old female, we picked her up on the street. Chief complaint is fainting. Bystanders saw her collapse while walking. On arrival, she was conscious but very groggy, GCS was a 13. She states she's been having diarrhea and just generalized abdominal pain for a couple of days and felt very weak before she passed out. Vitals are stable: BP is 135 over 78, heart rate is a little tachy at 116, respirations are 20, and she's satting 98 percent on room air. She rates her abdominal pain a 5 out of 10. We put her on the 3-lead and ran a 12-lead, which just showed sinus tach. No meds given. She has no known allergies and says she only takes birth control pills. Past medical history is significant for IBS. We're thinking it's hypotension secondary to dehydration.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient collapsed while walking on the street after experiencing diarrhea and abdominal pain for several days.

transcript: Okay, we're headed to you with a 56-year-old female from a residence, call was for a near-fainting episode. She says she felt dizzy and weak and almost passed out. She is alert and oriented, GCS of 15. Her vitals are BP 108 over 73, heart rate 103, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 94 percent. She's complaining of some neck pain and what she calls angina, a 3 out of 10. She also has a slight cough. We hooked her up to capnography and did a 12-lead, which showed sinus tach but no ST changes. For her pain, we gave 42.12 milligrams of Fentanyl IM. She has a history of GERD and takes Omeprazole for it. She's allergic to Codeine. Our primary impression is dizziness.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a near-syncopal episode at home, preceded by feelings of dizziness and weakness.

transcript: Morning. This is a 92-year-old male, we were called out for a medical alert alarm activation at his residence. Found him sitting in his chair, alert but confused, GCS is 11. He's complaining of shortness of breath, some angina he rates at a 3 out of 10, dysuria, and a fever. He also had an episode of hematemesis just before we got there. Vitals are BP 147 over 92, heart rate 99, respirations 21, and he's 100 percent on room air. History of hypertension and Afib. He takes warfarin and lisinopril. Allergic to penicillin. He was pretty nauseous so we gave him 3.76 milligrams of Ondansetron orally, which he tolerated. We've had him on the cardiac monitor and capnography since we got there. Primary impression is hypertension. We came in under the General-Anaphylaxis protocol based on the initial dispatch.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient activated his medical alert alarm. Found alert but confused, complaining of shortness of breath, chest pain, and other symptoms after an episode of hematemesis.

transcript: Hello, we have a 100-year-old female coming in from another place, her daughter's home. The chief complaint is weakness. The daughter states her mother has been running a fever for a day and today she found her with an altered mental status and too weak to get out of bed. The patient is alert but confused, so her GCS is a 14. She's complaining of 5 out of 10 dorsalgia. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 196 over 119, heart rate is 87, respirations are 22, and SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. Her past medical history includes hypertension and osteoporosis, and she has an allergy to sulfa drugs. We obtained a 12-lead electrocardiogram. Per our General-Diabetic Emergency protocol, we administered 4.63 milligrams of Morphine intramuscularly for her back pain. We're about two minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by her daughter with weakness and altered mental status after having a fever for a day.

transcript: We have a 53-year-old female, picked up from a local college. Staff called for a fainting spell. On arrival, she was alert, GCS of 15, but said she felt very dizzy and had some unspecified abdominal pain and a cough. She denies any significant past medical history but does have a known allergy to peanuts. Says she ate some cookies from the cafeteria about 20 minutes before it happened. Her initial pressure was low, so we suspected an allergic reaction. We administered 0.83 milligrams of epinephrine IM per our Trauma-Eye Injury protocol. Her pressure came up nicely, latest set of vitals are 152 over 88, heart rate 84, respirations 12, and an SpO2 of 94 percent. Pain is now a 2 out of 10 in her abdomen. We have her on the monitor. Our primary impression is hypotension, maybe secondary to some generalized abdominal pain. She's fully alert now.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a fainting spell after eating cookies at a college cafeteria, possibly due to a peanut allergy.

transcript: Hi there, we have a 35-year-old male, picked up from his house for a near-fainting episode. His partner called. He's fully alert now, GCS of 15. Primary complaint now is generalized weakness, fatigue, and some diarrhea. He also mentions some pain in his shoulder, which he rates a 3 out of 10. He has no known allergies. His vitals are a blood pressure of 154 over 106, heart rate 84, respirations 15, and he's 100 percent on room air. We did a 12-lead and checked his blood glucose, which was normal. He has a past medical history of Type 2 Diabetes. We administered 2.81 milligrams of Albuterol orally, per his prescription, under the General-Chest Pain protocol. We should be at your doors in 10.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a near-syncopal episode at his residence, leading his partner to call 911.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 59-year-old female, we were called to a downtown office for a person down. Co-workers found her slumped at her desk, said she seemed weak and was complaining of nausea and feeling feverish. On our arrival, she was alert, GCS 15, but complaining of severe, uh, seven out of ten pain in her right arm after she apparently fell out of her chair. Vitals are pretty impressive: blood pressure is 200 over 119, heart rate is 122, respiratory rate is 16, and she's 99 percent on room air. We established an IV in the left AC and have her on the monitor, sinus tach. We moved her to the stretcher for transport. She has a history of hypertension and diabetes. Allergies to penicillin. We were looking at the sepsis protocol due to the fever and tachycardia.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found slumped at her desk by co-workers. She complained of weakness and feeling feverish before EMS arrival, and fell from her chair, injuring her arm.

transcript: Alright, we're inbound with a 54-year-old female from a downtown retail store. The call was for a psychiatric problem, but this looks more medical. We found her with some notable aphasia, general malaise, and a fever. She's complaining of severe pain in her neck and shoulder, a 9 out of 10. She's hypotensive at 92 over 54, tachycardic at 106, respirations are 15, and her SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. She is alert, GCS 15. Her friend on scene says she has a history of migraines and is allergic to Sulfa drugs. We did a 12-lead which was unremarkable. Given the aphasia and other signs, we initiated our anaphylaxis protocol and gave 19.28 milligrams of Heparin sublingually. Her condition is unchanged. We will be at your facility in about ten minutes.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of aphasia, malaise, fever, and severe pain while at a retail store, prompting a call from a friend.

transcript: Morning. We have a 44-year-old female teacher from the middle school. Staff called after she had a near-syncopal episode in the classroom. She's been feeling weak, nauseous, and just extremely fatigued all morning, and also mentioned having some diarrhea over the last day. She's fully alert and oriented, GCS is 15. Her vitals are a little soft—BP is 107 over 64, heart rate 110, respirations 23, and SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. We've been monitoring her with pulse oximetry. She denies any pain, so zero out of ten. Her mental status seems slightly altered from her baseline per her coworkers, just a bit slow to respond. We established an IV line but have not administered any medications. We were running the chest pain protocol just to be safe. She has a history of anxiety and takes Sertraline daily.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt progressively weak, nauseous, and fatigued throughout the morning, culminating in a near-syncopal episode at work.

transcript: Good morning. We're en route with a 100-year-old male found by PD wandering on the highway. Primary complaint seems to be behavioral. He's alert but confused, GCS is 13. He did mention having a fever and some dysuria. He also pointed to his chest, rating the pain a 2 out of 10. Vitals are BP 109 over 65, heart rate is tachycardic at 110, respirations 20, and SpO2 is 95 percent on ambient air. His history includes dementia and BPH. He is allergic to codeine. We initiated IV access via catheterization of a vein in his left arm. Per the General-Neonatal Care protocol, we administered 17.54 milligrams of Heparin via the IV. We'll be there in about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by police wandering on the highway, appearing confused.

transcript: Alright, we're here with a 100-year-old female from a single-family residence. Chief complaint is fainting. Our primary impression is weakness. Uh, family called after finding her very difficult to rouse this morning. She's had a few days of diarrhea, fatigue, and complaining of some ear pain. On exam, she's alert but very confused, GCS is 11. She's experiencing shortness of breath. No pain, zero out of ten. Vitals are, uh, pretty concerning. BP is 182 over 125, heart rate 94, respirations are 18, and her SpO2 is only 89 percent on room air. We initiated the sepsis protocol based on our findings. We just did a physical assessment and vitals, no other treatments initiated. We have no known allergies or medical history for her.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found difficult to rouse by family this morning after several days of diarrhea, fatigue, and ear pain.

transcript: How's it going. We have a 52-year-old male, driver in a minor MVC on the highway. Chief complaint is traumatic injury, but our primary impression is just generalized weakness. He is alert, but his GCS is only 11. He's complaining of nausea, some shortness of breath, and neck pain. No pain rating given. Vitals are a little soft, BP is 92 over 62, heart rate is 86, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 94 percent. His past history includes asthma and chronic pain, and he's allergic to Codeine. We just did a set of vitals on him. We gave 2.4 milligrams of Albuterol / Ipratropium orally, and... uh, we also gave 2.19 milligrams of Naloxone via nebulizer per protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was the driver in a minor motor vehicle collision on the highway.

transcript: Good morning. We have an 18-year-old female student, chief complaint was breathing problems. We found her feeling generally weak. She's also complaining of dyspnea and some abdominal pain, which she rates a 3 out of 10. She's alert, but a little slow to respond, GCS is a 14. Vitals are BP 137 over 81, heart rate 84, respiratory rate 18, and SpO2 is 95 percent. We've been doing continuous assessment and monitoring, and we did get a 12-lead. She has a history of SVT. Per our protocols, we administered a few medications: 1.95 milligrams of Naloxone IM, 5.46 milligrams of Adenosine IV, and also 0.41 milligrams of Nitroglycerin sublingually. Her only known allergy is to Ibuprofen. We're pulling up now.

pcr_json.events_leading: Student presented with breathing problems, weakness, and abdominal pain while at school.

transcript: Evening. We've got a 79-year-old female from the nursing facility with an altered mental status. Staff states she's not at her baseline dementia. She's had diarrhea all day and was complaining of chest and shoulder pain earlier. On exam, we noted some minor hemorrhage from a skin tear on her forearm. She's alert but very confused, GCS is a 12. Her blood pressure is 143 over 83, heart rate 89, respirations 17, and she's a bit hypoxic with an SpO2 of 92 percent. She denies pain currently. Her history includes dementia and hypertension. We started an IV access and have been doing continuous assessments. We also administered 2.41 milligrams of Albuterol sublingually per the General-Diabetic Emergency protocol for her wheezing.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient at a nursing facility experienced altered mental status, diarrhea, and earlier complaints of chest and shoulder pain.

transcript: We've got a 71-year-old male found on the street for a behavioral problem. Bystanders said he was wandering and confused. On our arrival, he was alert but disoriented. His GCS is a 5. He's tachycardic at 113, blood pressure is 157 over 90, respirations are 22, and he's at 94 percent on room air. He's complaining of nausea with vomiting and some dorsalgia, but denies chest pain. Pain scale is a zero. We established IV access and put him on the cardiac monitor. We did an EKG. Based on his presentation, we're thinking this is nausea with vomiting. He has a history of hypertension. We gave 0.33 milligrams of Nitroglycerin via nebulizer for his symptoms, which is a weird route, I know. He seems a little less nauseous now.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found wandering on the street, appearing confused and agitated to bystanders who initiated the 911 call.

transcript: Hi, we have a 100-year-old female from a private residence, called in as a sick person. Family says she's been weaker and more anxious than usual. We found her with a significantly decreased level of consciousness. Her GCS is only a 6. She's on a home ventilator, and we had to do some adjustments to assist her breathing. Vitals are: BP 148 over 92, heart rate 89, respirations 21, and pulse ox is showing 91 percent. She has a fever and is generally very weak and fatigued. She isn't responding to pain, so pain scale is zero. We did a full evaluation, but no other interventions were performed. Primary thought is chest pain of some kind, maybe pneumonia, given the fever and sats.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found with a significantly decreased level of consciousness at home by family, who noted preceding weakness and anxiety.

transcript: We have a 47-year-old male for you, coming from his apartment. Call was for a psych problem. His caseworker called for a welfare check. We found him in a very hot apartment, he seems weak and has difficulty in walking. He's alert but confused, GCS is a 12. He has a history of epilepsy and schizophrenia. No known allergies. Vitals are: BP 180 over 105, heart rate 75, respiratory rate 18, and SpO2 95 percent. He is complaining of just generalized, unspecified pain, a 2 out of 10, plus some abdominal pain and fatigue. We're thinking this could be heat-related, so we used the Heat Stroke protocol. We performed an evaluation and administered 323.81 milligrams of Aspirin sublingually. He's been quiet during transport.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's caseworker requested a welfare check; patient was found in a hot apartment appearing weak and having difficulty walking.

transcript: Okay, we're coming in with a 57-year-old female we picked up on the side of the street. Call came in for a diabetic problem. On arrival, we found her with an altered mental status, GCS is 13. She's alert but has significant aphasia. There's some minor hemorrhage on her arm from a scrape. She is complaining of 8 out of 10 chest pain and some unspecified abdominal pain. Vitals are a little soft, BP is 99 over 65, heart rate is 51, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 92 percent. We assisted her in taking her prescribed dose of 17.27 milligrams PO. Her history includes Type 2 Diabetes and DVT. She's allergic to penicillin. We're on the General-Neonatal Care protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found with altered mental status on the side of the street, initially dispatched as a diabetic problem.

transcript: We have a five-year-old female, picked up from her home. Mom called for fainting. The child is complaining of severe, unspecified pain, rating it a nine out of ten. She looks very weak. Parents state she suddenly developed a high fever, started vomiting, and then became very lethargic. She’s alert now, GCS of 15. Her heart rate is 113, respirations are 15, BP is 137 over 79, and her SpO2 is 94 percent. We initiated our General-Sepsis protocol due to the fever and lethargy. We took her vitals and checked a blood glucose. Uh, treatments were a little unusual per our protocol... we gave Aspirin, 309.02 milligrams via nebulizer. When her mental status didn't improve, we administered Naloxone, 2.3 milligrams sublingually. We also gave one dose of EPINEPHrine 0.1 MG/ML, that was 1.03 milligrams IM. Parents report no significant medical history and no known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient suddenly developed a high fever and began vomiting, followed by a period of lethargy and fainting.

transcript: Alright, we have a 52-year-old male we picked up on the street. Bystanders said he just collapsed. Chief complaint is traumatic injury. He is alert but confused, GCS is a 10. He's got some neck pain and a pretty good nosebleed, likely from the fall. He's also febrile and complaining of generalized weakness. His pain is a 6 out of 10 in his neck. Vitals are blood pressure 115 over 69, heart rate 81, respirations 20, and SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. He has a history of Afib and COPD. On scene, per protocol, we administered a neb treatment containing 10.74 milligrams of Diltiazem, 1.6 milligrams of Magnesium Sulfate, and 0.49 milligrams of Ipratropium. My primary impression is fever, with the trauma being secondary to a syncopal episode. We've just been doing continuous assessments.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient collapsed on the street, sustaining a fall with associated neck pain and epistaxis.

transcript: We've got a 55-year-old male, chief complaint is MVC. The accident was about an hour ago; he refused transport at the scene and drove home to his residence. He called us from there because he developed a headache and weakness. He is also complaining of some non-radiating chest pain, diarrhea, and dysuria. He's alert and oriented, GCS 15. Rates his headache a 1 out of 10. His history is positive for hypertension, and he's allergic to sulfa drugs. Vitals are BP 117 over 72, heart rate 82, respirations are 11, and SpO2 is 96 percent. We did a full evaluation, put him on two liters of oxygen by nasal cannula, and ran a 12-lead. He felt nauseous so we administered 3.81 milligrams of Zofran via nebulizer. Primary impression is headache, secondary is weakness. We're on the sepsis protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed a headache and weakness after refusing transport from an MVC and driving home.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 79-year-old male for you, coming from a lecture at the local community college. Chief complaint is basically a syncopal episode. His friend says he was listening to the speaker, suddenly complained of weakness, and then just collapsed. He didn't hit his head. On arrival, he was awake and alert, GCS is 15. He's complaining of some generalized weakness and a new pain in his left shoulder, rates it a 2 out of 10. He's also a bit worried. His vitals are blood pressure 103 over 67, heart rate is 86, respirations are 22, and he's saturating 93 percent on room air. We noted a slight fever. We started an IV and got a 12-lead, but no interventions were needed. He has a history of hypertension and BPH. He is allergic to penicillin. Our primary impression is syncope.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was listening to a lecture when he suddenly complained of weakness and then collapsed.

transcript: Hey, we've got an 88-year-old male, called as a traffic incident at his residence. It looks like he tripped and fell getting out of his car in the garage. He's alert and oriented, GCS 15. He denies any pain, so zero out of ten. He is complaining of some nausea and vomiting, some vague abdominal pain, and a tingling sensation in his hands. Vitals are BP 168 over 103, heart rate is 76, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. We did a full physical assessment and obtained a 12-lead EKG which was unremarkable. Given his high BP and cardiac history of CAD and CHF, we gave him one dose of Nitroglycerin, 0.4 milligrams, sublingually. He says he's allergic to sulfa drugs.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient tripped and fell while getting out of his car in the garage.

transcript:
Morning. We have a 72-year-old male, called for being a 'sick person' at an elementary school. He was there for an event and suddenly felt very weak and had some dyspnea. He also reports some other chest pain, which he rates a 3 out of 10. His medical history includes coronary artery disease and hypertension. He has no known drug allergies.

On arrival, he was alert but seemed a little slow to respond, GCS is a 14. Vitals are BP 134 over 86, heart rate 77, respirations 17, and an SpO2 of 94 percent. Our primary impression is weakness, but we're concerned about a cardiac arrhythmia. We established IV access and acquired a 12-lead EKG. We were operating under the General-Neonatal Care protocol for this patient. No medications were administered.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was at an event at an elementary school when he experienced a sudden onset of weakness, dyspnea, and chest pain.

transcript: We are en route with a 55-year-old male we picked up from a school. The initial call was for a syncopal episode. Bystanders said he just collapsed. When we got there, he was slow to respond but is now alert, just a bit confused. GCS is a 14. He had a nosebleed, looks like from the fall, but the hemorrhage is controlled now. He denies any pain. His blood pressure is 130 over 80, heart rate 99, respirations are a little fast at 22, and SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. We noted a strong odor of alcohol on his breath. transcript: Good morning. We have a 77-year-old male, picked up from his residence after his wife called for a fainting spell. On our arrival, he was fully alert, GCS of 15. He's complaining of a cough, some unspecified abdominal pain, and says he feels worried. He's also febrile to the touch. He denies any pain currently, so a zero out of ten. His vitals are a blood pressure of 124 over 72, heart rate of 117, and respirations are at 21. His SpO2 is 100 percent on room air. His history includes primary hypertension and epilepsy. He takes Metoprolol and Keppra. He told us he's allergic to sulfa drugs. We've been doing continuous assessment, we got a blood glucose of 112, and we have him on a 3-lead showing sinus tach. As per our sepsis protocol, we administered 290.54 milligrams of Aspirin via nebulizer. We are about three minutes away.

transcript: We're en route with a 61-year-old female from a single-family residence. The call was for an unconscious person. Husband said she complained of chest pain and a bad headache, then fainted. He thought it was a cardiac arrest. On our arrival, she was alert and oriented, GCS 15. She still has some of that other chest pain, rating it a 5 out of 10, and feels very short of breath. She seems a little altered from her baseline. Vitals are hypertensive at 177 over 108, heart rate 104, respiratory rate 23, and her SpO2 is 92 on room air. We put her on the cardiac monitor and did a 12-lead, no obvious STEMI. We're on the Cardiac-Hypertensive Emergency protocol. Per orders, we gave 0.88 milligrams of EPINEPHrine 0.1 MG/ML via the PO route. Her secondary impression is headache.

transcript: Morning. We're bringing you a 100-year-old male, a sick person call from a local service area. Staff said he seemed unwell and then had a syncopal episode. transcript: Morning. We're bringing in a 53-year-old female from a residence. The call was for a 'sick person'. transcript: Hey, we're bringing you a 75-year-old female from the community center on campus. Staff called for a fainting episode. She's complaining of generalized weakness and fatigue. transcript: We're coming in hot with a 70-year-old female. Dispatch sent us for a well person check from her residence. Family found her in bed, extremely altered. Her GCS is 8. transcript:

She also feels weak and dizzy and seems to have a fever. Her vitals are: blood pressure 129 over 74, heart rate is 104, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 99 percent on room air. Her daughter, who arrived on scene, said she has a history of epilepsy and gave her 2.8 milligrams of her prescribed Midazolam orally just before we got there. Our primary impression is epilepsy, maybe with a secondary injury from the fall. We initiated continuous assessment and pulse oximetry.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a witnessed seizure-like event at a department store, resulting in a fall. She is now postictal and complaining of severe neck pain.

transcript: Good morning. We have a... an interesting one for you. This is a 70-year-old male we picked up on the highway. His chief complaint is, uh, that he's having uterine contractions. He is alert but very confused, GCS is a 10. He's complaining of generalized weakness, fatigue, and some abdominal pain he rates a 2 out of 10. His vitals are stable: BP 142 over 87, heart rate 94, respirations 16, and he's 100 percent on room air. He has a history of COPD and some unspecified mental disorder. No known allergies. We did a 12-lead EKG which showed a normal sinus rhythm. Per our protocols for his symptoms, we gave 337.58 milligrams of Aspirin via a nebulizer. For his history of COPD and some slight wheezing, we also administered 3.31 milligrams of an Albuterol and Ipratropium combination sublingually.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found wandering on the highway, disoriented and complaining of having uterine contractions.

transcript: Okay, we're bringing you a 33-year-old female from the university campus. Call was for a near-fainting episode. She had a syncopal event but was alert GCS 15 on our arrival. She immediately started vomiting and complains of a fever and some paresthesia in her skin. She rates her overall pain a 6 out of 10. Vitals are pretty elevated: blood pressure is 162 over 107, heart rate is 116, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 96 percent. She has a known history of asthma. We established IV access. Per the General-Diabetic Emergency protocol, we gave 2.3 milligrams of Albuterol by mouth. Primary impression is an unspecified injury, secondary is vomiting. She has no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a syncopal event at the university. Upon waking, she began vomiting and complaining of fever and paresthesia.

transcript: Okay, so we're coming in with a 39-year-old male from the high school. Chief complaint is diabetic problems. He had a syncopal episode in the classroom, but was alert on our arrival. He seems pretty anxious. Our primary impression is syncope. Vitals are uh, blood pressure 148 over 83, heart rate is 109, respirations 24, and he's saturating at 94 percent on room air. GCS is a 14, he's just a little slow to respond. He does complain of some unspecified abdominal pain, rates it a 3 out of 10. He has no known allergies. We placed him on the monitor, ran a 3-lead and a 12-lead, all looked okay. Per our General-Chest Pain protocol for syncope, we administered 0.39 milligrams of Nitroglycerin via nebulizer. We'll be at your ER in about 5 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a syncopal episode in a classroom.

transcript: We have an 81-year-old female, picked up from a grocery store for a behavioral problem. Staff said she became acutely confused. Her primary history is epilepsy. She's alert but not following commands, GCS is a 6. She's severely hypertensive at 167 over 115, heart rate is 118, respirations 22, and SpO2 is 94 percent. She's moaning and points to her chest, so we're calling it a 2 out of 10 pain. She also has a persistent cough. We got a 12-lead ECG, which shows sinus tach. We were operating under our General-Neonatal Care protocol. Uh, she seemed nauseous so we gave 4.26 milligrams of Ondansetron orally, which she tolerated. Her only known allergy is to Codeine.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient became acutely confused while at a grocery store. Staff called 911 for a behavioral problem.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 59-year-old female for you, found on the roadside by a passerby. Chief complaint is just "sick person," but we're going with weakness. On arrival, she was very confused, acting strangely, and complaining of some non-specific chest pain. GCS was a 9. She's alert now but still not making much sense. Vitals were pretty spicy: BP of 179 over 110, heart rate 123, respirations 18, and she was hypoxic at 90 percent on room air. She rates her pain as a 9 out of 10. We've got her on the cardiac monitor. We followed the General-Eye Injury protocol for some reason, that's what dispatch told us. Per protocol, we gave Aspirin, 366.5 milligrams IV, and Albuterol, 2.18 milligrams IV. Also gave 0.82 milligrams of epinephrine PO. Her O2 is coming up a bit with a non-rebreather. No known allergies or medical history per the patient, she's too confused to answer.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the roadside by a passerby. She was confused, acting strangely, and complaining of chest pain upon EMS arrival.

transcript: We're bringing in a 66-year-old female from a residence, emergent. The chief complaint was nausea and vomiting. Family states she's been sick for a couple days with a fever and what she calls acute pharyngitis. Today, they found her on the floor, so we're treating it as an unspecified head injury, possibly with secondary dehydration. She's alert, GCS is 14. She complains of some neck pain, a 4 out of 10. Vitals are concerning: BP is 89 over 51, heart rate is 115, respirations 17, and her SpO2 is only 90 percent on room air. We did a 12-lead and checked her glucose. No meds given. She has a history of essential hypertension, but look at that pressure now. She is allergic to sulfa drugs. We're about 2 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient has been sick for several days with nausea and vomiting; today she was found on the floor after a presumed unwitnessed fall.

transcript: We have an 82-year-old female, found at her residence. Family called because she's been sick with a cough and fever for a few days and is now very lethargic. We'd classify her as a 'sick person'. She's not really tracking, GCS is an 8. She's hypertensive and tachycardic, BP is 164 over 103, heart rate is 112. Respirations are 18, SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. She's also complaining of nausea and vomiting. She rates her pain as a one out of ten. Her past history includes COPD and diabetes. Per protocol for her respiratory distress, we administered 1.14 milligrams of Epinephrine via nebulizer. We also gave 50.26 milligrams of Fentanyl, also nebulized. We've just been doing continuous assessment and pulse oximetry. We should be at your doors in about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Family called because patient has had a cough and fever for several days and is now very lethargic.

transcript: Hey there, we have a 30-year-old female, initially called for a behavioral issue from her residence. On scene, she began complaining of shortness of breath and some vague abdominal pain. She is hypertensive. Vitals are BP 156 over 106, heart rate 87, respirations 16, and satting 97 percent on room air. She's a GCS 15, completely alert. She denies any pain right now, a zero out of ten. We established an IV and moved her to the stretcher. We also acquired a 12-lead, which showed a normal sinus rhythm with some tachycardia. Per a new protocol, we administered 222.56 milligrams of Lactated Ringer's Solution sublingually. She has no known allergies. She says she has a history of IBS but that's it.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Initially a call for a behavioral problem, the patient developed an acute onset of shortness of breath and abdominal pain at her residence.

transcript: Alright, so we got called for mutual aid for a 2-year-old male with altered mental status. On arrival, the first crew was bagging him, but he started coming around. Parents report a possible unwitnessed fall followed by seizure-like activity. He's been pretty nauseous and had one episode of vomiting. History of epilepsy. He's on Keppra, no known allergies. Vitals are stable: BP is 126 over 81, heart rate 75, respirations 22, and his SpO2 is 93 percent on room air. He’s alert now, GCS is 15. Denies any pain, so zero out of ten. Due to the unwitnessed event, we put a C-collar on for precaution. We've got him on the monitor, ran a 12-lead which shows sinus rhythm, and we established an IV. We gave 4.41 milligrams of Ondansetron IV for the nausea. He's also complaining of some neck pain, which he wasn't before. Primary impression is altered mental status, secondary is hypotension though his pressure is fine now. We're about 5 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a possible unwitnessed fall followed by seizure-like activity, leading to altered mental status and vomiting.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 35-year-old male we picked up from the street. Bystanders called for an unconscious person. When we got there, he was fully conscious, GCS of 15. He said he felt weak and then fainted for a few seconds. He denies any trauma from the fall, but our primary impression is acute pain due to trauma just in case. He is denying any pain right now, so a zero out of ten. He does mention having a cough, diarrhea, and some dysuria for the past day. His vitals are concerning: he's hypotensive and bradycardic. BP is 97 over 61, heart rate is 44. Respirations are 18, SpO2 is 96 percent. Given the numbers, we went with the Cardiac-Arrhythmia protocol and gave him 4.74 milligrams of Norepinephrine IM. We're just doing continuous assessment now. His only allergy is to sulfa drugs. We're about 5 minutes away.

pcr_json.events_leading: Bystanders called after patient fainted on the street. Patient was conscious on arrival, reporting a syncopal episode preceded by weakness.

transcript: Hey, we're bringing in a 90-year-old female from the public library. Call was for a person down. We found her on the floor next to her chair; she's alert, GCS 15. She is complaining of generalized weakness and has a fever. She also reports a 7 out of 10 pain, but it's diffuse and she can't pinpoint a location, so we're treating it as an unspecified injury from sliding out of the chair. Her past medical history includes osteoporosis and hypothyroidism, and she has an allergy to shellfish. Her vitals are stable: BP is 106 over 67, heart rate 86, respirations are 12, and SpO2 is 97 percent. We initiated the General-Sepsis protocol. We've established an IV, taken her vitals, and acquired a 12-lead EKG. She complained of nausea, so we gave her 4.76 milligrams of Ondansetron via the IM route.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the floor next to a chair at a public library, complaining of weakness, fever, and diffuse pain.

transcript: Hey, we've got a... an interesting one for you. We have a 100-year-old male found on the street. Dispatch chief complaint was, uh, uterine contractions. Our primary impression is just generalized weakness. The patient is very confused, GCS is 8 but he is alert. He's complaining of weakness and fatigue, and he feels warm. Vitals are BP 171 over 104, heart rate 124, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 100 percent on room air. He denies pain, so a zero on the pain scale. We've established catheterization of a vein and are monitoring pulse oximetry. For his condition, we administered 0.92 milligrams of oral EPINEPHrine 0.1 MG/ML, 0.46 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IM, and 42.46 milligrams of Fentanyl, also orally. He has a history of dementia and is allergic to ibuprofen. ETA is 5 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found wandering on the street, very confused.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 40-year-old female from a single-family residence, chief complaint was a syncopal episode. Husband states she felt dizzy with some paresthesia in her hands after a shower and passed out. On our arrival, she was alert, GCS 14. She complains of some unspecified, generalized pain from the fall, rates it a 4 out of 10. Initial vitals were blood pressure 136 over 89, heart rate of 60, respirations 15, and an SpO2 of 96 percent. We performed an initial patient assessment and have had her on continuous cardiac monitoring. The EKG is unremarkable. Per protocol, we administered 327.2 milligrams of Aspirin via IM injection. Oh, and uh, we initiated ventilator care... just following the general neonatal care protocol we were assigned. No known allergies. She has a history of hypertension and migraines.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt dizzy with paresthesia in her hands after a shower and had a syncopal episode, resulting in a fall.

transcript: Alright, we're coming in with a 100-year-old male, driver in a minor MVC on the highway. Our primary impression is acute pain, though he denies any, rating it a zero out of ten. He seems a bit confused, GCS is 14. He complains of dizziness, nausea and vomiting, and a cough. He also feels feverish. It sounds like he may have been sick before the collision. Vitals are BP 150 over 88, heart rate is 99, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 96 percent. Given his age and the mechanism, we're bringing him in for evaluation. We followed the General-Chest Pain protocol, did a 12-lead which was unremarkable, and have him on a 3-lead monitor. He has a history of BPH and takes Tamsulosin. He has no known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was the driver in a minor motor vehicle collision. He may have been experiencing medical symptoms prior to the crash.

transcript: We have a 79-year-old male we picked up from a grocery store. Staff called for a psychiatric problem, but he seems more medical. He was found weak and confused after vomiting. He has a history of essential hypertension. Uh, he's responding to verbal, GCS is a 9. He has some nausea and his mental status is definitely altered. Vitals are blood pressure 118 over 81, heart rate 83, respirations are 20, and SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. We put him on the pulse oximeter and established a saline lock. He rates his general discomfort a 2 out of 10. No medications given en route. He's allergic to sulfa drugs. We're about 10 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found weak, confused, and had an episode of vomiting while at a grocery store.

transcript:
Okay, so we have a 5-year-old male, picked up from his residence. Mom called for what she thought was a well person check, but he's quite sick. He's been feeling fatigued with a cough, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea for two days. Today he became acutely short of breath. He has a history of asthma but takes no daily medications. No known drug allergies.

On exam, he's alert but lethargic, GCS is a 13. Vitals are BP 81 over 53, heart rate 116, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. He's complaining of some pain, rates it a 3 out of 10. We're treating this under the General-Sepsis protocol. We administered 2.4 milligrams of Albuterol syrup orally. We've also informed the receiving doctor of our findings and ETA. Primary impression is shortness of breath.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient has been sick for two days with cough, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Today he became acutely short of breath and lethargic.

transcript: Hi, we have a 79-year-old female coming in from her home. We were called for nausea and vomiting. She states she's been feeling sick all day. She also mentioned some dysuria. She's alert and oriented, GCS is 15. Her vitals are stable: BP 146 over 86, heart rate is 64, respirations 18, and she's 94 percent on room air. She denies any pain, so zero out of ten. Her past medical history includes GERD and Type 2 Diabetes. She's allergic to sulfa drugs. We did a full evaluation and took vitals. Based on our protocols, we administered 0.35 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IV and also gave 9.49 milligrams of Dexamethasone orally. This is per the General-Neonatal Care protocol, which seemed odd but that's what we were directed to use. She tolerated the treatments well.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called EMS after feeling sick all day with nausea and vomiting.

transcript: We're headed your way with a 96-year-old male from a school. He was there for an event and had a sudden onset of hyperventilation. The primary complaint was an allergic reaction, but we're not seeing any signs of one. He's alert, GCS 15. No pain, zero out of ten. He has a history of COPD, hypertension, and A-Fib. His vitals are BP 207 over 135, heart rate is 79, respirations 20, and his SpO2 is 98 percent. He also developed an epistaxis on scene which has since stopped. We performed a 12-lead and have been doing continuous physical assessment. He's allergic to Codeine. We gave 1.63 milligrams of Magnesium Sulfate sublingual and 45.18 milligrams of Fentanyl IM per our protocol. His breathing has calmed down a bit since then.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings

transcript: Okay, so we've got a 54-year-old male we picked up from his house. His wife called because she found him slumped in his chair, very difficult to arouse. He's more with it now, GCS is a 9. He's alert but very confused. He's complaining of a headache, rates it a 4 out of 10, and has a pretty significant cough. His vitals are blood pressure 164 over 108, heart rate 79, respirations 19, and he's saturating at 94 percent on room air. He has a history of COPD and hypertension. We checked his blood sugar, it was normal. He was complaining of nausea so we administered 4.41 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually. We're here under the Airway protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's wife found him slumped in his chair and difficult to arouse.

transcript: Hey Doc, we're bringing you a 92-year-old female found on a sidewalk by a bystander. The initial dispatch was for non-traumatic chest pain, but she denies any pain, so it's a zero out of ten. She just says she feels very weak and was nauseous, and she vomited once before we got there. She's alert and oriented, GCS is 15. Her vitals are stable: blood pressure 137 over 83, heart rate is 106, respirations 16, and she's 97 percent on room air. We did a 12-lead, which just showed sinus tach. Past history includes atrial fibrillation and dementia; she takes Eliquis for it. She has an allergy to sulfa drugs. We just did an evaluation and provided transport. She's in the hallway bed.

transcript:
We have a 67-year-old male for you, coming from the elementary school. Initial call was for non-traumatic chest pain, but on our arrival, the pain had resolved. His main issue is dizziness and an altered mental status. We're going with a primary impression of dizziness and giddiness.

He's alert to voice, but very confused. GCS is a 9. Vitals are stable: BP 118 over 76, heart rate 63, respirations 24. Sats are 100 percent on room air. He denies any pain, so zero out of ten. He's also complaining of generalized weakness, some paresthesia in his hands, and a little pain in his shoulder. He denies any known allergies or medical history.

We've established an IV and have him on the monitor. The school nurse had given him 14 milligrams of Ketorolac by mouth before we got there. We're on the General-Neonatal Care protocol. See you in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient initially experienced chest pain which resolved prior to EMS arrival, but then developed significant dizziness and confusion.

transcript: Hey there, we're bringing in a 67-year-old male for weakness. We found him on the street, slumped over on a bus bench. Bystanders said he looked unwell for a while. He's alert but lethargic, GCS is 13. He's complaining of generalized fatigue, some chest pain, and unspecified abdominal pain. Rates his pain a 3 out of 10. Vitals are pretty concerning: BP is 169 over 116, heart rate 107, respirations 23, and SpO2 is 92 on room air. We suspect sepsis. His past medical history includes CHF and COPD. He's allergic to Codeine. We initiated an IV line and assisted him to the stretcher. We gave 298.88 milligrams of Aspirin via the IV line per our sepsis protocol. He's remained stable en route.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Bystanders called after finding the patient slumped over on a bus bench, appearing weak.

transcript: Alright, we have a 64-year-old female we picked up from a trade area. She experienced a sudden onset of weakness, dizziness, and generalized pain. She describes it as abdominal pain, back pain, and some classic angina-like chest pain. Her GCS is a 13; she's alert but seems a little confused. Her past history is significant for Type 2 diabetes and she is allergic to sulfa drugs. Vitals on scene were a blood pressure of 131 over 83, heart rate 102, and respirations 19. SpO2 is 100 percent. We established a large bore IV, performed a 12-lead EKG, and due to her GCS, we inserted an NPA as an airway procedure. We gave 0.41 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IV. She states her pain is now a 2 out of 10. We are about 5 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of weakness, dizziness, and pain in her abdomen, back, and chest while shopping.

transcript: Hey guys, we're bringing you a 76-year-old male from a school, looks more like a long-term care institution. Staff called for a 'Sick Person.' They found him slumped over and initiated CPR. He had a return of spontaneous circulation just as we arrived. He's alert now but confused, GCS is 14. He's complaining of generalized malaise and fatigue with a productive cough. He rates his overall discomfort as a 4 out of 10. His vitals post-ROSC are: BP 134 over 93, heart rate is 105, respirations 26, and he's 100 percent on a non-rebreather. We did a 12-lead, showed sinus tach. He takes lisinopril for blood pressure. We gave him 4.11 milligrams of Ondansetron orally for nausea. We're managing under the Sepsis protocol and performed a full adult care assessment. We'll be there in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found slumped over by staff at an institution. Staff initiated CPR and patient achieved ROSC upon EMS arrival.

transcript: We have a 34-year-old male, called for breathing problems, but it seems to be more of a syncopal episode. Wife says he collapsed just after calling 911. He's alert now but confused, GCS is a 12. He's just complaining of generalized weakness and malaise. Vitals are blood pressure 151 over 94, heart rate is 88, respirations 14, and he was hypoxic with an SpO2 of 90 percent on room air. He complains of some minor pain, a 2 out of 10. We suspect a possible CVA, so we're treating it as a syncope and collapse. We placed him on the cardiac monitor, got a 12-lead, and put a cervical collar on him since the collapse was unwitnessed. We have not administered any medications. His only past medical history is a PFO, and he has an allergy to sulfa drugs. We're about five minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called for breathing problems and then had an unwitnessed collapse.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 34-year-old male, coming from the college campus. Chief complaint is traumatic injury. He was lifting some heavy boxes and felt a pop in his back. He initially complained of some associated chest pressure, so we followed our chest pain protocol, but that's resolved. He's denying any pain now, a zero out of ten. He has a history of a herniated disc but takes no regular meds for it. He is allergic to penicillin. Vitals are stable: BP is 101 over 69, heart rate is 81, respirations are 23, and he's 97 percent on room air. He's alert and oriented, GCS of 15. On exam, he's got some tenderness in his lower back, some dorsalgia, and just feels generally weak and a little malaise. We established cardiac monitoring, and because of that initial chest pressure complaint, we did give 0.34 milligrams of Nitroglycerin via nebulizer, which he tolerated well.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was lifting heavy boxes when he felt a pop in his back, with some initial associated chest pressure that has since resolved.

transcript: We're en route with an 80-year-old male involved in a minor motor vehicle collision. It happened in the parking lot of a local school. He states he suddenly felt unwell and drove into a parked car. He's complaining of some cervicalgia, rating the pain a 3 out of 10, and also states he feels like he has a fever. His vitals are: blood pressure 110 over 69, heart rate 99, and respiratory rate 26. SpO2 is 100 percent. He is alert but a little slow, GCS of 14. We did a 12-lead EKG. He was very agitated on scene, so we administered 2.15 milligrams of Midazolam orally. He also got 4.72 milligrams of Ondansetron IM for nausea. He has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril. Based on his story, our primary impression is cardiac arrest as the cause of the accident, with generalized abdominal pain as a secondary finding. We're on the General-Anaphylaxis protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient suddenly felt unwell while driving in a parking lot, resulting in a low-speed motor vehicle collision with a parked car.

transcript: We're en route with a 77-year-old female from her residence. Family called for a behavioral issue after they found her on the floor. She apparently had a syncopal episode. She's alert but has some noticeable aphasia, GCS is a 14. She's also complaining of generalized muscle weakness and some nausea. Her vitals are blood pressure 139 over 91, heart rate 71, respirations 15, and an SpO2 of 92 percent on room air. She's complaining of a headache, rates it a 5 out of 10. We have her on the monitor. Given the presentation, we administered 324 milligrams of Aspirin orally, and for her pain we gave 50 micrograms of Fentanyl intranasally. She has a history of a previous TIA and hypertension. No known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the floor by family after an apparent syncopal episode.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 51-year-old male, chief complaint is chest pain, but it's presenting more like respiratory distress. We found him at his house. His wife says he's had a nasty cough, fever, and diarrhea for a couple of days, and tonight he just got super weak and confused. His initial vitals were pretty gnarly: blood pressure 202 over 124, heart rate was 53, respirations 21, and he was sating 97 percent on room air. He's only responsive to pain, GCS is a 13. He denies any pain, so a zero out of ten. We put him on the monitor, got a 3-lead and then a 12-lead. Per protocol General-Chest Pain, we gave 4.44 milligrams of Ondansetron via neb for his nausea and also pushed 1.06 milligrams of epi sublingually due to his profound hypotension that developed. His past history includes hypertension and asthma, he takes lisinopril. He's allergic to shellfish. Our primary impression is acute respiratory distress syndrome.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced several days of cough, fever, and diarrhea, leading to an acute onset of weakness and confusion at his residence.

transcript: We're en route with a 46-year-old male, found down on the street. Bystanders said he was complaining of a headache and generalized abdominal pain before we got there. He's alert but confused, GCS is 13. He also mentioned some dysuria. He denies any pain now, so a zero out of 10. Vitals are a little soft: BP is 109 over 69, heart rate is bradycardic at 51, respirations are 18, and SpO2 is 94 percent. We initiated patient monitoring and capnography. Based on his history of diabetes, we followed our diabetic protocol and administered 381.99 milligrams of Aspirin orally. No known drug allergies. We're about three minutes away.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found by bystander on the street, confused and complaining of headache and abdominal pain.

transcript: We have a 4-year-old female we picked up from home for nausea and vomiting. According to mom, she's had a fever and general weakness for two days, but tonight she started vomiting what looked like blood, so that's hematemesis. She's also had some dysuria. The patient is alert, GCS is 15. Her vitals are a little strange... BP is 146 over 96, heart rate is 65, respirations are 20, and SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. She rates her pain zero out of ten currently. We did an evaluation and per the General Neonatal Care protocol and a call to medical direction, we gave some medications. We administered 52.74 milligrams of Fentanyl via nebulizer and also gave 3.31 milligrams of Morphine IV. Mom says she has no significant medical history and no known allergies.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient has a two-day history of fever and weakness, with an acute onset of hematemesis.

transcript:
Alright, we're coming in with a 100-year-old female from the skilled nursing facility. Chief complaint is breathing problems, but the main issue seems to be profound weakness. Staff says she's been declining for two days with diarrhea and dysuria.

She's alert and oriented, GCS is 15. Vitals are a bit concerning. BP is 143 over 99, heart rate is down at 65, but her respirations are 26 and she's only getting 88 percent on room air. We've got her on a non-rebreather now. She says her pain is a 2 out of 10, just general discomfort. She's also complaining of some dizziness and neck pain.

Past history includes CHF and COPD. She's allergic to sulfa. We started a line... and per our protocol for weakness, which is listed as General-Neonatal Care, we gave 293.14 milligrams of Aspirin IV. We'll be in your bay in two minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found at her nursing facility with a two-day history of diarrhea and dysuria, now presenting with profound weakness and difficulty breathing.

transcript: We're coming in with a 49-year-old male from the group home on Elm. Chief complaint is breathing problems. Staff found him dizzy and tachycardic. He's responding to verbal stimuli, but he's pretty out of it. GCS is 14. Vitals are: BP is high at 187 over 128, heart rate is 114, respiratory rate is slow at 12, SpO2 is good at 98 percent on room air. Pain is a 1 out of 10. We checked his glucose, it was 95. Due to the slow respirations and altered status, we administered 1.88 milligrams of Naloxone IV with no change. He has a history of depression and bipolar disorder, and takes Seroquel and Zoloft. He has no known drug allergies. Our primary impression is other reduced mobility. We're following the General-Neonatal Care protocol, per our system's guidelines for this facility.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by staff at his group home feeling dizzy and tachycardic with breathing problems.

transcript: We've got a 27-year-old male for you, came in as a traumatic injury. Found him at a single-family residence after he apparently fell. He's alert, GCS is 15. He complains of some back and abdominal pain, rating it a 2 out of 10. He has a cough and feels warm to the touch. He admits to drinking heavily tonight. Vitals are a little soft, BP is 94 over 58, heart rate is 80, respirations are 16, and SpO2 is 93 percent on room air. No known allergies or medical history. We started a saline lock and acquired a 12-lead, which shows sinus rhythm. Primary impression is alcohol intoxication. We're also thinking about generalized muscle weakness secondary to the fall and intoxication.

transcript: Morning. We have a 36-year-old male, we picked him up from his office. He had a syncopal episode at his desk. He's alert now, GCS is 14. He denies any pain. His main complaints are some nausea and vomiting, and significant cervicalgia. The big issue is his blood pressure, it's 203 over 134. Heart rate is 104, respirations 16, SpO2 is 97%. We also gave a nebulizer with 3.03 milligrams of Albuterol and Ipratropium for some mild chest tightness he mentioned. Our primary impression is weakness. We’re treating him under the General-Neonatal Care protocol. We should be there in about 5 minutes.

transcript: Hey there, we're bringing in an 88-year-old female from a school. The call was for a well person check. We found her appearing generally ill. transcript: Morning. We've got a 40-year-old male, chief complaint of traumatic injury. He's a teacher at the high school who had a syncopal episode in his classroom. transcript: We're bringing in a 70-year-old female for diabetic problems. We found her at a department store. Employees called because of her strange and inexplicable behavior. We initiated an initial patient assessment, established IV access, and put her on the EKG monitor. We're on the Airway protocol due to her oxygen saturation. Her past medical history includes Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension. She is allergic to sulfa drugs. We haven't administered any medications. We let the charge nurse know we were on our way.

transcript: Hey there, we have a 99-year-old female coming your way from a residence. transcript: Hey, we're headed your way with a 36-year-old female from an office building, primary complaint is nausea and vomiting. The big issue here is her blood pressure, it's 184 over 103. transcript: Alright, we've got a 26-year-old male for you, chief complaint of weakness. We found him at his house. Her vitals are stable: BP 118 over 66, heart rate 82, respirations 18, SpO2 is 98 percent. She's A&O times four, GCS of 15. Pain is a 5 out of 10 in her abdomen. We're doing a continuous assessment. Per our eye injury protocol, we gave 351.36 milligrams of Aspirin IV, and for her pain we administered 58.91 milligrams of Fentanyl via nebulizer. Our primary impression is generalized abdominal pain with a secondary of weakness. We're about 2 minutes away.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of generalized abdominal pain, weakness, and a minor nosebleed while at work.

transcript: Morning. We have an 88-year-old male from a single-family residence, called for breathing problems. His primary issue seems to be nausea with vomiting, and family suspects some alcohol use today. He's alert and oriented, GCS is 15. He's complaining of generalized weakness and had an episode of epistaxis that was difficult to control before we arrived. His pain is about a 4 out of 10, just general malaise. Vitals are a little up: blood pressure is 143 over 80, heart rate is 124, respirations are 18, and he's 96 percent on room air. We established an IV for access. No medications were given. His history includes hypertension and atrial fibrillation, for which he takes Warfarin. He has an allergy to codeine. We're on the airway protocol just in case, given the vomiting risk.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was feeling weak and nauseous after consuming alcohol, which was followed by an episode of epistaxis that was difficult to control.

transcript:
Hey doc, we're bringing you a 100-year-old female we picked up from the side of the highway. Police called for a well person check and found her just sitting on the curb. Initially she denied any problems, but then started complaining of dizziness and some generalized abdominal pain. She rates her pain a zero out of ten, says it's just 'there'. Her primary impression is generalized abdominal pain, and we noted a secondary of epilepsy.

Vitals are stable: BP is 135 over 90, heart rate 91, respirations 22, and she's saturating at 94 percent on room air. She's a GCS of 15, alert and oriented. Past medical history includes hypertension and epilepsy. Allergies are to Penicillin. We did a 12-lead, showed sinus tach. Per protocol General-Sepsis, we administered 5.65 milligrams of Norepinephrine sublingually and 52.51 milligrams of Fentanyl via nebulizer. We're about five minutes out. Any questions?

transcript: We're bringing you a 24-year-old male from his house, called for being a 'sick person'. He had a sudden onset of severe shortness of breath. He's got a history of asthma and a peanut allergy. He was found to be extremely sweaty and then had one episode of hematemesis. He is alert but anxious, GCS is a 14. Primary impression is dyspnea, secondary is weakness. His vitals are a bit concerning: BP is 161 over 106, heart rate 81, and respirations are fast at 27. SpO2 is 97 percent. He rates his overall discomfort as a 2 out of 10. We went ahead with an evaluation procedure under the Airway protocol. We gave EPINEPHrine zero-point-nine-seven milligrams sublingually, and Fentanyl, fifty-five point five six milligrams, IM. His breathing seems to have eased slightly since the meds.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of severe shortness of breath, followed by diaphoresis and an episode of hematemesis.

transcript: Morning. We have a 70-year-old male, found at his single-family residence. He's here for injuries sustained in a traffic incident in his driveway. His main complaint is weakness and some abdominal pain, about a 6 out of 10. He's alert and oriented, GCS of 15. He's also got a persistent cough and some tingling, uh, paresthesia in his hands. Vitals are stable: BP 131 over 78, heart rate is 99, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 96 percent. We put a 3-lead on him and established an IO. His wife says he has a history of COPD and hypertension. He's allergic to Penicillin. We gave him two point two seven milligrams of Albuterol and eleven point five six milligrams of Dexamethasone, both sublingual. He's also showing some mild aphasia which is new for him. We're following the Trauma-Eye Injury protocol given the mechanism.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis

transcript: Alright, we're bringing you a 94-year-old male from his residence. Chief complaint is fainting. transcript: Hey, we're inbound with a 61-year-old female from a single-family residence. Family called for a possible allergic reaction, but we found her with an altered mental status. transcript:
Hey, we're headed your way with a 20-year-old male from a university dorm, chief complaint of breathing problems. He looks septic. He's been feeling sick for two days with a fever and general malaise. He's also very dizzy. He has a history of asthma and an allergy to peanuts.

transcript: We have a 100-year-old female, called for as a sick person from her residence. The home health aide found her profoundly weak and said she had an episode of hematemesis. transcript: Hi there, we have a 73-year-old female, Jane. We were called for an unknown problem, a person down on the highway. Her primary issue seems to be general weakness. transcript: Morning, we've got a 91-year-old male, a traumatic injury from a residential care facility. He had an unwitnessed fall from his bed. Staff found him on the floor. transcript: Morning. We're bringing in a 59-year-old female, found unconscious at her residence. On arrival, her GCS was 3. Her vitals are... uh... pretty concerning. BP is 170 over 113, heart rate 95, respirations 18, and her SpO2 was 92 percent on room air. We initiated nebulizer therapy. Following the diabetic emergency protocol, we administered a few medications. We gave 3.82 milligrams of Ondansetron via the nebulizer, 1.14 milligrams of EPINEPHrine orally, and 0.48 milligrams of Nitroglycerin via IM injection. She has a reported allergy to sulfa drugs. Pain is hard to assess, but she grimaces to stimuli, so we'll call it a 2 out of 10.

transcript: Hey, we're about five minutes out with an 85-year-old female. Chief complaint is near-syncope. We were called to a local school where she was attending an event. We're doing continuous assessment en route. Patient has no known drug allergies.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 59-year-old male, picked up from a trade area. He's a sick person, we're thinking anaphylactic shock. He was working when he was stung by an insect.
