transcript: Alright, we've got a 44-year-old male, a staff member at the high school, complaining of just being a sick person. His coworkers called because he seemed confused and weak. Patient states he's had about two days of diarrhea and some unspecified abdominal pain. He's alert but he's a GCS of 14. He's hypotensive, BP is 87 over 57. Heart rate is 86, respirations 19, SpO2 99 percent on room air. His pain is a three out of ten in his abdomen. Given his presentation, we're going with a primary of an unspecified mental disorder, with a secondary of weakness. We've initiated continuous physical assessment and established a catheterization of his vein, but we haven't given any medications. He has a past medical history of IBS but takes no daily meds. No known allergies. We'll be there shortly.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced two days of diarrhea and abdominal pain, leading to weakness and confusion at his workplace.

transcript: Hey doc, we have a 50-year-old male, a teacher from the local high school. Staff called because he was acting really confused. Chief complaint is just "sick person," but we're thinking altered mental status. He complains of some neck pain and has a bit of a cough. On arrival, he was alert but only knew his name, giving him a GCS of 11. Vitals are stable: blood pressure is 155 over 93, heart rate 96, respirations are 15, and he's 100 percent on room air. He denies any pain, so zero out of ten. His history includes hypertension and asthma. He's allergic to penicillin. We did a 12-lead, which showed sinus tach. Uhm, per our cardiac protocol, we started a neb treatment with 3.6 milligrams of Albuterol / Ipratropium and 2.81 milligrams of Albuterol, both administered orally via the nebulizer mask.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient, a teacher, was found by staff at the school acting confused.

transcript: We're en route with a 52-year-old male found on the highway median, initial complaint was breathing problems. He states he's felt weak and feverish all day. He developed a nosebleed, and then had an episode of hematemesis that he described as looking like coffee grounds. He's got generalized abdominal pain, rates it a 5 out of 10. He's alert but seems a little slow, GCS is 14. Vitals are 149 over 99, heart rate is 106, respirations are 20, and he's at 92 percent on room air. We just did a physical assessment. We did give 0.42 milligrams of sublingual Nitroglycerin based on his initial respiratory complaint before the hematemesis became clear. No known medical history or allergies per patient.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt weak and feverish all day, then developed a nosebleed and an episode of hematemesis.

transcript: We have a 76-year-old male, called in as a person down at his mobile home. A neighbor found him. He's alert with a GCS of 15, but he is in significant distress. He complains of severe, all-over pain that he rates a 10 out of 10. He also reports nausea with vomiting, extreme fatigue, and feeling feverish. His vitals are: blood pressure 164 over 99, heart rate 68, respirations 21, and SpO2 96 percent. We got a 12-lead and informed the doctor of our findings. Under the General-Sepsis protocol, we administered 1.0 milligram of EPINEPHrine sublingually and gave him 3.36 milligrams of an Albuterol Ipratropium combination by mouth. He has a history of asthma and is allergic to penicillin.

pcr_json.events_leading: A neighbor found the patient in distress at his home after not seeing him for a day.

transcript: We have a 95-year-old female we picked up at a retail store, initial call was for a sick person. She was complaining of diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, and had an episode of vomiting just before we got there. She rates her pain a 3 out of 10. Her vitals are blood pressure 127 over 83, heart rate is 135, and respirations are 22. Her SpO2 is 96 percent. She's... uh... GCS is 15, but she's only responding verbally. She’s not really tracking. We were dispatched under the General-Eye Injury protocol, not sure why. We started an IV and performed a 12-lead. We gave 58.83 milligrams of Sodium Bicarbonate orally, 1.13 milligrams of EPINEPHrine 0.1 MG/ML via IM, and a 58.16 milligram dose of Fentanyl sublingually for her pain. She is allergic to iodine.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of diffuse abdominal pain and nausea, with an episode of vomiting, while at a retail store.

transcript:
Alright, we've got a 54-year-old male victim of an assault. We found him on a paved roadway. He states he was attacked and now has generalized weakness and nausea. He's also complaining of dizziness, says he has a nosebleed, some diarrhea, and a sore throat. He rates his pain as a 6 out of 10. Patient is alert and oriented, GCS is 15.

His vitals are stable: BP is 124 over 80, heart rate 99, respiratory rate 18, and SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. He has no known drug allergies. His only past history is GERD, for which he takes Omeprazole. We started a line, just a saline lock in his left AC, got a 12-lead which shows normal sinus rhythm, and have been doing continuous assessments. We haven't given any meds. Our primary impression is weakness. We used the General Chest Pain protocol as a precaution given the circumstances.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient states he was physically attacked and is now experiencing generalized weakness and nausea.

transcript: We have an 86-year-old female, victim of a low-speed traffic incident in front of her house. She was the restrained driver. She's complaining of severe, unspecified pain, a 10 out of 10. She also mentions some chest pain and abdominal pain. She's alert, but a little confused about the details of the accident, so we're giving her a GCS of 14. Her pressure is up at 172 over 111, heart rate is 117, respirations are 16, and sats are good at 98 percent on room air. We also noted a fever on scene. We placed her on a 3-lead monitor which just shows sinus tachycardia. No medications were given. Past history of Afib and takes Eliquis for it. No known allergies. We have her C-collared and on a backboard for precaution.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was the restrained driver in a low-speed motor vehicle collision in front of her residence.

transcript: Good morning. We are bringing you a 62-year-old male found down on the highway. Chief complaint is 'sick person', but it looks like a syncopal episode. A bystander said he just collapsed while walking. He's alert but a bit confused, GCS is 14. He's complaining of some abdominal pain, neck pain, and nausea. He rates his pain a 1 out of 10. His vitals are pretty stable: BP is 119 over 69, heart rate is 61, respirations are 15, and he’s 100 percent on room air. We established IV access and did a physical assessment. Per our General-Heat Exhaustion protocol, we gave him 2.9 milligrams of Albuterol orally, and 1.1 milligrams of EPINEPHrine 0.1 MG/ML, also orally. We've been doing continuous assessments en route. His past medical history includes COPD and hypertension. He is allergic to iodine. We've informed the receiving nurse.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient collapsed while walking on the street according to a bystander.

transcript: Alright, so we have a 14-year-old male, found unresponsive by his parents. They called for an eye injury, but that was a misinterpretation. He's been complaining of a sore throat, back pain, and some nausea with vomiting all day. On arrival, he was GCS 11, just moaning to pain, so we'll call him unresponsive on the AVPU scale. He also had a pretty significant nosebleed. Vitals were blood pressure 148 over 83, heart rate 121, respirations 19, and an SpO2 of 93 percent on room air. We got a blood glucose, did a 3-lead and a 12-lead, which showed sinus tach. We followed the Airway-Rapid Sequence Induction protocol for his decreased GCS. Per protocol, we gave Aspirin, 303.14 milligrams IM, and also EPINEPHrine 0.1 MG/ML, that was 1.03 milligrams PO. He has no known allergies or medical history that the parents were aware of. The primary impression is generalized abdominal pain given his presentation. We're bringing him in as an Emergent patient.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found unresponsive by parents after a day of complaining of sore throat, back pain, and nausea with vomiting.

transcript: Alright, we're here with a 27-year-old male from a service garage. He called for chest pain, but on arrival, his pain was more generalized abdominal. He's very anxious. He also complains of dizziness, some neck pain, and he's had a nosebleed since we got there. He says he feels feverish. He denies any allergies. He takes Zoloft for anxiety. Vitals are BP 171 over 117, heart rate is 87, respirations are 17, and SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. He's a GCS 15, completely alert. Pain scale is a zero right now. We did a physical assessment, established an IV, and gave him 282.68 milligrams of Aspirin orally and 3.51 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually for his dizziness. Primary impression is generalized abdominal pain. We used the General-Dizziness protocol for treatment.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient at his workplace called for chest pain, which on assessment was more generalized abdominal pain accompanied by anxiety, dizziness, and neck pain.

transcript: Good morning, we have a 73-year-old female patient for you, picked up from a school. Initial call was for a psychiatric problem. We found her in significant respiratory distress. She has a history of COPD and anxiety. Patient is alert, GCS of 15, but is feeling very dizzy, fatigued, and nauseous. She denies any pain, so pain is a zero. Her vitals are a BP of 148 over 100, heart rate 89, respirations 16. Her SpO2 was 87 percent on room air, so we're providing oxygen. We are following our Airway protocol. We've placed her on the ECG monitor. We administered 13.92 milligrams of Ketorolac IV and also gave 56.59 milligrams of Glucose via nebulizer. She says she's allergic to penicillin. We should be at your doors in the next five to ten minutes.

transcript: Alright, we're coming in with a 40-year-old female from a single-family residence. She apparently fainted and hit her head. She's complaining of a headache, generalized abdominal pain, and some left shoulder pain. She rates the pain a 7 out of 10. On exam, she's alert, GCS 15. Her vitals are BP 97 over 60, heart rate 79, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. We initiated the Diabetic Emergency protocol based on her history. Given the syncope and hypotension, we gave 1.18 milligrams of EPINEPHrine 0.1 MG/ML via IV. We also administered Aspirin, 278.83 milligrams sublingually, just in case there's a cardiac component. She has a history of Type 2 Diabetes and high cholesterol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a syncopal episode at home, during which she fell and struck her head.

transcript: Hey, we're coming in with a 67-year-old female, driver in a minor traffic incident. She's extremely anxious. Complaining of 10 out of 10 pain, but it's kind of all over. She seems to have a lot of worries and general malaise and fatigue. No obvious injuries. Vitals are heart rate 133, respiratory rate of 22, blood pressure 104 over 58, and SpO2 is 95 percent. She is alert, but she's pretty confused, so we're giving her a GCS of 12. Primary impression is an anxiety disorder. We did get a 12-lead, which just shows sinus tach. transcript: Alright, hey, we have a 55-year-old male for you, chief complaint of weakness. We found him at his house. His wife said he was doing yard work all morning and then came inside feeling weak and complaining of some upper abdominal pain. He apparently sat down hard on the floor but denies hitting his head. No pain, zero out of ten. We did a 12-lead which just showed sinus tach, got a blood glucose which was normal, and he's been on the 3-lead. No meds given. He has a history of high blood pressure and cholesterol, and he's allergic to sulfa drugs. We're treating under the Heat Exhaustion/Stroke protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was doing yard work all morning, came inside feeling weak with upper abdominal pain, and then sat down hard on the floor.

transcript: Morning. We're inbound with a 68-year-old male, driver in a single-vehicle MVC at a sports area. Our primary is weakness, secondary is dizziness. He's complaining of severe pain, a nine out of ten. Location is his neck, unspecified shoulder, and some unspecified abdominal pain. transcript: Alright, so we have a 28-year-old female, we're coming from the university science building. Chief complaint is traumatic injury, but it's more of an emotional event. She's GCS 15, alert and oriented. Pain is a 4 out of 10 in her abdomen. We established an 18-gauge in the left AC and also started an IO in the right tibia just in case she deteriorated, but we haven't used it. We gave 4.38 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually for her nausea. We're treating this under our anaphylaxis protocol due to the unknown nature of the exposure and symptoms.

transcript: Alright, we're coming in with a 56-year-old male from the high school. He's a teacher, staff called for a "sick person." On arrival, he was complaining of some generalized malaise and a weird tingling, uh, paresthesia in his arms. He's alert but a bit confused, GCS is a 14. Initial vitals are blood pressure 161 over 97, heart rate 78, respirations 19, and his SpO2 was low at 92 percent on room air. He denies any pain now, so it's a zero out of ten. We also gave 54.6 milligrams of Fentanyl IV early on for what he described as severe limb pain, which has since resolved. He has a history of hypertension and is allergic to penicillin.

transcript: We've got a 67-year-old male we picked up on the highway, initial call was for a psychiatric problem. PD was on scene, said he was wandering in traffic. transcript: We've got a 21-year-old male from a trade area, initial call was for a psychiatric problem. Co-workers found him extremely weak and altered. transcript: Hey doc, we're coming in with a 77-year-old female from her home, complaining of acute eye pain. She says it started suddenly about two hours ago. transcript: Hey, we're coming in with a 29-year-old male, trauma activation. transcript: Hi, we have a 3-year-old female coming from a daycare with breathing problems. transcript: Hey, we have a 33-year-old male we picked up on the side of the highway. Chief complaint is breathing problems. He states he was driving when he felt suddenly weak and had to pull over. transcript: Alright, we have a 48-year-old male coming in emergent from a single-family residence. We were called for general weakness. His wife states he had what looked like a seizure about 30 minutes prior to our arrival. On assessment, he's alert but very confused, GCS is 11. He has a history of epilepsy. He's allergic to Penicillin. We contacted med control about his tachycardia and they advised Adenosine, so we gave 5.65 milligrams IV. We also gave 2.91 milligrams of Albuterol PO per the Cardiac-STEMI protocol. We've informed the receiving doctor of our findings. We'll be there shortly.

transcript: Alright, we've got a 79-year-old female we picked up from a local grocery store, so a trade/service area. Staff called for a behavioral issue, but she seems more medical. transcript:
Hey, we're bringing you a 77-year-old female from an assisted living facility. They called for a well person check, but she's definitely not well. We found her with a cough, and she's complaining of dysuria and 10 out of 10 generalized pain. She's very confused today per staff. Her GCS is an 11.

transcript: We have a 90-year-old female coming from the school's residential wing. Call was for a sick person. transcript: Morning. This is a 72-year-old male we picked up from a local school, found by staff. He's presenting as a sick person. He seems very anxious and is febrile to the touch. He also has a productive cough. The most concerning thing is some profound aphasia, he can't get any words out. His pain scale is a 6 out of 10 but he can't tell us where. Initial vitals were BP 139 over 83, heart rate is tachy at 115, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 94 percent. He's alert but his GCS is only a 5 due to the verbal component. transcript: Hey doc, we're bringing you an 84-year-old male from the assisted living facility. Staff found him on the floor after hearing a thud; he had a syncopal episode. His initial vitals were a blood pressure of 133 over 73, heart rate 68, respirations 16, and an SpO2 of 96 percent on room air. He has a history of hypertension and atrial fibrillation. His allergies are to codeine. We started a line and for his shoulder pain, we administered 50.03 milligrams of Fentanyl via IV push. We've been doing continuous assessment, and we're treating him under the Sepsis protocol just given his age and the unwitnessed fall.

transcript: Alright, we've got a 67-year-old female from a residence, call was for an unconscious person. Husband said she just fainted. When we got there she was, uh, GCS of 3. She came to pretty quick and is now alert, but she's still complaining of dizziness, nausea, and some shortness of breath. She also had a nosebleed that's since resolved. Her history is significant for hypertension and asthma, she takes lisinopril for it. No known allergies. Vitals are BP 159 over 106, heart rate is tachy at 106, respiratory rate 19, and she's 100 percent on room air. Pain is a zero. transcript: Morning. We're bringing you a 56-year-old male from the group home on Elm Street. transcript: We have a 60-year-old male, initially called out as an MVC. It was a very minor collision; seems more medical in nature. Patient states he suddenly felt very sick while driving. transcript: Hey, we've got a 28-year-old female, Sarah. transcript: Alright, we're bringing you a 63-year-old male we picked up on the highway. It's a bit of a weird one. transcript: Alright, we have an 11-year-old female, found by her parents with an altered mental status. Chief complaint is likely an overdose. transcript: Hey team, we've got a 66-year-old male coming from a school, chief complaint is just 'sick person'. Coworkers called because he seemed to have an altered mental status. transcript: Okay, we have a 100-year-old male found on the street, possible unwitnessed fall. Chief complaint was for abdominal pain. He is alert with a GCS of 15. transcript:

transcript:

transcript:
Morning. We're bringing you a 66-year-old male from a local auto shop. The call came in as a 'well person check', but we're thinking it's more of an altered mental status. transcript: Morning. We have a 40-year-old female from an institution, dispatched for an eye problem. The primary issue is actually an altered mental status. The patient is aphasic and has a fever. She does not follow commands but is alert. Her GCS is a 5. Based on posturing, we're estimating her pain at a 7 out of 10. Her vitals show a blood pressure of 166 over 103, heart rate is 89, respirations are 19, and her SpO2 is 96 percent on room air. We obtained a 12-lead. She began to retch, so we gave 4.03 milligrams of Ondansetron IM. We are following the General-Eye Injury protocol. Her past medical history is significant for schizophrenia and hypertension. There are no known drug allergies listed for her.

pcr_json.events_leading: Dispatched for an eye problem at an institution, but found patient with a primary issue of altered mental status.

transcript: Okay, we're coming to you from the institutional residence on Elm. We have a 60-year-old male, initial call was for an eye problem but it's much more than that. He's got a GCS of 10. He's alert but very confused. He's complaining of shortness of breath, a cough, some fatigue, and unspecified abdominal pain he rates a 3 out of 10. Vitals are BP 92 over 55, heart rate is 89, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 100 percent on room air. Staff gave him 0.49 milligrams of Hydromorphone IM for pain before we got there. We established IV access. Given his hypotension, we're thinking this is more than just pain. Primary impression is essential hypertension that's poorly controlled. We're on the general chest pain protocol. He has a history of Type 2 Diabetes and is allergic to Codeine.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was at an institutional residence when staff called for an eye problem. Upon arrival, EMS found the patient with a decreased level of consciousness, complaining of systemic symptoms including shortness of breath and abdominal pain. Facility staff had administered an IM pain medication prior to EMS arrival.

transcript: Alright, this is Edna. She's a 94-year-old female we picked up at her home. Family called because she was just generally sick and weak. She's complaining of a bit of everything: shortness of breath, a cough, nausea and vomiting, and some diffuse abdominal pain she rates a 2 out of 10. She also feels feverish. Her vitals are stable: BP is 135 over 85, heart rate is 76, respirations are 17, and her SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. GCS is 15, she's alert and oriented. Past history includes COPD and hypertension. She is allergic to penicillin. We did a 12-lead, which was unremarkable. Per protocol, we administered 1.13 milligrams of epinephrine sublingually. We followed the General-Headache protocol. Her breathing seems a little easier now.

transcript: Morning. We have a 63-year-old female from a downtown office building, a psychiatric call. Co-workers were concerned about altered mental status. Patient is alert, GCS is 14. Vitals: BP is 123 over 83, heart rate is 100, respirations 21, and her SpO2 is 91 percent on room air. We started an IV and moved her to the stretcher. Uh, this one's a bit strange, we had a long consult with the doctor. Per their order, we gave 21.14 milligrams of Heparin IM and 46.47 milligrams of Fentanyl PO for her pain. We're not really sure what's going on, but we're bringing her in for a general medical exam to rule out an organic cause for the AMS.

transcript: We're coming in with a 46-year-old male from a single-family residence. The initial call was for a sick person. He has generalized abdominal pain he rates a 10 out of 10. He's also reporting pain in an unspecified shoulder and had an episode of epistaxis before we got there. He's alert but seems confused, GCS is a 12. transcript: We have a 98-year-old male we picked up from his residence. The initial call was for an eye problem, but on arrival, the main issue was generalized weakness. Vitals are blood pressure 133 over 91, heart rate 89, and respiratory rate 19. His SpO2 is low at 87 percent on room air. We've been providing continuous assessment and placed him on a 3-lead EKG. He was quite lethargic on arrival, so we administered 2.04 milligrams of Naloxone IV. For his nausea, we gave 3.68 milligrams of Ondansetron via nebulizer. His history includes COPD and chronic back pain. He has no known drug allergies. Our primary impression is pain in limb.

transcript: Okay, we're bringing in a 70-year-old female, chief complaint of non-traumatic chest pain, though the story is a bit weird. Her husband called because she suddenly became weak and had difficulty speaking. On our arrival, she was definitely aphasic. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 149 over 95, heart rate is 68, respirations 21, and her SpO2 was low at 91 percent. She's a GCS of 15, alert but struggling with words. She denies any pain, so a zero out of ten. We did an EKG and started monitoring. We gave her 343.15 milligrams of Aspirin via IV and a dose of Nitroglycerin, 0.4 milligrams, also IV. transcript: Hey, we're coming in with a 56-year-old female from the reform school. Staff called for eye problems and altered mental status. She's alert but very confused, GCS is a 10. She complains of neck pain and general fatigue, rating her pain a 3 out of 10. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 130 over 78, heart rate is tachycardic at 103, respirations 16, and an SpO2 of 96 percent. Her history is significant for Type 2 Diabetes and she's allergic to Sulfa drugs. Given the presentation and a high blood sugar on scene, our impression is alcohol intoxication with hyperglycemia. We initiated cardiac monitoring and established an IV. Per our sepsis protocol for altered patients, we gave 115.0 milligrams of Methylprednisolone via nebulizer and 1.78 milligrams of Naloxone orally. No change in status en route.

transcript:
Hey, we've got a 43-year-old male for you, found on the highway after a witnessed syncopal episode. Chief complaint is unconsciousness. He's alert now, GCS of 15. His main symptom is just profound fatigue. He does have some generalized pain which he rates a 5 out of 10. We're considering this an Emergent patient.

His vitals are a bit of a mixed bag: BP is hypertensive at 169 over 108, heart rate is 63, respirations are 18, and his initial SpO2 was low at 90 percent. He's allergic to Codeine and has a history of hypertension. We have him on continuous pulse oximetry and did a blood glucose check, which was normal. For his symptoms, we gave 4.49 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually for nausea and 3.22 milligrams of Morphine IV for pain. We're treating his primary issue as an unspecified illness.

transcript:
Hey doc, we have a 51-year-old male, found at his residence, complaining of nausea and vomiting. Our primary impression is acute pain, though he denies any, rating it a zero out of ten. He's been throwing up what looks like coffee grounds for the last hour and seems pretty disoriented. Family says he has a history of hypertension and GERD. He takes Lisinopril but hasn't had it today. He's allergic to Penicillin.

His initial vitals were blood pressure 152 over 105, heart rate of 95, respirations 17, and an SpO2 of 94 percent on room air. He's alert with a GCS of 15. On scene, we established IV access, checked a blood sugar which was normal, and placed him on a 3-lead monitor. Per our General Anaphylaxis protocol, which was the closest fit for the altered mental status, we administered 0.49 milligrams of Atrovent IV and 0.46 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IM. No changes noted en route.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of nausea, followed by vomiting coffee-ground like material and becoming disoriented at home.

transcript:
We're en route with a 63-year-old male from the adult learning annex. Chief complaint is breathing problems. Staff said he suddenly became confused in class, complaining of chest pain and weakness. When we got there, he had an active nosebleed. Our impression is altered mental status, with a secondary of hypotension. He has a history of hypertension and is allergic to penicillin.

He's alert, GCS is 15. He rates the chest pain a 6 out of 10 and also reports some unspecified abdominal pain. His pressure is way up at 179 over 114, heart rate 77, respirations are 22, and he's 99 percent on room air. We performed a 12-lead. We established an IV and converted it to a saline lock. We gave 319.84 milligrams of Aspirin via IM injection and 4.32 milligrams of Ondansetron IV for his nausea. Per dispatch, we initially used the General-Neonatal Care protocol. He seems to be stabilizing. See you in a few.

transcript: Alright, we have a 48-year-old female, chief complaint of breathing problems. We found her at her residence. transcript: We're bringing you a 38-year-old female from the local school. Call came in as 'eye problems,' but she's mainly complaining of other fatigue and some neck pain, or cervicalgia. She rates the pain a 1 out of 10. She's alert but seems a bit off; GCS is a 10. Her vitals are blood pressure 107 over 60, heart rate 82, respiratory rate 22, with an SpO2 of 93 percent on room air. It's a hot day, so we went with the General Heat Exhaustion protocol. We performed phlebotomy for labs and did catheterization of a vein to establish an IV. She felt nauseous, so we gave 4.68 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually. Her PMH is only significant for hypothyroidism, and she has a known allergy to codeine.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient, a school employee, reported feeling fatigued with neck pain on a hot day, prompting an evaluation for heat exhaustion.

transcript: Morning. We have an 81-year-old male, driver in an MVC. Chief complaint is hemorrhage from a scalp laceration, which we've controlled. He was found unrestrained. His mental status is declining. On scene, he was hyperventilating, but en route his respiratory rate dropped to 8. His GCS is down to 5. He's complaining of chest pain and unspecified abdominal pain, rates it a 6 out of 10. His vitals are BP 115 over 70, heart rate is 98, and SpO2 is 96 percent. We have him on a 3-lead monitor and continuous pulse oximetry. He has no known allergies. We gave 1.14 milligrams of Epinephrine orally per the General-Sepsis protocol. His primary impression is generalized muscle weakness. He definitely needs continuous supervision.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was an unrestrained driver in a motor vehicle collision, sustained a scalp laceration, and has shown a declining mental status en route.

transcript: Hey, we're about five minutes out with a 65-year-old male from the university. Chief complaint is near-syncope. We found him at the campus health clinic. Staff said he was teaching a class, felt overwhelmed with anxiety, got dizzy, and felt hot before slumping in his chair. He never fully lost consciousness. On our arrival, he was alert, GCS is 15. He's complaining of a six out of ten headache. He has a history of hypertension and generalized anxiety disorder. First set of vitals showed a blood pressure of 181 over 126, heart rate 114, respirations 18, and an SpO2 of 92 percent on room air. He's got a fever, too. We started him on a cardiac monitor, and the ECG looks like sinus tach. He was feeling nauseous, so we gave 3.8 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually. He says he has no known allergies. We're thinking this is primarily an anxiety issue, but with that pressure, we're concerned.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Other symptoms and signs involving emotional state
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was teaching a class when he felt overwhelmed with anxiety, got dizzy, and felt hot before slumping in his chair.

transcript: Hey team, we're about five minutes out with an 82-year-old female. We were called to a grocery store for a behavioral issue. On arrival, we found her post-ictal after a witnessed seizure. Bystanders said she was tonic-clonic for about a minute. She's awake now but confused, GCS is a 14. Her chief complaint is just some mild abdominal pain, she rates it a 1 out of 10, and mentioned she had some diarrhea earlier today. Vitals are stable: blood pressure 110 over 60, heart rate 97, respirations 15, and an SpO2 of 96 percent on room air. We did a 12-lead which just showed sinus tach. Her daughter, who met us on scene, states she has a history of epilepsy and hypertension. She's allergic to Penicillin. For her nausea, we gave 4.36 milligrams of Ondansetron IM. We're managing her airway and monitoring en route.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a witnessed one-minute tonic-clonic seizure at a grocery store. Found post-ictal and confused by EMS.

transcript: Alright, we have a delicate one for you. This is a 100-year-old female, found on a street and highway corner. The call came in as an assault, but the patient is a poor historian. Her chief complaint is just general malaise and weakness. She also mentioned having some diarrhea today. She's alert with a GCS of 15 and denies any pain. Her vitals are surprisingly unremarkable: BP is 119 over 78, heart rate 74, respirations 17, and she's 100 percent on room air. Her past medical history is significant for epilepsy. We just performed an evaluation and informed the receiving physician of our impending arrival. No medications were given en route. We're primarily treating this as other malaise. See you shortly.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on a street corner. The call was dispatched as a possible assault, but the patient's main complaints are malaise and weakness.

transcript: Morning. We're bringing you a 66-year-old female from a hair salon, a trade/service area. Chief complaint is near-syncope. Staff called after she felt extremely weak and nauseous while in the chair. She's also complaining of some diffuse abdominal pain and says she feels feverish. Our primary thought is some kind of unspecified injury or illness, with secondary weakness. She's alert, GCS is 13, mainly for some confusion. Vitals are stable: BP 141 over 86, heart rate 85, respirations 17, and SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. She rates her pain as a 1 out of 10. We got a 12-lead which shows sinus tach, and we started a line in her left forearm. For the nausea, we gave 3.41 milligrams of Ondansetron via nebulizer. She has a history of GERD and takes Pantoprazole for it. No known drug allergies. We followed the Trauma-Eye Injury protocol for some reason, I think my partner just clicked the wrong one.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt extremely weak and nauseous while sitting in a chair at a hair salon.

transcript: We have a 27-year-old female, called for breathing problems from her residence. Her partner called after she had a syncopal episode. She's alert, GCS 14, but complaining of severe, 9-out-of-10 pain in her shoulder. She also feels febrile. She has a known history of essential hypertension. Her vitals are BP 159 over 104, heart rate is 108, respirations 14, and SpO2 at 97 percent on room air. We established IV access and acquired a 12-lead EKG, which was unremarkable. Given the atypical presentation, we followed our STEMI protocol and administered 2.9 milligrams of Albuterol via an IM injection. Her breathing has eased slightly. She states she has an allergy to sulfa drugs.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a syncopal episode at home, prompting her partner to call 911 for breathing problems and associated severe shoulder pain.

transcript: Morning. We have an 84-year-old female, picked up from a reform school. Staff called for nausea and vomiting. On arrival, patient was alert and oriented, GCS 15, but in extreme distress. She was complaining of 10-out-of-10 pain in her right leg, some unspecified chest pain, and abdominal pain. She seemed a bit disoriented as we were talking to her. She also mentioned some dysuria. Vitals are: BP 123 over 85, heart rate 105, respirations 21, and SpO2 is 96 percent on room air. Patient has a history of osteoporosis and generalized anxiety disorder. She says she's allergic to sulfa drugs. We placed her on the monitor, acquired a 12-lead, and established an IV in her left arm. For her nausea, we gave 4.35 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually. Due to her distress, we also administered 2.34 milligrams of Midazolam IM. We operated under the General-Neonatal Care protocol. Primary impression is pain in limb.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient, a staff member at a reform school, experienced a sudden onset of severe leg pain, nausea, and abdominal pain, prompting a call from her colleagues.

transcript: Morning. We have a 78-year-old male patient we picked up from a school. Initial call was for an eye problem, but that was nothing. His main issue is he's a bit confused, GCS is 14, and is complaining of severe back pain, a 7 out of 10. He also feels general malaise, has some abdominal pain, and mentioned some dysuria. He is alert. Vitals are: BP 131 over 89, heart rate 99, respirations 21, and SpO2 is 99 percent. We have him on the cardiac monitor and we started a saline lock in his right arm. Just as a precaution for a potential cardiac etiology, we gave him 324 milligrams of Aspirin orally. His only listed allergy is to Codeine.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient initially called for an eye problem but was found to be confused with severe back pain.

transcript: We have a 46-year-old male we picked up on the side of the highway, initial call was for a sick person. He was found slumped over by a passerby. On arrival, he was alert, GCS of 15. He's complaining of weakness, some abdominal pain, and significant neck pain, which he rates a 4 out of 10. He denies any trauma but due to the neck pain and location, we placed him in a C-collar. Vitals are stable: BP is 116 over 74, heart rate is 110, respirations 17, and SpO2 is 99 percent. We established a line. He has a history of asthma. We heard some slight wheezes so we gave him 2.75 milligrams of Albuterol via nebulizer. We also administered 1.96 milligrams of Naloxone sublingually as a precaution, though he denies any opiate use. He has no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by a bystander slumped against a wall on the side of a highway. He was alert on arrival but complained of weakness, neck pain, and abdominal pain.

transcript: Hey, we're about five minutes out with a 33-year-old female we picked up on the street and highway. Chief complaint was Unconscious / Fainting. Bystanders saw her collapse. She says before it happened she had some unspecified angina pectoris, pain in her unspecified shoulder, and some unspecified abdominal pain. She also has a persistent cough. Our primary impression is epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus, and a secondary of pain in limb, unspecified. She is alert, GCS is 14. Vitals are 116 over 68, heart rate 92, respirations 20, SpO2 is 94 percent. She rates her shoulder pain a 7 out of 10. We performed catheterization of a vein, placed her on cardiac monitoring and respired carbon dioxide monitoring, and we are informing the doctor. Per protocol, Cardiac Arrest-Return of Spontaneous Circulation, we gave 52.04 milligrams of Fentanyl sublingually for pain, 3.28 milligrams of Ondansetron IV for nausea, and 7.06 milligrams of Adenosine via nebulizer for respiratory symptoms. She's an emergent patient.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced chest pain, shoulder pain, and abdominal pain before a syncopal episode on a public street.

transcript: Alright, we're inbound with a 14-year-old female from a group home. Staff called for a possible seizure, chief complaint was listed as an eye problem, not sure why. On arrival, she was post-ictal. She's awake but confused, GCS is 13, and she's got some aphasia. She has a known history of epilepsy. Her vitals are a bit wild: BP 161 over 95, heart rate 106, respirations 20, and her SpO2 was 88 on room air. We've got her on high flow O2 now. She's complaining of generalized body pain, about a 4 out of 10. She also had an episode of diarrhea. We started an IV, did a 12-lead, and gave her 3.22 milligrams of Ondansetron for nausea. Per our chest pain protocol for her hypertension, we gave 0.45 milligrams of sublingual Nitroglycerin. No known allergies.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a seizure at her group home and was found in a post-ictal state by EMS.

transcript: We're coming in with a 1-year-old female from a daycare facility. The complaint was an eye injury, but it looks more like a traumatic injury. Staff said she fell and now has pain in her shoulder, and she's been vomiting. She also has a persistent cough. Our primary is dorsalgia, secondary altered mental status. She's alert but crying inconsolably. GCS is a 15. Her vitals are pretty concerning: BP 165 over 102, heart rate is 110, respirations 19, and sats are 96 percent. She appears to have essential hypertension based on that reading. She's an 8 out of 10 on the pain scale based on our assessment. We initiated an IV under the General-Eye Injury protocol. For pain and presumed nausea, we gave 295.18 milligrams of Aspirin IV and 4.56 milligrams of Ondansetron SL. No known past medical history or allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient fell at daycare, causing shoulder pain and subsequent vomiting.

transcript: Hey, we're five minutes out with a 23-year-old male found on the street by bystanders. He's got some kind of mental disorder as our primary impression. He's alert but seems confused, GCS is a 14. He's complaining of a cough, some diarrhea, and says he feels like he has a fever. He's hypotensive at 95 over 61, heart rate is 64, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 94 percent. The call was for a diabetic problem, and he does have a history of Type 1 Diabetes and schizophrenia. We're running him under the headache protocol. We performed an adult care assessment and got him on the EKG. He was quite agitated, so we administered 2.95 milligrams of Midazolam IV and 55.51 milligrams of Fentanyl IV. Current meds are Olanzapine and Insulin.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by bystanders on the street, appearing confused and agitated.

transcript: Hey Doc, we're bringing in a 77-year-old male for non-traumatic back pain. We picked him up at a restaurant. He stated he suddenly felt overcome with dizziness and giddiness, then sat down hard. Now he complains of weakness. He's also had some diarrhea for the past day. He's totally alert, GCS of 15. He denies any pain now, rates it a zero. Vitals are solid: blood pressure 120 over 78, heart rate 81, respirations 20, SpO2 is 99 percent. He says he has no significant past medical history. As a precaution for the mechanism of sitting down hard, we did perform cervical spine immobilization before moving him to our stretcher. We used the General-Neonatal Care protocol, which had us give 2.37 milligrams of Naloxone IV. We're about 10 minutes out.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt suddenly dizzy and weak at a restaurant, then sat down hard in a chair.

transcript: Good morning. We are bringing you a 61-year-old female from an apartment complex, primary complaint is just 'sick person.' She's been feeling very fatigued with a fever for two days. Today she developed neck and back pain, which she rates a 4 out of 10. She is alert and oriented, GCS 15. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 144 over 89, heart rate is tachycardic at 108, respirations 16, and her SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. We've established an IV in her right arm. Her past medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis and hypothyroidism. She reports an allergy to Codeine. As per dispatch, we've initiated the General-Eye Injury protocol for transport. We're about 5 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient has had a fever and fatigue for two days, with an onset of neck and back pain today.

transcript: Hey doc, this is Sarah, a 45-year-old female we picked up from a local school. The call was for an unconscious person. She's presenting with significant weakness and is definitely altered. She's complaining of some chest pain that feels like angina, plus unspecified abdominal pain and just a lot of fatigue. She rates the pain an 8 out of 10. Her initial vitals were a blood pressure of 133 over 86, heart rate of 101, respirations at 17, and an SpO2 of 94 percent on room air. Her GCS is 11, but she is Alert on the AVPU scale. We've initiated venous catheterization and have been monitoring her with capnography. She has a history of Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension. She's allergic to Sulfa drugs. We're following the General-Diabetic Emergency protocol and have administered 0.92 milligrams of EPINEPHrine orally. The events leading up to this seem to be that she felt weak after skipping a meal and then fainted.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt weak after skipping a meal and then fainted.

transcript: Evening. We're bringing in a 100-year-old male from a single-family residence. Family called for a psychiatric problem; they said he became very agitated. Our primary thought is that he's febrile. On arrival, he was alert and oriented, GCS is 15. He's had diarrhea for two days. He's also showing some signs of aphasia, which is new according to the family. He rates his abdominal pain a five out of ten. Vitals are BP 164 over 99, heart rate 90, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. We started an IV and have him on capnography. He has a history of atrial fibrillation and COPD. He's allergic to sulfa drugs. The family states they gave him 44.74 milligrams of his prescribed Fentanyl orally for the pain about an hour before we got there. We think this is more of a sepsis picture. ETA is 5 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced two days of diarrhea, followed by the new onset of aphasia and agitation after family administered oral Fentanyl for abdominal pain.

transcript: Hey Doc, we're coming in with a 73-year-old male from a workshop on the east side. Chief complaint is generalized weakness. Uh, we found him sitting on a stool, just looking pale. He says he suddenly felt extremely weak about an hour ago. He's also complaining of some neck pain and a vague abdominal pain, but he rates it a zero out of ten. His initial vitals are pretty impressive: blood pressure is 205 over 136, heart rate is 96, respirations are 19, and he's saturating at 93 percent on room air. He's alert but seems a little slow to respond, GCS is a 14. He denies any allergies. History includes hypertension and CAD. We established an 18-gauge in his right AC, but that's it for interventions. We'll be there in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of extreme weakness while at his workshop, accompanied by neck and abdominal pain.

transcript: Okay, so we're here with an 85-year-old male from his residence. Family called because he was acting erratically and seemed extremely weak. He's been worried about his health since having a bout of diarrhea over the last day. Our primary impression is a person with a feared health complaint, but no clear diagnosis. His initial vitals were pretty concerning: blood pressure 184 over 126, heart rate is 101, respirations are 21, and he's saturating at 92 percent on room air. He's responsive to verbal stimuli, so AVPU is 'V', with a GCS of 13. He denies any pain. He has a history of hypertension and BPH. He's allergic to penicillin. We just did a basic evaluation, no meds given. We followed the General-Neonatal Care protocol per our system.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Person with feared health complaint in whom no diagnosis is made
pcr_json.events_leading: Family called after patient began acting erratically and seemed extremely weak following a day-long bout of diarrhea.

transcript: We have a 100-year-old female we picked up from a beauty salon. The call was for a sick person. She was complaining of generalized weakness, some neck pain, and unspecified abdominal pain. She also had an episode of hematemesis just before we got there and mentioned some paresthesia in her hands. She's alert and oriented, GCS is 15. She denies any pain right now, zero out of ten. Her vitals are a little concerning: BP 138 over 90, heart rate is tachycardic at 126, respirations are 25, and her SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. We put her on the cardiac monitor, showed A-fib with RVR. Per protocol, we gave Diltiazem 12.08 milligrams sublingually and Atrovent 0.45 milligrams orally. She has a history of atrial fibrillation and GERD. No known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed weakness, neck pain, abdominal pain, and hematemesis while at a beauty salon.

transcript: We have a 71-year-old male we picked up on the side of the highway. Call came in for an unconscious person. We're thinking it's an unspecified cardiac arrhythmia. He's alert now but confused, so GCS is 14. He complains of significant weakness and other fatigue, some unspecified abdominal pain, and feels very anxious. Rates his pain a 6 out of 10. His vitals are BP 123 over 72, heart rate is 89, respirations are 14, and SpO2 is 92 percent on room air. We put him on some oxygen. We've done a 12-lead and started respired carbon dioxide monitoring. Past medical history includes hypertension and COPD. He takes Lisinopril for his blood pressure. No allergies that he knows of. We're about 15 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt suddenly weak and anxious while driving, pulled over, and lost consciousness.

transcript: Okay, we're bringing you a 27-year-old female from the university campus, initial call was for back pain. Her roommate called because the patient became confused and started vomiting what looked like coffee grounds. On exam, she has altered mental status and is responsive only to pain. She's also had diarrhea and complains of general fatigue. Her blood pressure is 143 over 89, heart rate is tachy at 111, respirations 16, and she's saturating at 92 percent. GCS is a 15, but AVPU is to Pain. Her back pain she rates a 3 out of 10. We did a physical assessment and got a 12-lead EKG. We gave her 4.47 milligrams of Ondansetron orally for the vomiting, and following our airway protocol for the low sats, we also gave 1.19 milligrams of Epinephrine sublingually. Primary impression is altered mental status. We'll be there shortly.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed acute back pain, then became confused and started vomiting coffee grounds, prompting roommate to call.

transcript: Okay, we have a 50-year-old male, a teacher here at the school. Chief complaint is near-syncope and abdominal pain. He was found by a student slumped over his desk. On our arrival, he was alert but very confused, GCS is a 9. He's complaining of generalized abdominal pain, rates it an 8 out of 10, and also some nausea. He's showing some signs of aphasia and has pronounced weakness on his right side. He also mentioned some difficulty swallowing. Vitals are BP 163 over 107, heart rate is 111, respirations are 18, and he's saturating at 91 percent on room air. We put him on a 3-lead, but that's the only procedure. No meds given. His past history includes hypertension, for which he takes Lisinopril. He has no known drug allergies. Our primary impression is generalized abdominal pain. We're following the General-Neonatal Care protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by a student slumped over his desk at school.

transcript: Good morning. We are en route with a 71-year-old male, found on the side of the highway after a call for a well person check. He was just sitting in his car, looking ill. He is alert, but a little confused, GCS is a 13. He's complaining of some vague, non-radiating chest pain, a 3 out of 10, along with some abdominal pain and an earache. He also mentioned feeling very fatigued. The main concern is his heart rate—he's profoundly bradycardic at 44. Blood pressure is 129 over 85, respirations are slow at 12, and SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. We've been doing continuous physical assessment. We have him on a 3-lead and we've acquired a 12-lead as well. He was nauseous so we gave him 4.67 milligrams of Ondansetron orally, which he tolerated. He has a history of a prior MI. No known drug allergies. ETA is 5 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found looking ill in his car on the side of a highway by a bystander who called for a well person check.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 60-year-old male we picked up from the college campus. The initial call was for a fire alarm, but we found him in his room complaining of profound weakness. He has a history of end-stage renal disease and COPD. He's alert but a little slow, GCS is a 14. He's also complaining of some pain in an unspecified shoulder, but rates it a zero out of ten. He feels warm to the touch and appears febrile. Vitals are, uh, blood pressure 157 over 106, heart rate is 59, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. We're treating this under the sepsis protocol. We've done continuous physical assessments. We did give 41.57 milligrams of Fentanyl sublingually per a physician order. We've already informed the receiving doctor.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found with profound weakness after a fire alarm activation on the college campus.

transcript: Hey, we have a 100-year-old female, found at an 'other place', not specified. The call came in as a psychiatric problem, and our field impression is an unspecified mental disorder. When we arrived, we found her unresponsive, GCS is 3. Dispatch notes said she was alert initially. Bystanders said she was complaining of shoulder pain and unspecified abdominal pain before she lost consciousness, rating her pain a 5 out of 10. She also has a fever and had an episode of epistaxis. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 133 over 80, heart rate is bradycardic at 58, respirations 18, and SpO2 96 percent. We established IV access. Per the Trauma-Eye Injury protocol, we administered 323.83 milligrams of Aspirin intramuscularly. We were unable to obtain any allergies or medical history.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was complaining of shoulder and abdominal pain, had a nosebleed, and then became unresponsive.

transcript: Hey, Medic 3, inbound to your location with an 84-year-old male from the assisted living facility on Main. Chief complaint is syncope. Staff found him on the floor, he passed out while trying to get out of bed. He's alert now, GCS 15, but is in severe distress. He's complaining of 10 out of 10 unspecified abdominal pain and is short of breath. Vitals are blood pressure 117 over 79, heart rate is tachy at 130, respirations 18, SpO2 98 percent. Past history of CAD and dementia, allergic to Codeine. We initiated monitoring and pulse oximetry. Per the Airway protocol for pain management, we administered 58.6 milligrams of Fentanyl IV and 53.78 milligrams of Ketamine sublingually. Primary impression is syncope and collapse. See you in a few.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient passed out while attempting to get out of bed at an assisted living facility. Found on the floor by staff.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 72-year-old female, chief complaint is an animal bite. She was at her residence and was bitten on the arm by her dog. The bite itself is minor. The concerning part is her mental status. She is alert on the AVPU scale—she opens her eyes and tracks—but she's non-verbal and only withdraws to pain. We're scoring her a GCS of 5. It's a strange presentation. On arrival, she also had an active epistaxis and a persistent cough. Vitals are BP 108 over 65, heart rate 73, respirations 15, and SpO2 is 93 percent on room air. She isn't indicating any pain. We performed an evaluation and initiated a saline lock. No medications were given. Husband on scene states she has a past medical history of COPD and hypertension.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was bitten on the arm by her dog at her home, after which she became acutely non-verbal with a decreased level of consciousness.

transcript: Alright, we're bringing you a 99-year-old female from the service area of a nursing home. Staff found her down, chief complaint was fainting. Our primary impression is unspecified injury from the fall. She is altered, GCS is a 5. She's been coughing for a couple of days and has some dyspnea. She's complaining of right shoulder pain, rates it a 7 out of 10. Also experiencing nausea with vomiting. Vitals are looking septic—BP is 89 over 50, heart rate is 120, respirations 24, and SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. We initiated the sepsis protocol. We have her on a 3-lead and ETCO2 monitoring. We established IV access in the left AC and I've already called in a physician consult to inform them of our findings. She has a history of dementia and CHF, and is allergic to Sulfa drugs. No meds given en route.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found down by staff at a nursing home after a fall. She has had a cough for several days.

transcript: Okay, so we've got an 83-year-old male, found at a hardware store where he works. It looks like he fell off a short ladder. Chief complaint is traumatic injury. He is alert, GCS is 15, but he's having significant trouble speaking, uh, some definite aphasia. He's complaining of severe neck pain and chest pain that gets worse when he breathes. Rates the pain an 8 out of 10. He also has a pretty good nosebleed going. His initial vitals were blood pressure 163 over 105, heart rate of 90, respirations 17, and his SpO2 was low at 90 percent on room air. We've got him on a couple liters via nasal cannula now. We've just been doing continuous assessment, and I let the receiving doc know we were coming. No meds were given. He has a history of hypertension and CVA, allergies are to penicillin.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient fell from a short ladder at his workplace.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 50-year-old male coming to you from a trade service area. He called for generalized weakness and an excruciating pain he rates at a 10 out of 10. He has a productive cough and states a history of COPD. On exam, he's alert and fully oriented, GCS of 15. Initial vitals are blood pressure 132 over 73, heart rate is tachycardic at 112, respirations 16, and SpO2 of 98 percent on room air. We performed a 12-lead which was unremarkable and his capillary blood glucose was normal. For his symptoms, we administered 2.44 milligrams of Midazolam via nebulizer and gave him 3.34 milligrams of Zofran, that was sublingual. No known drug allergies. ETA is about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced onset of generalized weakness and severe, excruciating pain while at work.

transcript: Hi there, we have a 32-year-old male, called from his home for a diabetic problem. His main complaint is actually some unspecified chest pain, general fatigue, and some vague abdominal pain. He is alert and oriented, GCS of 15. Vitals are blood pressure 153 over 93, heart rate is tachycardic at 126, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 99 percent on room air. He denies any pain, rating it a zero out of ten. We have him on the cardiac monitor and acquired a 12-lead EKG, which showed no acute changes. He has a past history of Type 1 Diabetes and asthma. Per his home regimen for a recent illness, we assisted him in taking 105.57 milligrams of Methylprednisolone orally. He has no allergies. See you in ten.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was at his residence when he began experiencing non-specific chest pain, fatigue, and abdominal pain, prompting an EMS call for a diabetic problem.

transcript: Hey team, we're coming in with a 28-year-old female, emergent. Chief complaint is dizziness following a fall at the university science building where she works. Apparently, she was startled and fell against a counter, causing a pretty deep laceration to her left forearm from some broken glass. She's also complaining of some weakness and altered mental status, just a bit confused. She rates her arm pain a 4 out of 10. Vitals are BP 177 over 108, heart rate is 88, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. She's a GCS 15, alert and oriented. Past medical history of COPD and Type 2 Diabetes. She's allergic to penicillin. We followed the chest pain protocol due to her dizziness and weakness. We started her on O2, did some monitoring, and gave Aspirin, 278.16 milligrams PO.
