transcript: We're en route with a 92-year-old female, picked up from a local school where she was attending an event. The call was for a psychiatric problem, but we think it's more medical. Our primary impression is other malaise and weakness. She's alert and oriented, GCS of 15. She complains of some unspecified pain, a three out of ten, and has been nauseous with one episode of vomiting. Her past history includes Type 2 Diabetes and dementia. Her vitals are blood pressure 138 over 94, heart rate 76, respirations 21, SpO2 96 percent. We established an IV in her left arm. Given her history and presentation, we followed the General-Eye Injury protocol which for us covers altered mental status. We gave 57.59 milligrams of glucose via IM injection. No change in her status.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced an acute onset of malaise, weakness, and nausea while attending an event at a school.

transcript: Good morning. This is a 91-year-old female we picked up from her home. Initially called for diabetic problems, but she appears to have had a fall. We're thinking unspecified head injury. Family found her on the floor this morning; she was complaining of fatigue and had a fever all day yesterday. She is alert, GCS 13, but a little slow to respond. Her vitals are BP 163 over 92, heart rate is 70, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 96 percent. She's complaining of head pain, about a 4 out of 10. We have her on the cardiac monitor and started a line. Um, past history includes Type 2 Diabetes and Hypertension. Allergies are to Sulfa drugs. Per the Anaphylaxis protocol for some reason, we administered 10.19 milligrams of Diltiazem via nebulizer and also gave 55.87 milligrams of Fentanyl IV for pain.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the floor by family after complaining of fatigue and fever the previous day.

transcript: Alright, we're coming in with a 75-year-old male we picked up from a public library. He called for abdominal pain and weakness. The patient has a major history of anxiety and severe allergies, and he thought he was having a reaction so he self-administered his EpiPen before we got there. That's 1.16 milligrams of Epinephrine IM. On our arrival, he was tachycardic and hypertensive, which we think is from the Epi. Vitals are BP 159 over 106, heart rate 114, respirations 20, and SpO2 is 98 percent. He also had a fever, so we gave 675.68 milligrams of Acetaminophen IV. He's alert but anxious, GCS is a 14. He only rates his pain a 1 out of 10 now. We checked his sugar and it was normal. We also have him on capnography. He says his only medical problem is GERD. We are treating him as a person with a feared health complaint per the Cardiac-Hypertensive Emergency protocol.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Person with feared health complaint in whom no diagnosis is made
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced weakness and abdominal pain and self-administered an EpiPen due to fear of an allergic reaction.

transcript: Hi, this is Medic 5 with a patient report. We're bringing in a 76-year-old male from a trade service area. The call was for a person down. He's alert and oriented, GCS 15. His main complaint is abdominal pain, which he rates a 5 out of 10. He also reports some nausea with vomiting, a cough, and a tingling feeling in his hands. He says he was working when he suddenly felt very dizzy and sick to his stomach before the pain started. On exam, he does feel warm, consistent with a fever. Vitals are blood pressure 130 over 84, heart rate is 84, respirations 21, and SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. He has a history of Afib and takes Eliquis. No known drug allergies. We have him on the cardiac monitor and started a saline lock. We’ll be there in about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient suddenly felt dizzy and nauseous with abdominal pain while working, leading him to sit down and feel unable to get up.

transcript: Morning, we're bringing you a 74-year-old male from his residence, complaining of chest pain and fatigue. He's alert, GCS 14. He rates his chest pain a 4 out of 10. Vitals are BP 103 over 59, heart rate 122, respirations are slow at 10, and SpO2 is 91 percent on room air. Per protocol, we administered 371.8 milligrams of Aspirin via nebulizer. We also acquired a 12-lead EKG and his blood glucose was normal. His past history includes anxiety and CAD. No known drug allergies. Our primary impression is cardiac arrest, though he's obviously not in arrest now. Secondary is an anxiety disorder. We're working off the Trauma-Eye Injury protocol, per the system. See you in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed chest pain and fatigue at his home, prompting a call for emergency services.

transcript: Hey, we're about five minutes out with a 70-year-old female from a residence. The family called for increasingly erratic behavior. Our primary impression is acute respiratory distress syndrome, possibly secondary to a UTI. She's alert, GCS is 15, but she's hyperventilating and seems fatigued. She’s complaining of some generalized abdominal pain, about a 3 out of 10. Initial vitals were blood pressure 154 over 105, heart rate of 112, respiratory rate 23, and her SpO2 was low at 90 percent on room air. We've placed her on the cardiac monitor and started an IV. We did give 3.7 milligrams of Ondansetron IV for her complaints of nausea. We're following our General-Sepsis protocol. We did inform the receiving physician of our findings. Her past medical history includes hypertension and early-onset dementia. She's allergic to sulfa drugs.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Urinary tract infection, site not specified
pcr_json.events_leading: Family called for patient's increasingly erratic behavior. Patient found hyperventilating, fatigued, and complaining of abdominal pain.

transcript: Morning. We have a 57-year-old male, found unresponsive by his wife at their single-family residence. She says he was complaining of weakness and dizziness all day. On arrival, he was initially unresponsive but came to, and is now a GCS of 15. He's complaining of some other chest pain and significant fatigue. He rates the pain a 5 out of 10. His initial vitals were blood pressure 132 over 87, heart rate was way up at 163, respirations 14, and SpO2 was 99 percent on room air. We established IV access and put him on the monitor. We did a 12-lead which shows sinus tach. Due to the initial unresponsiveness, we gave 2.23 milligrams of Naloxone orally, with no change. For the chest pain, we also administered 352.36 milligrams of Aspirin PO. His history includes GERD and high cholesterol. He has no known drug allergies. We also have him on capnography.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found unresponsive by his wife after complaining of weakness and dizziness throughout the day.

transcript: Hey doc, we have a 59-year-old female, chief complaint of fainting. We found her at her job in a service area. Coworkers said she complained of chest pain, felt hot and nauseous, and then just passed out. On our arrival, she was just coming to. She's been alert and oriented since, GCS is 15. She says the chest pain is an 8 out of 10. Initial vitals were blood pressure 108 over 66, heart rate of 87, respirations 14, and an SpO2 of 97 percent on room air. We put her on a three-lead, which just shows sinus tach. We established an IV. She has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril. She's allergic to codeine. We did give her 4.09 milligrams of Ondansetron via neb for the nausea, 603.64 milligrams of Acetaminophen IM for a slight fever we noted, and, uh, 0.6 milligrams of Ipratropium sublingually, she was feeling a little tight of breath. Her chest pain is still there but the nausea has improved.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient complained of chest pain, feeling hot, and nausea while at work, then experienced a syncopal episode. Coworkers called for an ambulance.

transcript: We have a 51-year-old female from the local high school, initially called for a behavioral problem. She is alert, GCS is 10. She's extremely anxious, complaining of chest pain, some abdominal pain, and neck pain. She also had an episode of epistaxis just before we got there. She rates her pain a 6 out of 10. BP is elevated at 161 over 107, heart rate is 105, respirations are a bit slow at 13, and her SpO2 is 94 percent. We initiated CPAP and are doing continuous assessments. Based on her chest pain and vitals, we followed the eye injury protocol— wait, no, that's not right. Scratch that. We gave 0.43 milligrams of sublingual Nitroglycerin which provided no relief. Her past medical history includes hypertension and anxiety. Primary is a feared health complaint. Secondary is ARDS.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Person with feared health complaint in whom no diagnosis is made
procedures: Continuous positive airway pressure ventilation treatment
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called for a behavioral issue at a school, found with anxiety, chest pain, and a recent nosebleed.

transcript: We have an 84-year-old female from a school, called for a behavioral problem. She's alert and oriented, GCS is 15. She is denying any pain, so a zero out of ten. Staff were just concerned. She does complain of some nausea with vomiting and earlier had what she describes as unspecified angina pectoris. Vitals are BP 154 over 94, heart rate 70, respirations 20, SpO2 is 96 percent. We did an evaluation and a 12-lead. Due to her symptoms, we followed our General-Sepsis protocol. We gave 0.42 milligrams of Ipratropium IM and a rather large dose of Fentanyl, 57.66 milligrams, also IM. Our impression is she's here for a general examination without a specific complaint, maybe some other malaise. She has a history of dementia and COPD.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
pcr_json.events_leading: School staff called due to concerns about the patient's behavior. Patient reported nausea and earlier angina-like symptoms.

transcript: Morning. We're bringing you a 43-year-old female from a local school. The call was for heart problems, she has an AICD. It seems she had a fall. She complains of a nine out of ten pain from the trauma, and also a headache. She feels feverish. She seems a bit confused, maybe some aphasia, but is alert. GCS is 15. Her vitals are pretty impressive: BP is 182 over 109, heart rate is 111, respirations are 12, and she's a hundred percent on room air. History of Afib and hypertension, she takes Eliquis and Metoprolol. She's allergic to Penicillin. We ran the General-Sepsis protocol due to the fever. On the way we gave 0.32 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IV and 0.59 milligrams of Ipratropium via neb. We did a full pain assessment and moved her to our stretcher for transport.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient with a history of heart problems and an AICD sustained a fall at a school, resulting in acute pain, headache, and fever.

transcript: We're coming in with a 65-year-old male found on the highway. The initial dispatch was for a behavioral problem. He's alert but confused, with a GCS of 12. He's complaining of unspecified abdominal pain, which he rates a 2 out of 10. He has a persistent cough and had one episode of hematemesis on scene. We're concerned about his vitals: he's extremely tachycardic with a heart rate of 147, BP is 108 over 63, and respiratory rate is 21. His SpO2 is 100 percent on room air. We've established IV access and have him on a 3-lead monitor, which just shows sinus tach. Primary impression is acute pain, with a secondary of weakness. He states he has a history of liver problems from drinking but takes no meds. He denies any allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient with altered mental status found on the street complaining of abdominal pain and had an episode of hematemesis.

transcript: Hey team, we've got a 65-year-old female coming in from a workshop downtown. Call was for an eye problem after a fall. We think her primary issue is hypertension-related. She tripped over some equipment and fell, hitting her shoulder and neck. A coworker said she initially complained of shoulder pain, rating it a six out of ten, along with some neck pain and fatigue. Her mobility is definitely reduced. She became unresponsive just as we arrived. We found her with a GCS of 4—no eye-opening, incomprehensible sounds, and extension posturing to pain. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 108 over 71, heart rate 110, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 94 percent. We're doing continuous assessments. We did get a 12-lead done. Per her coworker, she has a history of hypertension and arthritis, and is allergic to Penicillin. We gave 49.75 milligrams of Fentanyl IM for her initial pain complaint and 147.84 milligrams of Methylprednisolone IM.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient tripped and fell at her workplace, hitting her shoulder and neck, then became unresponsive upon EMS arrival.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 72-year-old female coming from a local school where she was volunteering. Staff called because she had progressive fatigue all morning, then developed a fever and started sweating profusely. She's complaining of a diffuse, generalized pain at a 9 out of 10. She's alert and oriented, GCS 15. Her vitals are BP 161 over 95, heart rate 94, respirations 21, and SpO2 is 93 percent on room air. Her past history includes Type 2 Diabetes and COPD, and she has an allergy to Sulfa drugs. We initiated an IV, put her on a 3-lead, and started capnography. Under the Sepsis protocol, we administered 45.97 milligrams of Fentanyl via nebulizer for her pain. That's it so far.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced progressive fatigue throughout the morning, which escalated to a fever, profuse sweating, and severe generalized pain.

transcript: We have a 76-year-old male, picked up from a private commercial establishment for a complaint of eye problems. His family called because he was acting anxious and complaining of blurry vision. Shortly after, he had a coughing fit and experienced some hematemesis, looked like coffee grounds. He's also complaining of generalized fatigue and weakness. He’s got a history of hypertension and GERD. His vitals are a BP of 173 over 102, heart rate 96, respirations 18, and an SpO2 of 96 percent. He's a little confused, GCS is 14. He denies any pain. We performed a 12-lead ECG. We're on the Airway protocol and administered 110.42 milligrams of methylprednisolone orally. He's allergic to sulfa drugs. He takes Lisinopril for his blood pressure.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Person with feared health complaint in whom no diagnosis is made
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced blurry vision and anxiety, followed by an episode of coughing and hematemesis.

transcript: Hey doc, we're coming in with a 19-year-old female from the reform school on 5th Street. Staff called for a syncopal episode. They said she was arguing with another student, then just collapsed. On our arrival, she was conscious but very weak and complaining of some pain in her right shoulder. She's got a GCS of 11, just not tracking very well. Uh, initial vitals are blood pressure 155 over 88, heart rate is 55, respirations are 14, and she's satting 96 percent on room air. She denies any pain, so zero out of ten. We established an IV in the left AC. She was feeling pretty nauseous, so we pushed 4.35 milligrams of Ondansetron IM. Her past medical history includes anxiety and depression, and she states she's allergic to codeine. We're about five minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient collapsed following an argument with another student.

transcript: We are inbound with a 66-year-old female we picked up on the side of the highway. Initial call was for eye problems, but she seems to have more going on. Our primary impression is unspecified hypotension with a secondary of weakness. She's alert but a bit confused, GCS 14. She reports shortness of breath, a fever for two days, and some diarrhea. She also complained of nausea, for which we gave 4.46 milligrams of Ondansetron PO. Her pain is a 4 out of 10, just generalized discomfort. Vitals are BP 110 over 73, heart rate 95, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 99 percent. We did some phlebotomy for you guys. Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes and she takes metformin. No known allergies. We're on the Cardiac Arrest-Fibrillation protocol. Should be there shortly.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the side of the highway after calling for an eye problem, which was associated with systemic symptoms of weakness, fever, and shortness of breath.

transcript: Hey doc, we're coming in with a 63-year-old male from the reform school, initially a well-person check. We found him with a chief complaint of severe back pain, rating it a 6 out of 10, and a headache. He's alert, GCS is 15. Vitals are pretty wild—BP is 192 over 128, heart rate is 120, respirations 16, and he's 99 percent on room air. He also mentioned some ear pain and diarrhea starting this morning. History of hypertension and asthma, but he has no known allergies. We started an IV and put him on the monitor. Given his pressure and the wheezing we heard, we followed our airway protocol. We gave him one neb of Albuterol/Ipratropium, three point five four milligrams. We also gave him a dose of Nitroglycerin, zero point three six milligrams IV, but his pressure hasn't budged much. We're thinking this is more than just dorsalgia. See you in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was seen for a well-person check when he was found to have severe back pain, headache, and extremely high blood pressure.

transcript: Good morning. We're bringing you a 56-year-old male from the local college. Staff called for an eye problem, but on arrival we found him with an altered mental status. He's alert but confused, GCS is 15. He was also generally sweaty and complaining of some neck pain, but denies any trauma. Vitals are blood pressure 118 over 79, heart rate of 65, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 96 percent. We checked a blood sugar, it was normal. We put him on a 3-lead and did a 12-lead, both were unremarkable. A coworker mentioned he has a history of end stage renal disease and diabetes. He states he has no known allergies. We haven't administered any medications. His primary issue seems to be the altered mental status. We'll be there in about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced altered mental status, diaphoresis, and neck pain while at work at a college.

transcript: Hello, we have a 91-year-old female we picked up on the side of the highway. Her chief complaint is just being a 'Sick Person.' She has a fever, some epistaxis, diarrhea, and is complaining of dizziness. She rates her pain at an 8 out of 10. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 138 over 78, heart rate 87, respirations 24, and an SpO2 of 87 percent. She's Alert with a GCS of 15. We did our initial patient assessment and established IV access via catheterization of a vein. She has no known drug allergies. Past medical history includes COPD and hypertension. Per protocol, uh, the General-Eye Injury protocol, we administered 2.41 milligrams of Albuterol by mouth. The events leading up to the call were that her car broke down and she began feeling increasingly ill while waiting for help. She also has some reduced mobility.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's car broke down and she began feeling ill while waiting for help.

transcript: We're bringing in a 96-year-old female from her home. The initial call was for a psychiatric problem; family said she was extremely agitated. Our primary impression is cardiac arrest, as a working diagnosis, given her presentation. She's been vomiting and has had diarrhea all day. On arrival, she was alert, GCS 15, but very tachycardic. Her heart rate is 120, BP is 144 over 90, respirations 17, and SpO2 98 percent. She complains of abdominal pain, a 6 out of 10. We have her on the cardiac monitor and have established IV access. We also performed an EKG and have continuous pulse oximetry. For her agitation, we gave 2.7 milligrams of Midazolam sublingually. Family also states they gave her 1.2 milligrams of oral Epinephrine before we arrived. Her history includes dementia and hypertension. She is allergic to Codeine.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a bout of extreme agitation accompanied by vomiting and diarrhea, prompting a 911 call from family.

transcript: Hey, we're bringing you a 55-year-old male from a trade area, called for a possible seizure. Co-workers stated he was acting normal and then had seizure-like activity. He was postictal when we got there but is now alert and oriented, GCS 15. He denies any pain right now, but does complain of some diarrhea. His past medical history includes a seizure disorder, and he says he takes Keppra for it. He has no known drug allergies. Vitals are a bit up: BP is 165 over 101, heart rate is 100, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 99 percent. We put him on two liters of oxygen via nasal cannula just to be safe and got him on the 3-lead monitor. We did administer Ondansetron, 4.36 milligrams, via nebulizer for his complaint of nausea. Our primary impression is just bed confinement status given the post-ictal state on arrival.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a witnessed seizure-like activity at work. Was postictal on EMS arrival.

transcript: Okay, we're coming into Bay 3 with a 64-year-old female from a single-family residence. She is bed-bound. Caregiver called for increasing abdominal pain. Patient also has a fever, some dysuria, and new-onset angina. Her history includes end-stage renal disease and COPD. Her pain is a 6 out of 10. She is alert, but her GCS is only a 5. Vitals are blood pressure 148 over 97, heart rate is 80, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. We followed the Cardiac Arrest-Resuscitation protocol and administered Fentanyl, 51.79 milligrams, by mouth for her pain. We just monitored her pulse otherwise. Primary impression is bed confinement status with a secondary of end stage renal disease. She has no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Caregiver called for increasing abdominal pain, fever, and new chest pain in a bed-bound patient.

transcript: Hey, we're bringing in a 39-year-old male from an institutional residence. Staff found him altered. He was complaining of a severe headache, neck pain, and dysphagia before he became difficult to arouse. Our primary is for an unspecified injury with a secondary of weakness. His vitals are BP 136 over 91, heart rate is tachy at 121, respirations 18, SpO2 96 percent. He appears to be in significant pain, we'd rate it a 7 out of 10. Now, his neuro status is weird... his GCS is a 14, but on the AVPU scale he's Unresponsive. We've initiated ECG monitoring and are managing his airway per protocol. No meds given by us.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found altered by facility staff after complaining of severe headache, neck pain, and difficulty swallowing, subsequently becoming unresponsive.

transcript: Hey, we're bringing you a 37-year-old female from the admin building at the college. Staff called for strange and inexplicable behavior. Patient's chief complaint is dizziness and shortness of breath. She is alert and oriented times four, GCS is 15. She also reports pain in her unspecified shoulder, rating it a 3 out of 10, and some dysuria. Vitals are: BP 152 over 104, heart rate 107, respirations 22, and SpO2 96 percent on room air. We did a full adult care assessment, placed her on the monitor, and got a 12-lead. Per our General-Dizziness protocol, we administered 1.06 milligrams of EPINEPHrine sublingually. Her only past history is anxiety, and she has an allergy to sulfa drugs. We should be there in 5.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient began exhibiting strange behavior at her workplace, complaining of sudden dizziness and shortness of breath, which led her coworkers to call 911.

transcript: Alright, we're inbound to you from a private commercial establishment with a 49-year-old female, emergent, chief complaint of traumatic injury. She was struck by a falling object in a warehouse. Our primary impression is acute abdomen. She’s also got some unspecified injuries. She's alert but confused, GCS is 11. She's complaining of weakness, a headache, and also says she's had a cough and fever for a couple days. Her pressure is low, 73 over 43. Heart rate is 96, respirations are 15, and she's satting 100 percent on room air. She denies any pain, so zero out of ten. We've established an IV and have her on a 3-lead. Per protocol, we gave 45.53 milligrams of Ketamine PO and 10.49 milligrams of Diltiazem SL. Her only known allergy is to Codeine. She has a history of Type 2 diabetes and high cholesterol. She takes Metformin and Atorvastatin.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was struck by a falling object at a warehouse, resulting in abdominal trauma.

transcript: Hey team, we're bringing in a 66-year-old male found on the street, post-ictal. Bystanders said he had a seizure, but we didn't witness it. On arrival, he was alert but confused, GCS is a 14. He's complaining of generalized weakness and has some epistaxis, probably from the fall. He does feel warm, but we didn't get a temp. Vitals are stable but concerning: BP is 156 over 100, heart rate is tachy at 123, respirations are 13, and he's saturating at 98 percent on room air. We put him on a nasal cannula just in case. Per protocol, we gave him 350.35 milligrams of Aspirin IV and 15.12 milligrams of Glucose IM after a fingerstick. He denies any pain. No known allergies. He says he has a history of hypertension.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a witnessed seizure on the street. Found post-ictal, confused, and weak with a nosebleed.

transcript: Incoming with a 100-year-old male from a residence. The call came in as a psychiatric problem, but we believe it was a syncopal episode. His caregiver said he was agitated, then collapsed briefly. He's alert now, GCS is 15. No pain, zero out of ten. The concerning finding is an episode of hematemesis shortly after he woke up. Vitals are BP 132 over 77, heart rate is bradycardic at 54, respirations 17, SpO2 95 percent. History includes A-Fib and a prior GI bleed. He's allergic to Ibuprofen. We've done a full physical assessment. Access was difficult so we established an IO. We're doing digital CO2 monitoring and continuous assessment. For the bradycardia and potential cardiac etiology, we administered 367.37 milligrams of Aspirin orally. We are treating this as an emergent GI bleed with associated syncope.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's caregiver called for agitation, but patient then experienced a syncopal episode. Upon arousal, he had an episode of hematemesis.

transcript: Hey, we're bringing you a 62-year-old male from a school. Chief complaint is an eye injury. He was working in a lab and splashed something in his eye. He's now complaining of severe, nine-out-of-ten pain. It's diffuse... he's got chest pain, pain in an unspecified shoulder, and cervicalgia. He also developed a cough and some diarrhea. He's alert, GCS 14. He's hypertensive and tachycardic, likely from the pain... BP is 157 over 105, heart rate 124, respirations 25. SpO2 is 97 percent. We checked his glucose. We informed the receiving doctor of our ETA. He has a history of hypertension. He's allergic to Codeine. We administered 47.2 milligrams of oral glucose and also gave 295.06 milligrams of Lactated Ringer's Solution via nebulizer for the cough. Primary is encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings, secondary is acute pain. This is an emergent patient under the General-Neonatal Care protocol.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was working in a lab when he splashed an unknown chemical into his eye, followed by onset of severe, diffuse pain and a cough.

transcript: Hey team, we're inbound with an 86-year-old male found by his coworkers at a trade school. Chief complaint is altered mental status. They said he was complaining of weakness and feeling feverish this morning before they found him unresponsive at his station. He responds to painful stimuli, so AVPU is 'P'. His GCS is an 8. Vitals are pretty unstable: blood pressure is 74 over 46, heart rate is tachy at 134, respirations are 17, and he was saturating at 91 percent on room air. We have him on a high-flow non-rebreather. He grimaces with palpation, so we're calling his pain a 3 out of 10. We're thinking this is a possible CVA, but there's also a faint odor of alcohol. We've established an IV in the left AC and have just been evaluating him en route. We're following our General-Eye Injury protocol for the AMS. He has a history of Afib and takes Eliquis, but no known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient complained of weakness and feeling feverish before being found unresponsive by coworkers at his work station.

transcript: Hey there, we have an 85-year-old female coming from a residence. The call was for an unconscious person. Family states she was found on the floor after a fall, but she was alert on our arrival. Her GCS is 15. She's complaining of a headache, some unspecified abdominal pain, and neck pain. She rates her pain a 2 out of 10. Also mentions having diarrhea and a fever for the last day. Vitals are BP 148 over 86, heart rate is 68, respirations 17, and she's 99 percent on room air. We established an IV and converted it to a saline lock. Per family, she has a history of CAD and hypothyroidism. She has a known allergy to iodine. We'll be in your bay in about 3 minutes.

procedures: Conversion of intravenous infusion to saline lock
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the floor by family after a fall; she was briefly unconscious but alert on EMS arrival.

transcript: We're en route with a 12-year-old male, picked up from a street corner after a near-syncopal episode. His friends called it in. He's complaining of chest pain on breathing, which he rates a 3 out of 10. He also states he feels feverish and has had some diarrhea today. His vitals are a blood pressure of 133 over 81, heart rate is tachy at 102, respirations are 15, and SpO2 is good at 97 percent. He's alert but seems confused, so his GCS is 11. Per our cardiac resuscitation protocol for pediatric chest pain, we administered 287.81 milligrams of Aspirin via nebulizer, and also gave 4.11 milligrams of Ondansetron IM for nausea. His mom was on scene, states he has a history of asthma, no known allergies.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a near-syncopal episode, now presenting with pleuritic chest pain, fever, and diarrhea.

transcript: We are inbound with a 47-year-old male involved in a minor traffic incident in a school parking lot. He is complaining of neck pain but denies any other injuries, zero out of ten pain. He seems very anxious and is a bit disoriented. We have him as a GCS 14. Vitals are a BP of 162 over 96, heart rate is 66, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 93 percent. We established an IV for him. He has a history of asthma, so with his low sats, we administered 2.88 milligrams of Albuterol / Ipratropium via an IM injection. No change in his respiratory status. His only known allergy is to codeine. We performed a full evaluation en route.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was involved in a minor traffic incident in a school parking lot, after which he developed neck pain and disorientation.

transcript: We're bringing you a 60-year-old female from a school. She called for heart problems, specifically a racing heart. She also complains of fatigue, some neck pain, and back pain, but denies any chest pain. She's alert and oriented, GCS 15. Vitals are: blood pressure 135 over 83, heart rate is tachycardic at 109, respirations are 17, and SpO2 is 97 percent. She says she has no pain. We did an evaluation and placed her on the monitor. Uh, past medical history is significant for hypertension and coronary artery disease. She has no known drug allergies. We gave her 0.34 milligrams of Nitroglycerin by mouth, and we also administered 2.2 milligrams of Naloxone sublingually. Vitals remained stable post-medication.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called EMS from a school due to a racing heart, fatigue, neck pain, and back pain.

transcript: We are 5 minutes out with a 60-year-old female, chief complaint is assault. We were called to an unspecified residence by PD. On arrival, patient was alert but very distressed. She's complaining of dizziness and giddiness, shortness of breath, and some cervicalgia. She's also showing generalized hyperhidrosis. Her pain is a 9 out of 10. Vitals are a little soft: BP is 98 over 62, heart rate 92, respirations are 20, SpO2 is 99 percent. GCS is 14. We did a full physical assessment and a 12-lead which was unremarkable. We've established an IV line. Given her distress, we followed the airway protocol and administered 2.32 milligrams of Midazolam orally for sedation. Her history includes anxiety and she is allergic to codeine. We'll be pulling in shortly.

transcript: Good morning. We're coming in with a 92-year-old male from a school, it's an adult care facility. They called for breathing problems, but the story changed. Our primary is essential hypertension. He's got a low-grade fever, some diarrhea, and abdominal pain. The big concern is he started having some hematemesis just before we arrived. He's also quite anxious. His GCS is 15 but he only responds to verbal, so AVPU is Verbal. Pain is low, a 2 out of 10. Vitals are BP 147 over 92, heart rate 85, respirations 14, and he's 100 percent on room air. We did a physical assessment and a 12-lead. Per the CHF protocol for his initial complaint, we gave Aspirin, 291.12 milligrams IM. His past history includes A-Fib and hypertension. He's allergic to sulfa. See you in a few.

pcr_json.events_leading: Called for breathing problems, patient was found to have multiple GI symptoms including abdominal pain and vomiting blood.

transcript: We have a 41-year-old female found at her residence with a chief complaint of breathing problems. Her husband called because she was acting confused. We found her GCS to be a 10; she's alert but not oriented. She's also reporting dizziness and some generalized abdominal pain. The husband mentioned she had a brief episode of tachycardia before we arrived. She has a history of hypertension. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 162 over 100, heart rate is 55, respirations are 15, and she's at 100 percent SpO2 on ambient air. She denies any pain, so a 0 out of 10. She has a known allergy to codeine. Based on her presentation we initiated the sepsis protocol. We've just been taking her pulse manually and we administered 353.75 milligrams of Aspirin through her IV.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's husband called EMS after she became confused and experienced breathing problems, dizziness, and abdominal pain at home.

transcript: We're en route with a 10-year-old female from a school, called out for breathing problems. When we got there, she was mostly just complaining of a headache and weakness. Her GCS is an 8. She's alert but not really tracking. She says her headache is a three out of ten pain. Vitals are concerning—BP is high at 145 over 97, heart rate 88, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 100 percent. We did a full evaluation, including a blood glucose measurement which was normal. We also did electrocardiographic monitoring. She has some signs consistent with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. No medications were given. We're coming in for observation, this is an emergent transport. We were following the General-Neonatal Care protocol per dispatch. The school nurse wasn't aware of any past medical history or allergies.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient became weak and complained of a headache and breathing difficulty during class, prompting a call from the school nurse.

transcript: Hey there, we're bringing in a 100-year-old male from the assisted living facility on Elm. Staff called for a sick person. On arrival, he was complaining of generalized abdominal pain and significant weakness. He also has a cough, a sore throat, and had a small nosebleed just before we got there. He's having some difficulty walking. He's alert, GCS 15, and rates his pain a 2 out of 10. His vitals are BP 138 over 76, heart rate is 60, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. We initiated an IV and, following our anaphylaxis protocol, we administered 0.82 milligrams of Epinephrine IV push and also gave 0.38 milligrams of Nitroglycerin PO. His past history includes coronary artery disease and he is allergic to sulfa drugs. No change in his condition during transport.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient at an assisted living facility experienced a sudden onset of generalized abdominal pain, weakness, cough, and epistaxis.

transcript: Good morning. We're coming in with a 73-year-old female from her residence, called for being a sick person. She's had about two days of diarrhea and fatigue, which worsened today with diffuse abdominal pain. Her past medical history is significant for epilepsy. She is alert and oriented, GCS 15. Vitals are: blood pressure 118 over 72, heart rate is tachy at 118, and she's breathing fast at 24 times a minute. SpO2 is 99 percent. She rates her pain a 2 out of 10. We treated her under the anaphylaxis protocol. We started some nebulizer therapy, gave 3.52 milligrams of Ondansetron IM for her nausea, and 1.19 milligrams of Epinephrine sublingually. Our primary thought is generalized abdominal pain, with her epilepsy as a secondary consideration. We'll be there in 5.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced two days of diarrhea and fatigue which acutely worsened with the onset of diffuse abdominal pain.

transcript: Evening. We have a 93-year-old male from his residence with a chief complaint of non-traumatic chest pain. His caregiver states he started grabbing his chest about an hour ago, complaining of severe pain. He's also nauseous and vomited once. Primary impression is just an exam without abnormal findings for now, secondary for the nausea. He is alert but confused, GCS is a 10. He rates his pain a 9 out of 10, says it's in his chest and left shoulder. He also has some neck pain. Vitals are BP 158 over 102, heart rate 105, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 92 percent on room air. We've done a 12-lead and are doing continuous assessment. We have an IV established. We administered Methylprednisolone one hundred twenty-nine point one six milligrams IV, and Midazolam two point zero seven milligrams sublingually for his agitation. His history includes dementia and A-fib, and he's allergic to Codeine.

transcript: Morning, we're inbound with a 90-year-old male, found unconscious on the highway shoulder. Bystanders were unsure how long he was down. He's got generalized weakness and is complaining of a headache and some abdominal pain. He's not really tracking well, GCS is a 6. He's alert though, just very confused. His vitals are a pressure of 157 over 92, heart rate is flying at 153, respirations are 16, and he's saturating at 94 percent. We did a 12-lead which just shows sinus tach. His only known allergy is to Sulfa. We believe his primary issue is profound weakness, with his altered mental status as a secondary. We gave 2.81 milligrams of Albuterol intramuscularly per our sepsis protocol. No other interventions at this time. We'll be there in about five minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found unconscious on the shoulder of a highway by bystanders.

transcript: Hey, we have a 22-year-old female, dispatched as a possible cardiac arrest but she was conscious on our arrival. We're at a university dorm. She has a history of asthma but that's it. No known allergies. Vitals are stable: BP is 119 over 66, heart rate is 90, respiratory rate is 21, and her SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. She's a GCS of 15, completely alert and oriented. We started a line and got a 12-lead which shows sinus tach. We also gave 0.35 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IM, per our protocol for dyspnea, with no change.

transcript: Hey, we have a 77-year-old male coming from an institutional setting. The initial dispatch was for choking. He's now complaining of some generalized weakness and unspecified abdominal pain with some diarrhea earlier today. Our primary is just unspecified illness. He is alert and oriented, GCS 15. transcript: Hey, we're about five out with a 46-year-old female we picked up on the side of a roadway. She called us for a near-fainting episode. She was driving when she suddenly felt her heart racing and became lightheaded, so she pulled over. On our arrival, she had an active nosebleed and felt feverish. Her history is negative except for mild asthma. Vitals are BP 159 over 97, heart rate is 90, respirations 13, and her SpO2 was low at 91 percent. She's alert, GCS 15, pain is a 1 out of 10. We established IV access and put her on the monitor. Following the ROSC protocol, we gave Albuterol, two point two four milligrams, uh, orally, and Nitroglycerin, zero point three seven milligrams, intramuscularly. The primary impression is a feared health complaint with no clear diagnosis.

transcript: We have a 100-year-old female patient, found on a street corner. Chief complaint was nausea and vomiting. She's got a history of epilepsy and COPD. She also complains of some neck pain, difficulty walking, and dysphagia, but denies any pain right now, zero out of ten. She's alert and cooperative, GCS is 15. transcript: Hey team, we've got an 84-year-old male, found down on a highway median. Bystanders called it in. When we arrived he was conscious but had an altered mental status, GCS of 14. His vitals are BP 137 over 89, heart rate 95, respiratory rate 16, and an SpO2 of 94 percent. History is significant for a previous cerebral infarction and A-Fib. He takes Eliquis and a statin. No known allergies. We initiated the Airway protocol given the unknown problem. After moving him to our stretcher he became nauseous, so we administered 3.55 milligrams of Ondansetron IM. transcript: Hey, we're coming in with a 95-year-old female from the nursing home, called for seizure activity. Staff reports she had about a one-minute tonic-clonic episode. She has a history of dementia and a seizure disorder. On our arrival, she was postictal but alert, GCS of 13. She's got a fever and is hyperventilating a bit. Vitals are blood pressure 142 over 82, heart rate is 118, respirations 19, and SpO2 98 percent. She seems to be in discomfort, complaining of some abdominal and shoulder pain, which she rates a 4 out of 10. Prior to our arrival, nursing staff administered 46.01 milligrams of nebulized Fentanyl for agitation. We performed a 12-lead EKG which showed sinus tachycardia. We're treating this as an observation case, but suspect the seizure was provoked by a UTI, given she's bed-confined. We'll be there in two minutes.

transcript: Hey doc, we're bringing in a 47-year-old female, found on the side of the highway following what looked like a minor MVC. Vitals are BP 111 over 73, heart rate is tachy at 126, respirations 20, and her SpO2 was low at 91 percent. She says her pain is a 9 out of 10. transcript: Hey Doc, we have a 100-year-old female for you from the senior center. Chief complaint is just "sick person," but it looks like she had a fall. Staff found her on the floor, she was complaining of significant back pain and there was a small amount of hemorrhage noted on the floor beneath her. She says she tripped over a rug. Vitals are uh, BP 158 over 97, heart rate 85, respirations are 20, and she's saturating 88 percent on room air. We put her on a non-rebreather. She has a history of hypertension and osteoporosis. No known allergies. We're thinking unspecified pain and injury. Our protocol was Airway due to the low sats.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found on the floor at a senior center after tripping over a rug, complaining of back pain with associated hemorrhage.

transcript: Hey doc, we have a 75-year-old female, coming from her home. Chief complaint is severe nausea and vomiting. She says she’s had diarrhea all day and felt like she was going to pass out, which is why she called. She's got a primary impression of an acute abdomen. transcript: We're bringing you a 58-year-old male, involved in a ground-level fall. He was complaining of general weakness and malaise for a couple of days and just lost his footing. transcript: Bringing in a 65-year-old female, victim of an alleged assault at a service area. Police are on scene. Primary impression is unspecified injury. Patient is alert and oriented, GCS of 15. transcript: We're bringing you an 85-year-old male from a single-family residence, called in as a person down. Family found him on the floor. He's alert but confused, GCS is 14. transcript: Hey, we've got a 45-year-old male found down on the street. Chief complaint is… well, he's just generally unwell. Vitals are BP 118 over 66, heart rate 97, respirations are fast at 26, and his SpO2 was only 87 percent on room air. He's a GCS of 15, alert, but confused. No pain, so zero out of ten. transcript: Hey team, we have a 63-year-old male, dispatch was for a medical alert alarm. We found him at his single-family residence, supine on the ground next to his back patio. transcript: We're coming in with a 56-year-old male, found unconscious on the highway. Witnesses stated he had a cough and seemed feverish right before he collapsed. transcript: Okay, so we have a 40-year-old male, found down by a coworker at his auto shop. Chief complaint is basically person down, but our primary impression is syncope. transcript: Medic unit to ED, we have a 53-year-old male, picked up at the local college for a hemorrhage. transcript: We have a 95-year-old male from an institutional residence with a chief complaint of breathing problems. transcript: Hey, we're coming in with a 100-year-old male from his residence. He called for eye problems after he stumbled and fell. He states he was reading when he had a flash of light in his eye, got dizzy, and fell, hitting his neck. He's now complaining of 5 out of 10 substernal chest pain, some neck pain, and a tingling paresthesia in both hands. He's alert, GCS is 15. Vitals are BP 115 over 78, heart rate 93, respirations 17, and he's 93 percent on room air. He's also diaphoretic. We started an IV, placed him on the cardiac monitor, and applied a small bandage to a scrape on his elbow from the fall. His only history is A-Fib and he takes Eliquis for it. No known allergies. We were considering the GI bleed protocol given the fall and his anticoagulant use.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a flash of light in his eye while reading, became dizzy, and fell, hitting his neck. He subsequently developed chest pain and tingling in his hands.

transcript: Hey there, we've got a pediatric patient for you. She's a one-year-old female, from an incident on the highway. Parents called for chest pain after a minor fender-bender. The primary impression is 'person with feared health complaint in whom no diagnosis is made.' The child is alert, irritable but consolable, GCS is a 13. Vitals are a bit weird: blood pressure is 139 over 96, heart rate 103, respirations 22, and SpO2 is 98 percent. She also has a low-grade fever and what seems to be some unspecified abdominal pain. We're scoring her pain a 3 out of 10 on the FLACC scale. We have her on the cardiac monitor, got a 12-lead, and established IV access. Per online medical control, we gave a few medications. She received fifty point zero three milligrams of Fentanyl IM, two hundred eighty-six point one three milligrams of Lactated Ringer's Solution via neb, and zero point three three milligrams of Nitroglycerin PO. No known allergies. We're two minutes out.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Person with feared health complaint in whom no diagnosis is made
pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was involved in a minor fender-bender, and parents called due to concerns about chest pain.

transcript: We have a 16-year-old male here, picked up from a friend's house. Chief complaint is non-traumatic chest pain. It started suddenly about an hour ago. He describes it as sharp, rates it a 7 out of 10. He's alert and oriented, GCS of 15. He's very diaphoretic. His vitals are a little concerning: BP is 138 over 80, heart rate is tachycardic at 118, respirations are 21, and his SpO2 is 100 percent. He has a history of asthma and is allergic to sulfa drugs. We established an IV and moved him to the stretcher. We followed the General-Neonatal Care protocol for some reason... it was a weird call. We gave him 0.95 milligrams of Epinephrine orally and 3.78 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually for his nausea. He denies any recent trauma. His primary impression is an unspecified injury, secondary of weakness.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of sharp chest pain while at a friend's house.

transcript: Alright, we have a 36-year-old male from a trade services building, complaining of weakness. He's alert but confused, GCS is 14. He has been vomiting and also complains of cervicalgia, rating the pain a 2 out of 10. He says he started feeling unwell this morning and it progressed rapidly. His vitals are a little concerning: BP 140 over 87, heart rate is 123, respirations 21, and SpO2 is 95 percent. We're working under the General-Influenza-Like Illness protocol. We started an IV and ran a 12-lead which was just sinus tach. We also performed ventilator care and adjustment on scene. For his meds, we gave 16.68 milligrams of Glucose sublingually and 45.06 milligrams of Fentanyl IV. He has no known allergies but has a history of Type 1 Diabetes. Primary impression is altered mental status.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a rapid onset of weakness, confusion, vomiting, and neck pain while at work.

transcript: Hey charge, we've got a 59-year-old male coming in from a residence. His wife called for a sick person, says he's been weak and getting more confused over the last day. He's complaining of some generalized fatigue, diarrhea, and some non-specific abdominal pain. He also mentioned some chest pain he described as angina-like. On exam, he's alert but not tracking well, GCS is a 10. Initial vitals were a BP of 145 over 85, heart rate 82, respirations 23, and his SpO2 was 92 percent on room air. He's rating his pain at a 4 out of 10. We've established an IV. Given the presentation, we initiated our sepsis protocol. His only past history is hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He has no known allergies. We did give him 58.25 milligrams of Fentanyl sublingually for his pain. See you in two.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's wife called due to progressive weakness and confusion over the last day, with associated fatigue, diarrhea, and abdominal pain.

transcript: We're bringing you a 52-year-old female from her home. Chief complaint is eye problems. She experienced a sudden onset of blurry vision, followed by tingling in her skin and just overwhelming fatigue. She seems a bit confused, her GCS is a 14, but she is alert. She denies any pain. Her vitals are stable: BP is 138 over 83, heart rate is 83, respirations are 20, and O2 sat is 95 percent. We did a 12-lead, no acute changes. Uh, she did have some unrelated back pain, so we gave 51.72 milligrams of Fentanyl orally for that. She also felt nauseous, so we gave her 3.76 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually. Her history includes migraines and anxiety. She's allergic to Ibuprofen. See you in a few.

signs_symptoms: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of blurry vision, followed by skin tingling, fatigue, and confusion.

transcript: We are inbound with a 100-year-old male from a trade area, called for breathing problems. On arrival, we found him barely responsive. Family says he's had a fever and bad cervicalgia for about 24 hours. He is alert to painful stimuli only, GCS is a 6. He is writhing and seems to be in severe pain, so we'll call it a 9 out of 10. Vitals are a heart rate of 109, BP 102 over 58, respirations are 20, and he's saturating 94 percent on high-flow O2. We did a blood glucose measurement, it was 110. For his pain, we gave a 54.66 milligram IV push of Fentanyl. Family is unaware of any past medical history or allergies. He looks septic.

transcript: Hey, we're coming in with a 76-year-old female, found on the street. Bystanders called it in as a seizure. She's postictal and confused, GCS is 7, but she is technically alert and tracking. Our primary impression is other general symptoms and signs. She's complaining of some non-specific abdominal pain and pain in her unspecified shoulder, plus just other fatigue. She denies pain, so zero out of ten. Vitals are BP 159 over 102, heart rate 90, respirations 21, and she's satting 100 percent. We did a basic evaluation procedure and established an IV. We were using the General-Neonatal Care protocol. For her symptoms, we gave 3.76 milligrams of Morphine IV, and we also gave 318.46 milligrams of Aspirin IV. She has a history of epilepsy and she's allergic to codeine. She takes Keppra at home.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a seizure-like event on a public street, witnessed by bystanders.

transcript: We have a 47-year-old male picked up from his house for breathing problems. His wife called after he apparently fell. Our primary is an unspecified injury, with a secondary of other chest pain. He's complaining of neck pain, some abdominal pain, and a sore throat. He also reports feeling very fatigued. His pain scale is zero. He is alert but seems a little confused, GCS is 14. Vitals are blood pressure 140 over 88, heart rate is 99, respirations are 14, and SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. He has a history of hypertension. No known allergies. We did a 12-lead which shows sinus tach, and his blood glucose was normal. No medications were given en route.

transcript: Hey team, we're bringing you an 83-year-old male we picked up from a street corner. The initial call was for 'eye problems', but on our arrival, his primary issues were profound weakness and one episode of hematemesis, which a bystander witnessed. He's alert with a GCS of 15. He's profoundly tachycardic at 131, blood pressure is 167 over 111, respirations 17, and he's saturating 92 percent on room air. He denies any pain. Patient has a history of atrial fibrillation and liver cirrhosis. His only allergy is to iodine. Our treatment has been an IV in the right forearm and continuous assessment. We're about two minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called for 'eye problems' but was found with profound weakness and had an episode of hematemesis just prior to EMS arrival.

transcript:
Hi, we're en route with a 57-year-old female who had a syncopal episode at a commercial establishment. Bystanders said she was shopping, complained of severe nausea and generalized abdominal pain, and then fainted. She also states she's had diarrhea and an acute pharyngitis for about a day.

On our arrival, she was conscious but confused, GCS is a 14. Vitals are stable: blood pressure 103 over 66, heart rate 70, respirations 16, and an SpO2 of 94 percent. She's rating her abdominal pain an 8 out of 10. We have her on the cardiac monitor, got a 12-lead, and started a line. For her pain, we administered 52.98 milligrams of Fentanyl via nebulizer. She has a history of Crohn's disease and is allergic to Codeine. We'll be there in five.

transcript: We're en route with a 93-year-old male from a single-family residence. We were called for a sick person; his son found him on the floor after a possible syncopal episode. He is alert but definitely confused, so we have his GCS at a 13. He's complaining of generalized weakness, a fever, and some unspecified abdominal pain. His blood pressure is extremely high at 198 over 124. Heart rate is 76, respirations 21, and SpO2 is 99 percent on room air. We initiated an IV line and performed a 12-lead, which showed no acute changes. He rated his abdominal pain a 1 out of 10. We gave him 57.81 milligrams of Fentanyl orally. His past medical history includes hypertension and A-fib, and he's allergic to Penicillin. We're bringing him in under the anaphylaxis protocol based on dispatch. See you in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a syncopal episode and was found on the floor by his son.

transcript: This is an 84-year-old male we picked up on the highway. PD called us for a behavioral issue. He was found wandering in traffic. The patient is alert, GCS 15, but very confused about his whereabouts. He's got a history of hypertension and dementia. He denies any allergies. While we were assessing him, he had an episode of hematemesis. He complains of pain in his right shoulder and some unspecified abdominal pain, but rates it only a 1 out of 10. Vitals are: BP 140 over 82, heart rate 111, respirations are 20, and his SpO2 is 88 percent on room air, so we have him on a non-rebreather now. Primary impression is essential hypertension. We're on the chest pain protocol. We administered 272.6 milligrams of Aspirin via nebulizer, and also gave 4.2 milligrams of Ondansetron orally for the vomiting. We've just been doing continuous assessment otherwise.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by police wandering in traffic on the highway. During assessment, he had an episode of hematemesis.

transcript: Hey charge, we're coming in with a 39-year-old female from a school. Initial call was a medical alert alarm. We found her extremely weak. She complains of some other chest pain, non-radiating, and has had diarrhea all day. She also feels like she has a fever. She's alert, GCS is 15. Vitals are a blood pressure of 89 over 59, heart rate of 88, respirations 20, and her SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. We put the pulse oximeter on her and got a 12-lead, no ST changes. Her history includes a seizure disorder and she's allergic to Codeine. When we arrived, she was very agitated, so we administered 2.26 milligrams of Midazolam IM to calm her down. We're working off the General-Eye Injury protocol for... for a generic medical call. She reports no pain now, zero out of ten. ETA is about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient activated a medical alert alarm. Found weak with diarrhea, and was agitated on EMS arrival.

transcript: Alright, we have a 32-year-old female from the local high school. Staff called for chest pain, but on arrival, we found her post-ictal from a witnessed seizure. Her GCS is a 14. She has a known history of epilepsy. She's also complaining of a cough and feels feverish. Vitals are stable: BP 121 over 79, heart rate of 61, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 97 percent. Pain scale is zero. Given the initial confusion, we gave 1.61 milligrams of Naloxone IM and 13.72 milligrams of oral Glucose, with no change. We did a 12-lead, which was unremarkable, and took her temperature. We're treating this as epilepsy, but also considering some generalized abdominal pain she mentioned. We are operating under the ROSC protocol due to the initial unresponsiveness post-seizure.

transcript: Hey team, we've got a 100-year-old male from the VFW hall on 5th. He was found unconscious, but he's alert for us now. GCS is 15. Bystanders say he was drinking and complained of weakness before he passed out. He denies any pain. He's also had some diarrhea and a sore throat, uh, acute pharyngitis, for a couple days. He's tachycardic at 103, BP is 135 over 84, respirations are 22, and SpO2 is 97 percent. Primary impression is weakness, maybe some alcohol intoxication. We checked his blood glucose, it was normal. He has a history of A-Fib and CHF. He's allergic to Sulfa. Per protocol for his agitation on arousal, we gave 0.47 milligrams of Atrovent IV and 2.83 milligrams of Midazolam via nebulizer. We're following the General-Eye Injury protocol for some reason, I think it was mis-clicked.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was drinking alcohol and complained of weakness before becoming unconscious.

transcript: Good morning, we've got an 82-year-old female, victim of a single-vehicle MVC. It looks like she had a syncopal episode and drove into a ditch. We're thinking the primary is a cardiac arrhythmia. She was found at her single-family residence after the crash. On exam, she's alert with a GCS of 14, complaining of some unspecified abdominal pain, rates it a 4 out of 10. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 151 over 90, heart rate is 87, respirations 18, and her SpO2 is 93 percent. We started a line and gave a 247.8 milligram bolus of Lactated Ringer's. We also did a 12-lead which shows some PACs. She has a history of congestive heart failure and is allergic to sulfa drugs. We also gave 2.76 milligrams of Albuterol neb for some slight wheezing we heard. We should be at your doors in about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a syncopal episode while driving, resulting in a single-vehicle motor vehicle collision.

transcript: We're en route with an 11-year-old female from a school. The chief complaint is abdominal pain. The nurse says the patient suddenly felt very weak and complained of a stomach ache, which she rates a 3 out of 10. The patient also had an episode of diarrhea. She is alert. Her vitals are a little strange for her age: blood pressure is 154 over 103, heart rate is 112, respirations 19, and her SpO2 is low at 91 percent on room air. Her GCS is 14, she's just a bit slow to respond. We used the General-Headache protocol. We've performed continuous physical assessment and we've established an IV. No medications were administered. No known allergies or significant past medical history provided by the school.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of abdominal pain, weakness, and diarrhea while at school.

transcript: We're en route with a 65-year-old female from a group home, chief complaint is a psychiatric problem. Staff called for her strange and inexplicable behavior today. She's also complaining of diarrhea, fatigue, and feeling feverish. She is alert, but her GCS is only 11. She rates her abdominal discomfort a 2 out of 10. Her vitals are stable: BP is 145 over 98, heart rate 84, respirations 21, and SpO2 is 100 percent. Past medical history includes schizophrenia and hypertension. We've established an IV and have her on capnography. She was nauseous, so we administered 3.88 milligrams of Ondansetron via nebulizer. Our primary field impression is unspecified pain, likely secondary to a medical issue causing the behavioral changes.

transcript: Hi, we are inbound emergent with a 100-year-old female from a residence. Chief complaint is nausea and vomiting. Per our General-Diabetic Emergency protocol, we administered 357.61 milligrams of Aspirin via nebulizer. We've moved her to our stretcher and informed the receiving doctor of our findings. transcript: Alright, we have an 84-year-old male, picked up from a school auditorium. Staff called for a near-fainting episode. The patient is complaining of profound weakness and feeling dizzy. We've established an IV and performed a 12-lead, which just shows sinus tach. We're following the General-Heat Exhaustion/Stroke protocol. transcript: We're en route with a 66-year-old male from a private commercial establishment. Chief complaint is 'Sick Person' after a syncopal episode. He is now Alert but slightly confused, so his GCS is 14. He's complaining of generalized weakness, some neck pain, or cervicalgia, and some unspecified abdominal pain. He rates his pain a 4 out of 10. His vitals are a BP of 99 over 55, heart rate 94, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 96 percent on room air. We established an IV via catheterization of a vein in his right arm. His wife on scene states he has a history of epilepsy. He has a known allergy to Codeine. Given the initial report of a collapse, we operated under our Cardiac Arrest-Fibrillation protocol. We administered 698.91 milligrams of Acetaminophen orally for his pain. The events leading up to this was he was shopping and suddenly felt weak before collapsing.

transcript: We have a 91-year-old female coming in emergent from a single-family residence. Chief complaint is breathing problems. transcript: Morning. We're coming in with a 100-year-old male from a single-family residence. Call was for a traumatic injury after a fall. transcript: Hey, we're bringing in a 55-year-old female, called for 'sick person' from a department store. She just felt an overwhelming sense of malaise and weakness and had to sit down. transcript: Good morning. We're en route with a 33-year-old male from a private residence. Call came in as a cardiac arrest, but we found him conscious on the floor upon arrival. His girlfriend said he was complaining of a fever, significant fatigue, and some vague abdominal pain before he just collapsed. He's alert now, but a little slow to respond... GCS is 14. transcript: Morning. We've got a 91-year-old male from a single-family residence. The initial call was for abdominal pain, but he says it's a zero out of ten now. transcript: Hey doc, we're bringing you a 48-year-old male, picked up from his house for a chief complaint of just feeling sick. Wife called because he was acting confused. transcript: We have a 79-year-old female we picked up from her residence. The initial call was for a well person check after her daughter couldn't reach her. transcript: Alright, so we've got a 91-year-old male from the VA clinic on the base. The initial call was for a syncopal episode. transcript: Hey doc, we have a 43-year-old female, coming from the high school. Chief complaint is breathing problems. It seems to be anxiety related. She was in her classroom when she started feeling weak and fatigued, had a coughing fit, and said she saw a little bit of hemorrhage, uh, blood, when she coughed. That's when the panic started. Per our protocol for influenza-like illness, we’ve just been providing supportive care. She has a history of anxiety but takes no meds for it. No known allergies.
