transcript:
Hey there, we're bringing you a 29-year-old male from a workshop downtown. Initial call was for a traumatic injury. A piece of equipment fell near him, he wasn't hit but he fell trying to get away. He's now complaining of shortness of breath and some odd neurological symptoms, so we're thinking possible cerebral infarction. Secondary impression is shortness of breath. He has a history of epilepsy and, surprisingly, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. No known allergies.

On assessment, he's alert and oriented, GCS of 15. His vitals are blood pressure 109 over 73, heart rate is 75, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 97%. He has a cough and some tachycardia. He denies any pain, a 0 out of 10. We established an IV. We gave 3.49 milligrams of Ondansetron for nausea. He did have some brief pain after the fall, so we also gave 53.69 milligrams of Fentanyl sublingually, which resolved it. We followed the Airway protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient fell while avoiding a falling piece of equipment at work; subsequently developed shortness of breath and neurological symptoms.

transcript: Hey, we have a 33-year-old male, picked up from his house, a single-family residence. His chief complaint is weakness. He had a fall and is complaining of pain in his unspecified shoulder and some diarrhea. He appears to have an unspecified injury of the hip, which is our primary impression. A secondary impression is acute pain. The patient is Alert, but confused, GCS of 10. Vitals are bizarre: BP is 165 over 112, heart rate is 54, respirations 17, SpO2 97 percent. He's not complaining of any pain right now, so a zero out of 10. His wife says he has a history of atrial fibrillation. He's allergic to NSAIDs. Per the chart, we're under the General-Neonatal Care protocol for some reason. We performed a continuous physical assessment and evaluation. We gave 4.63 milligrams of Norepinephrine IM.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a fall at his residence, resulting in a hip injury, shoulder pain, and subsequent weakness and confusion.

transcript: We're on our way with a 43-year-old female from a residence who called for a fainting episode. She was alert on our arrival, GCS of 15. She reports some diarrhea and has a cough. She also had a brief epistaxis which is now controlled. She denies any pain. Her blood pressure is 144 over 85, heart rate is 107, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. The tachycardia is notable. We've established an IV and have her on the monitor. Given the fainting and associated symptoms, our primary impression is acute abdomen. We haven't given any meds. Her allergies are to codeine and she takes iron supplements for anemia. See you in a few.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a fainting episode at her residence, associated with diarrhea, cough, and a brief nosebleed.

transcript: We're bringing you a 64-year-old male from a single-family residence. His wife called for altered mental status and breathing problems. We found him on the couch, responsive to verbal stimuli only. He's complaining of some other chest pain, rates it a 6 out of 10, and profound fatigue. His vitals are pretty soft—BP is 76 over 44, heart rate is 110, and respirations are 24. Sats are 94 percent on room air. We established IV access and ran a 12-lead. Given his pain, we administered Fentanyl, 50.32 milligrams IM. His mental status has not improved. He has a history of Afib and takes Eliquis. He's allergic to penicillin. ETA is about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's wife called 911 after finding him with altered mental status and difficulty breathing.

transcript: Hey team, we're inbound to your ED with a 58-year-old male involved in a single-vehicle MVC at a school. Chief complaint is the MVC, but our primary concern is acute respiratory distress. He was driving a groundskeeping vehicle and hit a wall. On arrival, he was ambulatory but complaining of weakness and had a significant cough. He also noted some difficulty swallowing and had a minor nosebleed. His initial vitals were a pressure of 90 over 50, heart rate 77, and respirations of 15. His SpO2 was low at 90 percent. He's alert with a GCS of 15, denies any pain. He has a history of hypertension, takes Lisinopril for it, and is allergic to Penicillin. We administered 2.22 milligrams of Midazolam sublingually per the heat stroke protocol due to ambient temperatures on scene. His respiratory status is unchanged. See you in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was driving a groundskeeping vehicle and struck a wall. He was ambulatory on scene but developed progressive weakness and respiratory symptoms.

transcript: Alright, we have a 78-year-old male, picked up on the side of the highway. Chief complaint is abdominal pain. He's Emergent. Patient states the pain is a 5 out of 10. He also complains of a headache, general malaise, and he's got some generalized hyperhidrosis, he's sweating buckets. His GCS is an 11, he's a little confused. Vitals are BP 167 over 99, heart rate 78, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 100 percent. We placed him on the ECG monitor and have been doing continuous assessments. Given his altered mental status, we gave 1.76 milligrams of Naloxone IV. For his pain, we administered 0.43 milligrams of Nitroglycerin via IM injection. He also got 0.41 milligrams of Ipratropium via nebulizer for some mild expiratory wheezing.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the side of a street/highway complaining of abdominal pain and altered mental status.

transcript: We are inbound with a 63-year-old female, emergent, complaining of severe breathing problems. We picked her up on the highway after she pulled over. She's experiencing angina-like chest pain, which she rates a 3 out of 10, along with some nausea. She's alert and oriented, GCS 15. Her blood pressure is elevated at 162 over 109, heart rate is 61, respirations are 22, and SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. We have IV access and have been doing continuous physical assessments. We did a 12-lead, looks like STEMI criteria are met. Per protocol, we gave her 13.96 milligrams of Diltiazem IV. For her nausea and pain, she received 4.32 milligrams of Ondansetron and 56.2 milligrams of Fentanyl, both via nebulizer. Her past history includes hypertension and a prior heart attack. She takes Lisinopril and Aspirin daily.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced acute onset of breathing problems and angina while driving, causing her to pull over on the highway.

transcript: Hey, we're here with a 37-year-old female from the office park on 5th. Chief complaint is nausea and vomiting. She was at her desk and suddenly felt extremely nauseous and fatigued, which is why her coworkers called us. She also endorsed some shortness of breath. Vitals are stable: BP is 134 over 75, heart rate is 78, respirations 19, and she's 95 percent on room air. She is alert, GCS is 15. She says her pain is a 2 out of 10, just a general discomfort. We established an IV in her left AC but haven't given any meds. Her only past medical history is GERD. She has no known allergies. Our primary impression is just a general exam for now, but we're also considering the shortness of breath. We're following our General-Chest Pain protocol.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was at her desk at work when she experienced a sudden onset of extreme nausea and fatigue.

transcript: Hi, we're bringing you an 89-year-old female from a residence. The initial call was for abdominal pain, but the family says her main issue is just profound weakness. She is a bed-bound patient. She's been feeling fatigued with a cough and shortness of breath for a couple of days. She's alert but confused, GCS is 11. Her blood pressure is way up at 188 over 130. Heart rate is 89, respirations are 17, and her SpO2 is 96 percent on room air. She denies any pain, so zero out of ten. Her history includes dementia and hypertension. Given the vitals, our main concern is her bed confinement status complicated by this possible infection. We performed a 12-lead which was unremarkable and have been doing continuous assessments. No medications administered. We're about 10 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's family called due to several days of increasing weakness, fatigue, cough, and shortness of breath for the bed-bound patient.

transcript: Hey, we have a 52-year-old male complaining of breathing problems. We found him at his home. He says he's had diarrhea for the last day, and this morning started having hematemesis, uh, throwing up blood. That's what made him call. He's feeling very weak, which is probably why he felt short of breath. Our primary impression is weakness, likely from dehydration. He's alert, GCS is a 14. Vitals are a bit concerning—blood pressure is 176 over 105, heart rate is 110, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 100 percent. There's no pain right now, a zero out of ten. We put him on the monitor and his EKG is just showing sinus tach. To help with the nausea, we administered 3.26 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually. He has a history of GERD but no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a day of diarrhea followed by an episode of hematemesis, causing weakness and a sensation of breathing difficulty, which led him to call 911.

transcript: Morning. We're bringing you a 15-year-old female found on the street, chief complaint is weakness. Bystanders called it in. She's alert but seems to have some altered mental status, GCS is a 14. She admits to drinking with friends. She's complaining of nausea and vomiting, and says she just feels weak and feverish. She rates her pain a 3 out of 10, but can't specify where. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 110 over 68, heart rate 67, but her respirations are fast at 28. Her SpO2 was only 90 percent on room air. We just placed a catheter in her vein for IV access and put her on the EKG monitor. Our primary impression is acute pain, with a secondary of alcohol intoxication. We're using the General-Diabetic Emergency protocol as a guideline for the altered mental status. No meds were given.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the street by bystanders after drinking with friends, presenting with weakness and altered mental status.

transcript: Good morning. We are inbound with an 83-year-old female from a single-family home. Dispatch was for a traffic incident, but it was a minor fender bender in her own driveway. Her main issue now is weakness, some other chest pain, and shortness of breath. She has a known history of COPD. She is alert, GCS is a 14. Vitals are BP 96 over 53, heart rate 113, respirations are 18, and her SpO2 is 95 percent. We've established an IV, placed her on the cardiac monitor and pulse oximetry. For her chest pain, which she rates a 2 out of 10, we administered 57.69 milligrams of Fentanyl IV. We are treating her under the airway protocol. ETA is about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was involved in a minor motor vehicle collision in her driveway, subsequently developing chest pain and weakness.

transcript: Alright, we've got an 86-year-old female we picked up from the street. Bystander call. Our primary impression is acute pain, though she denies any pain, says she's a zero out of ten. She was nauseous and has a history of COPD. Her vitals are pretty concerning: BP 102 over 64, heart rate is down at 47, respirations 19, and her SpO2 was only 86 percent on room air. She's alert, GCS 15, just seems a little out of it. We initiated continuous physical assessment. For the nausea, per our General-Neonatal Care protocol, we gave 3.83 milligrams of Ondansetron via nebulizer. That's all for her.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by a bystander on the street, appearing confused and nauseous.

transcript: Morning. We have a 69-year-old male from the local community college, chief complaint is chest pain. He's alert, but his GCS is only a 9. He also reports weakness, a cough, and some back pain. He had a small nosebleed on scene. His pressure is real soft, 84 over 47. Heart rate is 83, respirations are 16, and he's saturating at 94 percent. Pain started as an 8 out of 10 but is now a 1. His past medical history includes COPD. We did a 12-lead and a 3-lead, and established a line. We followed the General-Neonatal Care protocol. We administered 0.4 milligrams of Nitroglycerin via nebulizer and gave 48.45 milligrams of Fentanyl orally for his pain. The 12-lead just shows sinus rhythm.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed chest pain, weakness, and back pain while at the community college.

transcript:
Hey, we're coming in with an 88-year-old male from an institutional facility. The chief complaint was unresponsiveness. Staff found him difficult to arouse. He's been febrile and complaining of dysuria for about 24 hours. His GCS on our arrival was 12. He seems to be tracking now, but he's still pretty weak. He rates his overall discomfort at a 2 out of 10. Past history of dementia and BPH is all they could tell us.

Vitals are blood pressure 142 over 83, heart rate 82, respirations 21, and an SpO2 of 92 percent on room air, so we have him on a nasal cannula. We established an IV, put him on the monitor, and got continuous pulse oximetry. Our primary impression is shortness of breath with a secondary of weakness. Based on our General-Diabetic protocol, we administered 4.04 milligrams of Ondansetron IM for nausea and 303.85 milligrams of Aspirin PO, just in case.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found difficult to arouse at his facility, with a recent history of fever and painful urination.

transcript: Hey, we have a 57-year-old female from the local high school. Chief complaint was initially eye problems, but the bigger issue is generalized muscle weakness. She's also got some epistaxis and neck pain, rating the pain a 1 out of 10. She's alert but seems confused, GCS is 11. On scene her vitals were: blood pressure 138 over 82, heart rate 112, respirations 20, and SpO2 was 97 percent via pulse oximetry. Her history is significant for A-fib and she takes Eliquis, which might explain the nosebleed. She is allergic to Penicillin. Given the tachycardia and acute weakness, we followed our chest pain protocol and administered 307.55 milligrams of Aspirin sublingually. She denies any chest pain though.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient, a teacher, experienced a sudden onset of generalized weakness, epistaxis, and neck pain while at work.

transcript: Morning. We have a 100-year-old female from the assisted living facility. The call was for a laceration after a fall. We found her on the floor, she'd been there for about an hour. Staff states she's been complaining of weakness, dizziness, a cough, and some diarrhea for the past day. She's also reporting some neck pain and paresthesia in her fingers. Our primary is weakness with a secondary of dizziness. She has a history of CAD and osteoporosis. No known allergies. She's alert and oriented, GCS 15. She denies any pain, so zero out of ten. Vitals are a bit concerning—BP is 164 over 108, heart rate is 118, respirations 16, and her SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. We controlled the minor bleeding with direct pressure, no other meds or interventions besides placing her on a 3-lead monitor. We're treating her as emergent based on the vitals and the fall.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a fall after a day of progressive weakness, dizziness, cough and diarrhea. She was found on the floor by facility staff.

transcript: Alright, we've got a 43-year-old female, found at her house. Chief complaint is behavioral. Family states she was outside in the heat, acting erratically, and seems dehydrated. She has some general fatigue and complains of neck pain, or cervicalgia. She's alert, but a GCS of 14, a little confused. Vitals are uh, pulse 125, respirations 21, BP 131 over 82, and SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. She has an unknown injury on her arm, rates the pain there an 8 out of 10. We established an IV and put her on a 3-lead, which just shows sinus tach. Per protocol, we gave 2.43 milligrams of Albuterol sublingually. Uh, just a heads up, we did have a brief moment of CPR, but she regained consciousness almost immediately... it was very strange. Her history includes bipolar disorder and asthma. She has an allergy to Penicillin. We're thinking unspecified injury and dehydration.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found outside her house in the heat, acting erratically and seemed dehydrated. She sustained an unknown injury to her arm.

transcript: Hey, we have a 52-year-old female we picked up on the highway. Bystanders called for a psychiatric problem. Uh, she's alert but confused, GCS is 14. Patient is complaining of unspecified abdominal pain, she's nauseous, and she's having some shortness of breath. She also has a slight nosebleed and some aphasia. Pain scale is low, a 1 out of 10. Her vitals are a BP of 137 over 88, heart rate 115, respirations 22, and SpO2 is 100 percent. Her only known history is essential hypertension. We did a full evaluation and got IV access. For the shortness of breath, we administered 2.89 milligrams of Albuterol / Ipratropium via IM injection per our protocol. We're about 5 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found on the highway after bystanders called for a psychiatric problem; patient was confused with multiple physical complaints.

transcript: We're bringing in a 54-year-old male, driver in a low-speed motor vehicle collision in a commercial parking lot. His primary issue seems to be an altered mental status. He is alert, GCS is 15, but he's got some noticeable aphasia. He's also complaining of some dizziness and neck pain, which he rates a 3 out of 10. He also mentioned some dysuria. Vitals are stable: BP is 140 over 91, heart rate 76, respirations 21, and SpO2 is 98 percent. Given the aphasia, we're concerned about a possible medical cause for the MVC. We did a 3-lead EKG, just shows normal sinus. No meds were given. His wife was on scene, states he has a history of a TIA and is allergic to Sulfa drugs.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was the driver in a low-speed motor vehicle collision and is now presenting with aphasia and other neurological symptoms.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 59-year-old male for you, chief complaint is just general weakness. His wife called after she heard a thud and found him on the floor of their living room. He was initially pretty out of it, GCS was a 9 on our arrival, but he's a bit more with it now. He's complaining of some abdominal pain, rates it a 6 out of 10, and he vomited once before we got there. Vitals are stable-ish: blood pressure is 154 over 85, heart rate is 101, respirations are 15, and he's 95 percent on room air. He's alert now but still confused. We just did a basic evaluation and have been monitoring him en route. We followed the general headache protocol given the collapse. He has a history of hypertension and Type 2 diabetes. No known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's wife heard a thud and found him on the floor; he had collapsed while in the living room.

transcript: Alright, we're bringing you a 65-year-old female we picked up on the highway after a fall. She's complaining of a severe headache, neck pain, and back pain, a solid 10 out of 10. She says she felt feverish right before she fell. On our arrival, she had one episode of hematemesis. She's GCS 15, alert and oriented. Her vitals are BP 117 over 78, heart rate is 92 and tachycardic, respirations are 18, and SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. For her pain, we administered 57.51 milligrams of Fentanyl IV. We also gave one dose of Nitroglycerin, that's 0.44 milligrams PO, for some associated chest pressure that has since resolved. We just continued to assess her en route. She has a history of hypertension and GERD, and is allergic to penicillin.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt feverish and then fell while walking on the highway, subsequently developing severe head, neck, and back pain, and vomiting blood.

transcript: We have a 70-year-old female coming from a school library. She called for abdominal pain. On scene, she stated she suddenly developed a bad headache, neck pain, and some generalized abdominal discomfort. She says the pain is now a zero out of ten, but she feels very fatigued. She is alert and oriented, GCS of 15. The main concern is her blood pressure, it's 196 over 134. Heart rate is 93, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 92 percent. We placed her on the cardiac monitor. She has a history of hypertension and GERD. Current meds are amlodipine and omeprazole. She is allergic to penicillin. We're coming in emergent based on that BP. Primary impression is just an encounter for observation.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was at a school library when she experienced the sudden onset of a headache, neck pain, and abdominal pain. The pain has since subsided, but she feels very fatigued.

transcript: Alright, so we've got a 74-year-old male, uh, involved in a single-vehicle MVC on the highway. Bystanders report he was driving erratically, then crashed, and they found him seizing. The seizure lasted about two minutes. On our arrival, he was postictal, GCS of 3, totally unresponsive. He's got some unspecified abdominal pain and nausea that was reported by his wife on the phone. Vitals are, uh, pulse 113, respirations 19, BP 141 over 79, and he's saturating at 96 percent on room air. He has no known allergies. We did a 12-lead, showed sinus tach. Given the presentation, we're going with a primary of other seizures, with secondary acute pain. We're on the General-Sepsis protocol. We initiated an IV and are transporting emergent.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was driving erratically before being involved in a single-vehicle MVC. Bystanders found him actively seizing post-crash.

transcript: We're en route with a 59-year-old female found on the street. Initial complaint was just a sick person. We found her unresponsive to verbal stimuli, though her GCS was 11. She appears very weak, complains of nausea, and has signs of a recent nosebleed. Her pressure is 140 over 82, but her heart rate is tachycardic at 128. Respirations are 20, and SpO2 is 94 percent. Our primary impression is unspecified hypotension causing the tachycardia. We did a full physical assessment. For her nausea, we gave 4.0 milligrams of Ondansetron orally. We also gave 2.8 milligrams of Albuterol via IV, per protocol. She rates her pain a 2 out of 10. She is allergic to Codeine.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found unresponsive on the street by a bystander, complaining of weakness, nausea, and showing signs of a recent nosebleed.

transcript: We have a 100-year-old male, picked up from his residence for a sick person call. He's been vomiting and complaining of a severe headache and some abdominal pain. He rates his pain a 4 out of 10. He's alert with a GCS of 15. We're suspecting a cerebral infarction. He has a history of COPD and an anxiety disorder. His vitals are BP 108 over 72, heart rate 109, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 98 percent. We checked his pulse, got a BGL, and started a line. The initial call was for chest pain so under our General-Neonatal Care protocol we gave him one dose of Nitroglycerin, 0.43 milligrams, sublingually... uh, I mean PO. It didn't change his symptoms. His only allergy is to penicillin.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's caregiver called for vomiting, severe headache, and abdominal pain.

transcript: We're bringing in a 70-year-old female from a residence. The initial call was for an eye injury after a fall. On arrival, we found her extremely lethargic with significant weakness and shortness of breath. Her GCS is 8. Vitals are 126 over 73, heart rate is 84, respirations are shallow at 12, and SpO2 is 93 percent. Based on her presentation, our primary impression is acute pain. We started an IV and put her on capnography. She has a history of COPD but no known drug allergies. Due to her condition, we administered a neb with 5.74 milligrams of Adenosine and 2.59 milligrams of Albuterol IV push. When she became combative, we gave 55.11 milligrams of Ketamine IM for sedation. We're transporting emergent. See you in a few.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient initially called for an eye injury after a fall, but was found extremely lethargic with respiratory distress upon EMS arrival.

transcript: We're bringing in a 36-year-old female from the university library with a traumatic injury. Staff reports she fell down a flight of stairs. She is not responsive to verbal commands, GCS is an 8. They also said she's had a fever and fatigue for the last couple of days. Vitals are blood pressure 148 over 87, heart rate 86, respirations 17. Her SpO2 was 95 percent on room air. We just have pulse oximetry on her right now. She has a known history of asthma and we heard some wheezing, so we administered 2.16 milligrams of Albuterol intravenously. No known allergies. She is not reporting any pain.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient fell down a flight of stairs at the university library. Bystanders report she had been feeling fatigued with a fever for a few days prior.

transcript: Hey, we're coming in with a 59-year-old female, picked up from a single-family residence fire. She was outside on our arrival, complaining of shortness of breath and chest pain. She rates the pain a nine out of ten. She's alert and oriented, GCS is 15. Vitals are BP 138 over 84, heart rate is tachy at 105, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 96 percent. We did a 12-lead which shows sinus tach. She has a history of asthma and high cholesterol. She denies any allergies. We gave her 381.3 milligrams of Aspirin via IM injection and 2.68 milligrams of an Albuterol and Ipratropium combination, which she took orally. The shortness of breath has improved slightly since then. We're bringing her in emergent for evaluation.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed chest pain and shortness of breath after evacuating her single-family residence during a fire.

transcript: Medic 2, en route to your facility. We have an 89-year-old male from the school nursing center, initial call was for eye problems but we found him with an altered mental status. Staff reports an unwitnessed fall. He's complaining of some neck and shoulder pain, but he's too confused to rate it... we're calling it a zero. He is febrile. Vitals are BP 114 over 65, heart rate 91, respirations 22, and he's saturating 91 percent on room air. His GCS is a 12. He was pretty agitated, so for his pain and sedation we gave 51.43 milligrams of Ketamine sublingually. We also administered 11.67 milligrams of Dexamethasone IV and 4.12 milligrams of Ondansetron orally for nausea. Uh, we did perform a 12-lead which was unremarkable. He has a history of COPD. No known allergies. See you in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had an unwitnessed fall at his nursing facility and was subsequently found with an altered mental status, complaining of neck and shoulder pain.

transcript: Alright, we're about five minutes out with a 40-year-old male we picked up on the side of the highway. Chief complaint is just "feeling sick." He was found by a bystander looking pale and confused. On exam, he's complaining of some vague abdominal pain and generalized muscle weakness. He rates his pain a 4 out of 10. He has a history of epilepsy. His initial vitals are a bit concerning: BP is low at 90 over 61, heart rate is 96, respirations are 21, and he's saturating at 95 percent on room air. He's alert, but his GCS is only a 12. We've established a saline lock and are doing continuous assessment. No medications were administered. Given the presentation and tachycardia, our primary impression is angina, possibly sepsis given the low pressure. We're on the General-Sepsis protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found by a bystander on the side of the highway appearing pale and confused.

transcript: We are inbound with a 100-year-old female from the nursing school next door. She was found unresponsive after a syncopal episode. She's alert now, GCS is 15. She had a small amount of hematemesis on scene, but no more since. Her chief complaint is just mild discomfort, a 1 out of 10. Initial vitals are blood pressure 128 over 74, heart rate is tachycardic at 117, respirations are 16, and her SpO2 is 96 percent on room air. Her past medical history includes A-Fib and CHF, and she's allergic to Codeine. On scene, her airway became compromised and we performed an orotracheal intubation, she's been stable since. Per General-Neonatal Care protocol, we also gave a dose of Nitroglycerin, zero point three-nine milligrams, IM. We'll be at your doors in about two minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found unresponsive after a syncopal episode. Airway became compromised requiring intubation.

transcript: We're en route with a 90-year-old female from a residence. Chief complaint is fainting. Caregiver found her slumped in a chair, difficult to arouse. She's been experiencing a cough and fever for two days, and was feeling dizzy before she passed out. She's alert to voice, GCS is a 9. She's complaining of back pain, dorsalgia, rates it a 2 out of 10. Vitals are BP 165 over 104, heart rate 69, respirations are slow at 12, SpO2 is 99 percent. We established IV access and have informed the receiving doctor. Under the chest pain protocol, we gave Aspirin 369.26 milligrams sublingually, and also Morphine 3.99 milligrams via nebulizer. Her past history includes osteoporosis, COPD, and dementia. She has an allergy to Codeine. Our primary impression is unspecified dorsalgia.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Caregiver found patient slumped in her chair after a syncopal episode. Patient reports feeling dizzy beforehand and has had a cough and fever for two days.

transcript: Hey team, we have an 84-year-old male, call was for a traumatic injury at his residence. Family found him on the floor. He's been feeling unwell for a couple days with a cough and diarrhea. He's responsive to verbal stimuli but a little slow to respond, GCS is 15. He's experiencing generalized hyperhidrosis and has a fever. His vitals show a BP of 110 over 70, heart rate 86, respirations 17, and an SpO2 of 92 percent. He denies any pain, so a zero out of ten. History includes CHF and COPD. He's allergic to Codeine. We initiated continuous physical assessment and acquired a 12-lead EKG. Our primary impression is hypotension, secondary is the unspecified injury from the possible fall. We're on the General-Heat Exhaustion/Stroke protocol. No medications administered en route.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the floor by family after feeling unwell for two days with a cough and diarrhea.

transcript: We're on our way with a 49-year-old female from a residence. Family found her with an altered mental status. On arrival, her GCS was a 7. She's hypotensive, BP is 76 over 46. Heart rate is 71, respirations 19, SpO2 95 percent. She's got a bit of a cough and is generally weak. Family states she complained of a severe headache right before becoming unresponsive. Due to the profound hypotension and altered mental status, we treated for potential anaphylaxis. We gave 1.19 milligrams of EPINEPHrine and 286.61 milligrams of Aspirin, both intramuscularly. We did get a 12-lead which was unremarkable. Her mental status has not improved. She is not reporting any pain.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient complained of a severe headache before becoming unresponsive with an altered mental status.

transcript: We're coming in with a 42-year-old female from a school, looks like a post-seizure patient. Staff witnessed a two-minute tonic-clonic event that resolved before we got there. She's now post-ictal. Primary impression is seizure. She is alert but her GCS is 13, mainly due to some aphasia. She's complaining of a fever, a cough, and some unspecified abdominal pain she rates at a 5 out of 10. transcript: Hey, we're inbound to bay 3 with a 52-year-old male. We were called to the community college for an eye problem, but found him with an altered mental status. GCS is 14, he's confused about the date. Staff said he complained of dizziness before becoming altered. He feels warm to the touch and admits to a fever and some unspecified abdominal pain, which he rates a 5 out of 10. His pressure is way up at 171 over 118, heart rate is tachycardic at 132, respirations 18, and sats are 98 percent. We established catheterization of a vein in his right AC and have him on digital respired carbon dioxide monitoring. His past medical history includes hypertension and epilepsy. He is allergic to sulfa drugs. We're following the General-Sepsis protocol. ETA is about 2 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient complained of dizziness and abdominal pain at a college before becoming confused.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 50-year-old female, Jane Doe. We were called to the corner of 5th and Main, on the street, for a report of an unconscious person. Bystanders found her unresponsive. On our arrival, she was unresponsive to painful stimuli. We got a full set of vitals: blood pressure was 112 over 74, heart rate of 90, respirations 20, and an SpO2 of 95 percent on room air. She started to come around as we were assessing her, GCS is now a 14. She's a bit confused. She reports a history of COPD and diabetes, but denies any allergies. We established an 18-gauge in her left AC. Given her history, we followed the General-Diabetic Emergency protocol. Primary impression is really just an encounter for a general medical exam at this point since she's improved so much, with a secondary of other general symptoms. She denies any pain.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found unresponsive on the street by bystanders.

transcript: Hi, we're about 5 minutes out with a 100-year-old female from her home. The call was for chest pain, but on arrival she denies any. Pain scale is zero. She is complaining of some shortness of breath and pain in her unspecified shoulder. She is alert, GCS 15. Her vitals are blood pressure 133 over 81, heart rate 79, respirations 17. Her SpO2 is a little low at 92 percent on room air. We've been doing continuous assessment. Her past medical history includes COPD and she's allergic to Codeine. We're on the General-Neonatal Care protocol for some reason, and based on that we administered 0.44 milligrams of Ipratropium by mouth. Just wanted to give you a heads up on that.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called for chest pain, which resolved prior to EMS arrival.

transcript: We're bringing in a 56-year-old male from his house, chief complaint was breathing problems. His presentation seems more like a mental disorder, possibly anxiety. He's also complaining of generalized muscle weakness and dizziness. He did have one episode of hematemesis before our arrival. He is Alert and oriented, GCS is 15. He rates his pain a 3 out of 10. Vitals are BP 114 over 63, heart rate is tachy at 119, respirations 15, and SpO2 is 97 percent. We did a capillary blood glucose measurement, which was normal, and a general evaluation procedure. No medications were administered. We initiated care under the, uh, General-Neonatal Care protocol. He has a history of anxiety and a gastric ulcer. He has no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called for a subjective feeling of breathing problems, accompanied by weakness, dizziness, and an episode of vomiting blood.

transcript: Good morning. We're bringing you a 74-year-old male from a hardware store, complaining of chest pain. Uh, turns out the pain is more abdominal, he says it's an 8 out of 10 and radiates to his left shoulder. Onset was sudden, about 30 minutes ago. He also developed a nosebleed and has a persistent cough. He's GCS 15, totally with it. His vitals are BP 124 over 85, heart rate 77, respirations 17, and he's 100 percent on room air. We did a 12-lead and put him on a 3-lead for monitoring, shows sinus rhythm. He has a history of atrial fibrillation and GERD. He takes Eliquis and a daily aspirin. He denies any allergies. We initiated sepsis protocol due to the presentation, but no temperature. No interventions needed so far.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed sudden 8/10 abdominal pain radiating to his shoulder while at a hardware store.

transcript: Hey team, we've got a 26-year-old female, driver in a low-speed MVC in the college parking lot. Chief complaint is MVC. Our primary impression is just general weakness for now. On our arrival she was found very diaphoretic, vomiting, and exhibiting some aphasia. We're concerned about a potential head bleed. Her GCS is an 8. Vitals are BP 143 over 90, heart rate 121, but her respirations are slow at 11. Her SpO2 is 92 percent. She appears to be in pain, rates it a 7 out of 10 but can't really verbalize where. She's alert to pain. We initiated patient monitoring, got a blood glucose, and acquired a 12-lead EKG. We didn't give any meds. Her roommate arrived on scene and states the patient has no known drug allergies and has a history of epilepsy.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was the driver in a low-speed motor vehicle collision in a school parking lot and was found with altered mental status.

transcript: Hey Doc, we're inbound with a 38-year-old female from a local school, found postictal after a witnessed seizure. The teacher said she fell and struck her face, which explains the epistaxis. She's alert now, GCS 15, but complaining of severe fatigue and a 10 out of 10 pain. On exam, we noted she was extremely tachycardic, rate of 157, so we put her on a 3-lead. BP was 113 over 70, respirations 18, but her SpO2 was low at 90 percent. We started her on some O2. Her past history is significant for epilepsy. She has no known allergies. Per protocol, we administered 10.12 milligrams of Diltiazem IV for the heart rate and gave her 54.77 milligrams of Fentanyl by mouth for the pain. We'll be there in about five.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a witnessed seizure at school, resulting in a fall where she struck her face.

transcript: Hi, we're en route with a 48-year-old female from a school. The call came in as a cardiac arrest, but on arrival we found her fully alert and oriented, GCS 15. She's complaining of severe upper abdominal pain, an 8 out of 10. She also had an episode of hematemesis just before we got there. Vitals are BP 128 over 72, heart rate 83, respirations 17, and SpO2 is 93 percent on room air. We have her on cardiac monitoring and performed both a 3-lead and a 12-lead. Per the Cardiac-STEMI protocol, we gave her 3.97 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually for the nausea associated with her vomiting. Her history includes GERD and she is allergic to Codeine. We're treating this as a possible GI bleed, but our primary impression is unspecified injury. We'll be there in 5.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of severe upper abdominal pain followed by an episode of hematemesis, which led a bystander to call 911 believing she was in cardiac arrest.

transcript: Okay, so we're coming in with a 92-year-old male from the nursing facility. The chief complaint from staff was just "sick person." They called because he's had a productive cough and has been acting strangely all day, much more confused than his baseline. On our arrival, he was alert but definitely confused, GCS of 11. Vitals are blood pressure 133 over 77, heart rate is 98, respirations 14, and he's 100 percent on room air. He seems to be in significant distress, moaning and guarding, but when we ask his pain he just yells, so we're calling it a 10 out of 10. We established a line, put him on a 3-lead, and acquired a 12-lead which shows sinus tach. He has a history of dementia and CHF. He's allergic to Penicillin. No meds given.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed a productive cough and increased confusion from his baseline, prompting a call from facility staff.

transcript: Hey team, we have a 38-year-old female teacher from the local elementary school. Chief complaint is just "sick person." We found her in the nurse's office feeling acutely short of breath. She also has a headache but denies any pain, a zero out of ten. Her history includes asthma, but she has no known allergies. On arrival, she was responsive to verbal, GCS was a 14. Vitals are BP 147 over 91, heart rate is 98, respirations 21, and her SpO2 was 93 percent on room air. Lungs are a bit tight. We placed her on the cardiac monitor. Per our chest pain protocol, we administered 1.08 milligrams of nebulized epinephrine, which seemed to help slightly. We also gave Fentanyl 48.41 milligrams sublingually. Her breathing is a little easier now.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt acutely short of breath while teaching class.

transcript: Good morning, we're about 10 minutes out with a 16-year-old male from a residence. His chief complaint is just 'sick person'. The main issue is a severe headache he rates as a 9 out of 10. He says it's been going on for 12 hours. He has a history of migraines, but his home meds aren't touching it. Today he's also developed some diarrhea, fatigue, and a minor epistaxis, which is why his mom called. He's alert and oriented, GCS is 15. No known allergies. Vitals are a little weird for his age—blood pressure is 155 over 85, heart rate is 88, respirations 19, and his SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. He's pretty anxious. We did a 12-lead, which just showed sinus rhythm. No medications were given by us. We're thinking this is a complex migraine, so our primary impression is headache. See you soon.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient has had a severe headache for 12 hours unrelieved by home medications, with subsequent onset of diarrhea, fatigue, and epistaxis.

transcript: Okay, so this is a 31-year-old female, we picked her up from her friend's apartment. She called us for severe nausea and vomiting. She's also complaining of dizziness, some diarrhea, and a weird tingling, uh, paresthesia in her hands. Her blood pressure is elevated at 144 over 94, heart rate is 63, respirations 17, SpO2 is 94 percent. She's alert, but seems a little off, GCS is 13. She rates her pain as a 1 out of 10, just generalized discomfort. Her history is significant for hypertension and diabetes with hyperglycemia. We did a 12-lead, no ST changes noted. For the nausea, we followed the hypertensive emergency protocol and administered Ondansetron, uh, 3.76 milligrams sublingually. She reports an allergy to sulfa drugs.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Other specified diabetes mellitus with hyperglycemia
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced the sudden onset of severe nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and paresthesia in her hands, prompting the call to EMS.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 54-year-old male, uh, victim of a penetrating trauma. We were called to a single-family residence for a reported stabbing. Patient states he was in an argument and was stabbed with a kitchen knife. He's complaining of generalized abdominal pain and some referred pain to an unspecified shoulder. He's also got a persistent cough and feels very weak. Vitals are, uh, blood pressure 155 over 100, heart rate is 83, respirations 24, and SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. He is alert but confused, GCS is 11. Pain is a 10 out of 10. We placed him on a 3-lead and checked a BGL which was normal. Per protocol, we administered 41.3 milligrams of Fentanyl via nebulizer for his pain. No known drug allergies. He takes lisinopril for hypertension. That's his only medical history.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was in an argument and was stabbed with a kitchen knife.

transcript: Morning. We're coming in with a 97-year-old male from his residence. Chief complaint is an eye problem, but he seems generally unwell. We're thinking other general symptoms, maybe weakness. His son called because the patient had been vomiting and had diarrhea for about 24 hours. He found him today feeling very dizzy and worried. The house was extremely hot. His history includes COPD and glaucoma. He's allergic to Sulfa drugs. On exam, he responds to verbal stimuli, GCS is a five. Vitals are BP 166 over 100, heart rate 68, respirations 20, SpO2 is 92 percent on room air. He's also febrile. He denies any pain. We initiated an evaluation and, uh, per the General-Heat Exhaustion/Stroke protocol, we administered Albuterol, 2.61 milligrams via IM injection for his respiratory history. We'll be in your ER in about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's son called after finding him dizzy and weak; patient had been experiencing vomiting and diarrhea for 24 hours in a hot house.

transcript: Okay, we're coming in with a 100-year-old female from a single-family residence. Dispatch originally sent us for a possible cardiac arrest, but we found her conscious. Her primary issue is severe back pain, which she rates a 4 out of 10, along with some nausea. She's alert but seems to have difficulty walking on her own. Her initial vitals were a blood pressure of 203 over 124, heart rate of 85, respirations 18, and she's 100 percent on room air. She has a history of dementia and hypertension, no known allergies. Partway through transport she developed some hives on her chest, so per our anaphylaxis protocol we administered 1.12 milligrams of epinephrine IM. We have her on continuous monitoring. We'll be there in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced sudden onset of severe back pain and nausea. Developed hives on chest during transport.

transcript: Good morning, we are en route with a 100-year-old male found on the highway. Dispatch was for a seizure. Bystanders saw him have a brief tonic-clonic seizure on the sidewalk. He was postictal when we got there but is now alert, GCS 15. He's complaining of some fatigue, pain in an unspecified shoulder, and some paresthesia of the skin. He is also febrile. Vitals are blood pressure 130 over 80, heart rate of 110, respirations 21, and SpO2 was 91 percent. We've initiated oxygen administration by nasal cannula. We have him on the cardiac monitor and acquired a 12-lead. We've given him a few medications: 0.34 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IV, 4.36 milligrams of Ondansetron IM, and 279.5 milligrams of Lactated Ringer's Solution, also IM. His history includes a seizure disorder and coronary artery disease. He is allergic to codeine. We'll be there in less than five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Bystanders witnessed the patient have a brief tonic-clonic seizure on the sidewalk. He was postictal on arrival and returned to a GCS of 15.

transcript: Alright, we have an 85-year-old male, picked up from his single-family residence. The chief complaint is an allergic reaction. Family states he was stung by a wasp about an hour ago while in his garden. Our primary impression is acute respiratory distress syndrome. He's confused, GCS is a 14, but he's unresponsive on the AVPU scale. On scene, he was vomiting and had some diarrhea, also complaining of major fatigue. Vitals are blood pressure 144 over 90, heart rate of 89, respirations 18, and an SpO2 of 97 percent on room air. He's a zero out of ten for pain. We started a line, ran a 12-lead electrocardiogram, and did continuous electrocardiographic monitoring. Per our General-Eye Injury protocol, we administered 4.44 milligrams of Ondansetron via neb for the vomiting, 280.22 milligrams of Aspirin IV, and 53.59 milligrams of Fentanyl IM. His past history includes atrial fibrillation and he takes Eliquis. He has no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was stung by a wasp while in his garden approximately one hour prior to EMS arrival.

transcript: We have a 69-year-old male, called for a syncopal episode at a trade area. Bystanders said he just passed out. He's alert now but confused, GCS is a 13. He says he had some generalized abdominal pain and diarrhea before it happened. He denies any pain now, so pain scale is zero. His vitals are a little concerning: blood pressure is 145 over 92, and he's tachycardic at 126. Respirations are 14, SpO2 is 97 percent. We've established an IV and have him on a 3-lead monitor. He has a history of coronary artery disease and atrial fibrillation. He takes Eliquis and Metoprolol. No known drug allergies. We're suspecting chest pain as the underlying cause despite his denial. We'll be there in two minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a syncopal episode while in a public place, preceded by abdominal pain and diarrhea.

transcript: We are en route with a 39-year-old male from the university. Dispatch was for an eye injury, but on scene the patient was just exhibiting strange and inexplicable behavior. He's alert but has a GCS of 9. He's complaining of weakness, some unspecified abdominal pain, malaise, and chest pain on breathing. He also mentioned having diarrhea earlier. No pain, a zero out of ten. His vitals are pretty concerning: BP is 185 over 120, heart rate is 126, respiratory rate is 16, and SpO2 is 93 percent on room air. We initiated blood pressure taking in a series and placed him on a 3-lead. He took 14.65 milligrams of Ketorolac orally before we got there for some other pain. His history includes hypertension and anxiety, and he's allergic to codeine. We're considering this an altered mental status under the drowning protocol as a catch-all.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient initially called for an eye injury. On arrival, he was found with altered mental status and bizarre behavior, with multiple other systemic complaints.

transcript: We're coming in emergent with a 68-year-old female, we believe this is anaphylactic shock. We were called to the university for a female with chest pain. On arrival, we found her profoundly weak and fatigued, GCS of 7. She has a known peanut allergy and was exposed at a luncheon. Her chief complaint is 10 out of 10 chest pain, and she's developing some respiratory distress. Vitals are BP 145 over 88, heart rate is tachy at 123, respirations 16, and SpO2 94 percent. Due to her initial unsteadiness when she stood up, we placed her in cervical spine immobilization as a precaution. We've completed our initial evaluation and moved her to our stretcher. We administered Diphenhydramine, 41.82 milligrams, via IM injection. Given the presentation, we're following the fibrillation protocol. See you in a few.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient with known peanut allergy was exposed at a luncheon, leading to weakness, chest pain, and respiratory distress.

transcript: We have a 48-year-old female with a chief complaint of breathing problems. She was at her office when she had what she describes as a panic attack with sudden shortness of breath. She initially mentioned some fleeting general discomfort, which has since resolved. Her vitals are stable: blood pressure 121 over 81, heart rate is 84, respirations are 13, and SpO2 is 99 percent on room air. GCS is 15, and she denies any pain, rating it a zero. We performed a physical assessment and put her on a 3-lead, which just shows sinus rhythm. She was feeling nauseous, so we administered 4.8 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually. Due to her initial symptoms, we also gave 0.46 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IV. Her only past medical history is generalized anxiety disorder. No known drug allergies.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Other symptoms and signs involving emotional state
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden panic attack with shortness of breath while at her office.

transcript: Hey there, we have a 90-year-old male for you. This was a mutual aid call to a skilled nursing facility. Staff called for general weakness and malaise. The patient is alert, GCS 15. He also complains of other fatigue, some unspecified angina, and dysuria. He rates his pain just a 1 out of 10. Vitals are blood pressure 148 over 87, heart rate is tachy at 120, and respirations are 26. SpO2 is 96 percent. He has an extensive cardiac history, including A-Fib. Per staff, he took his scheduled dose of Amiodarone, which was 173.12 milligrams, orally, just before our arrival. We initiated care under the General-Neonatal Care protocol, placed him on a 3-lead monitor, and packaged him for transfer of care. We'll be at your doors in about 2 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Nursing facility staff requested mutual aid for a patient experiencing general weakness, malaise, fatigue, angina, and dysuria.

transcript: Alright, we have a 71-year-old male we picked up on the side of the highway for non-traumatic chest pain. PD found him confused and exhibiting some strange and inexplicable behavior. He's complaining of abdominal pain and some dorsalgia, rates it a 4 out of 10. He also just seems really fatigued. His GCS is 12, he's alert but not oriented. Vitals are BP 169 over 93, heart rate 117, respirations are slow at 12, and SpO2 is 97 percent. We got a 12-lead and established venous access. His history includes diabetes and he takes insulin, but he couldn't remember anything else. No known allergies. His blood sugar was low, so we gave 26.54 milligrams of Glucose 500 MG/ML IM. For his pain, we also gave 44.99 milligrams of Fentanyl IM. We're operating under the Airway-Obstruction protocol due to his depressed respiratory drive. Primary impression is pain in limb, unspecified.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by police on the side of a highway, confused and exhibiting strange behavior, with complaints of diffuse abdominal and back pain.

transcript: Alright, we have a 34-year-old female we picked up from her office. Chief complaint is just sick person, but we're thinking altered mental status. Staff found her confused at her desk. She complained of a bad headache, then had a brief nosebleed. She also mentioned some coughing and dysuria earlier today. On exam, she's alert but not oriented, GCS is a 12. Vitals are BP 156 over 108, heart rate 123, respirations 15, and she's 96 percent on room air. She has a history of migraines but says her pain is a zero now. No known allergies. Based on her headache protocol, we initiated some ventilator care. She was given Fentanyl, 58.43 milligrams via nebulizer. We've just been doing continuous assessments en route. Her primary impression is altered mental status, but with a possible unspecified injury from maybe hitting her head at her desk.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's colleagues found her confused at her desk after she complained of a headache and had a nosebleed.

transcript: Good morning. We are bringing you a 52-year-old male from a private commercial establishment. Initial call was for heart problems. On arrival, the patient was alert, complaining of some unspecified angina and cervicalgia. He denies any other pain, so pain scale is a zero. His GCS is a 13. Vitals are BP 143 over 87, heart rate is bradycardic at 58, and respirations 21. His SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. We placed him on a 3-lead monitor, which just shows sinus bradycardia. His past medical history is significant for a TIA and hyperlipidemia. His only current medication is Atorvastatin. He is allergic to Codeine. We did not administer any medications. Our primary impression is a possible cerebral infarction. We'll be there in 10.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of angina and cervicalgia while at a commercial establishment.

transcript: Hey Doc, we have a 44-year-old male we picked up from his house. Initial call was for chest pain, but when we got there he was pretty altered. He's a GCS of 14, alert but confused. He complains of some trouble swallowing and seems extremely anxious. His vitals are a bit soft, BP is 105 over 59, heart rate 91, respirations are up at 23, and he's 97 percent on room air. We ran a 12-lead as part of the Cardiac-STEMI protocol, which was negative. He denies any pain now, a zero out of ten, but does say his back hurts. Given his anxiety, we gave 2.74 milligrams of Midazolam sublingually. We also gave 9.28 milligrams of Dexamethasone SL for potential airway issues with the dysphagia, and 366.11 milligrams of Aspirin IV. Primary impression is altered mental status. Family found him on the couch not making any sense. He has no known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the couch by family, not making any sense.

transcript: Hey, we're coming in with a 65-year-old female, picked up from a department store. The call was for a sick person. We found her with diffuse, unspecified abdominal pain and significant fatigue. She rates the pain a 3 out of 10. She also feels pretty nauseous. She's alert, GCS of 15. Her vitals are a bit soft, BP is 87 over 49, heart rate is 67, respirations are 17, and her SpO2 is 99 percent on room air. She has a known history of essential hypertension and GERD, and allergies to sulfa drugs. Given the hypotension and other signs, we initiated our General-Sepsis protocol. We've established a line, have her on the cardiac monitor and pulse oximetry, and moved her to the stretcher. She also mentioned feeling a little wheezy, so we gave her 2.62 milligrams of Albuterol orally, per her recent prescription. Our primary impression is acute abdomen.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, and significant fatigue while at a department store.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 45-year-old male we picked up on the side of the highway. Call was for a sick person. He appears to be having a severe allergic reaction. He states he has a history of diabetes and hypertension. He's on Metformin and Lisinopril for that. He is allergic to peanuts. He was eating a snack bar and didn't realize it had peanuts in it. He's complaining of shortness of breath and some fatigue. He's pretty altered, GCS is an 8, though he is alert. His vitals are BP 148 over 99, heart rate 101, respirations are 16, and his SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. He's rating his pain a 5 out of 10, but he's a bit vague on the location due to his mental status. Uh, we did a 12-lead which just shows sinus tach. Based on his presentation, we gave Diphenhydramine, 58.27 milligrams sublingual, and EPINEPHrine, 0.99 milligrams, also sublingual. We were initially running the diabetic protocol because of his history, but it seems more allergic in nature.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient consumed a snack bar containing peanuts, triggering an allergic reaction while on the side of the highway.

transcript: Alright, so we've got an 83-year-old male for you. We were dispatched for a fire, but it turned out to be a minor kitchen incident. Family found him on the floor, very weak. Primary impression is weakness, possibly septic. He's been febrile and complaining of some right shoulder pain and an earache for a couple of days. On exam, he's got altered mental status, GCS is a 12. He's unresponsive to voice but moans to pain. Initial vitals were blood pressure 95 over 54, heart rate 118, respirations 19, and an SpO2 of 93 percent on room air. He rates his pain a 3 out of 10. We established an IV. Per our sepsis protocol, we administered two point three four milligrams of Albuterol and five hundred ninety-seven point one two milligrams of Acetaminophen, both via nebulizer. We also gave one point one milligrams of Epinephrine PO, per family who said it was one of his regular meds. He has a history of COPD and hypertension. No known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had been feeling unwell for a couple of days; family found him on the floor after a minor kitchen fire incident.

transcript: Hey doc, we're coming in with a 58-year-old female from her residence, dispatched as a behavioral issue. Family states she's had a cough and fever for two days, and today became extremely weak and confused, which is why they got worried. She's alert but not really with it, GCS is an 8. She's just staring and seems very worried. No pain, she rates it a zero. Vitals are a bit concerning—BP is 143 over 87, heart rate's tachy at 113, respirations are 25, and she's saturating 95 percent on room air. We put the pulse ox on and ran a 12-lead, which just shows sinus tach. She has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril. No known allergies. We haven't given any meds. Our primary concern is altered mental status, possibly secondary to an infection. We should be there in about five.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Person with feared health complaint in whom no diagnosis is made
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's family called due to two days of cough and fever, followed by onset of extreme weakness and confusion.

transcript: Hey, we're coming in emergent with a 26-year-old female from a school dorm. Chief complaint is abdominal pain. She was found by her RA. Patient has had a fever and diarrhea for about a day. She's also aphasic. Her mental status is poor, GCS is a 5. Vitals are blood pressure 116 over 63, heart rate is down to 55, respirations are 20, and she's 100 percent on oxygen. We started an IV and have her on two liters via nasal cannula. Due to her condition, we've pushed a few meds under the Airway protocol. We gave Epinephrine 1 MG/ML, 1.06 milligrams IM. Then Aspirin 325.78 milligrams IV. And also EPINEPHrine 0.1 MG/ML, 1.01 milligrams via nebulizer. Her pain is a 4 out of 10. Primary impression is weakness with a secondary of generalized muscle weakness. We'll be at your doors in 2 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found in her school dorm room by an RA after a day-long history of fever, diarrhea, and abdominal pain, with a significantly decreased level of consciousness.

transcript: Alright, so we have a 61-year-old female, found unconscious at the community college where she teaches. Initial complaint was near-fainting. Uh, she came to right as we arrived, GCS is about a 13 now. She's complaining of severe fatigue, a bad cough, and says she vomited what looked like coffee grounds just before we got here. That tracks with some hemorrhage. Her vitals are a little soft, BP's 91 over 61, heart rate is 103, respirations are 22, and she's only saturating 91 percent on room air. We're assisting ventilations currently. She's pointing to her chest, says pain is a 9 out of 10. We're thinking this is cardiac, so we're on the STEMI protocol. We gave 260.24 milligrams of Aspirin orally. She has a history of essential hypertension but no known allergies. We've just been doing continuous assessments en route.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a near-fainting episode at her workplace, a community college. She became briefly unconscious before EMS arrival. Prior to the event, she was experiencing severe fatigue, a cough, and had an episode of coffee-ground emesis.

transcript: Good morning. We have a 100-year-old male we picked up from a local school. He was there for an event and developed some chest pain. He describes it as a 4 out of 10 pain, along with some associated cervicalgia, or neck pain. He is alert and oriented, GCS is 15. Initial vitals are blood pressure 109 over 61, heart rate 79, respirations 16, and SpO2 96 percent on room air. He has a history of atrial fibrillation and hypertension. He's allergic to codeine. We performed a 12-lead which confirmed his baseline Afib. We moved him to our stretcher and administered 362.98 milligrams of Aspirin sublingually. His pain is unchanged. We'll see you in five.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed chest pain and neck pain while attending an event at a school.

transcript: Hey doc, we've got a 75-year-old male coming into bay three. We were called for a well person check on the highway. He was driving and felt an acute onset of dizziness and fatigue, so he pulled over. He rates his pain at an 8 out of 10, just generalized. On our arrival, he was alert, but his GCS was a 4. Vitals are BP 153 over 106, heart rate of 92, respirations at 23, and he's saturating 100 percent on room air. We did a blood glucose check, which was normal.
