transcript: Okay, we're coming in to Trauma 2 with a 98-year-old male from the service area, dispatched as an assault. On arrival, we found the patient unresponsive. Staff states there was an altercation, but details are unclear. He's got a GCS of 11, just moaning and withdrawing to pain. His vitals are: blood pressure 129 over 80, heart rate is tachy at 116, respirations 22, and he's saturating at 99 percent on room air. He has some active epistaxis from the right nare and feels febrile to the touch. He also seems to guard his neck, so we're concerned about some cervicalgia. Pain is hard to assess, but we'll call it a 1 out of 10. We placed him on a 3-lead monitor per our Airway protocol. We couldn't get any history from him, so allergies and current meds are unknown.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found unresponsive by crew after an alleged altercation with another person at the location. Details of the event are unclear.

transcript: We have a 41-year-old female, victim of a traffic incident in the parking lot of a commercial establishment. She was the restrained driver. Per bystanders, she was initially unresponsive. On our arrival, she was a GCS of 14, confused but able to talk. She complains of generalized abdominal pain, some neck pain or cervicalgia, and chest pain on breathing. Her vitals are blood pressure 155 over 102, heart rate is 65, respirations 17, and SpO2 is 100 percent. She's not alert enough to rate her pain, so we're calling it a zero. She has a history of COPD and reports some fatigue. We did a 12-lead and continuous pulse oximetry. Per our General-Neonatal Care protocol for chest pain, we administered 290.88 milligrams of Aspirin via an IM injection. No known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was the restrained driver in a motor vehicle collision in a parking lot and was initially found unresponsive by bystanders.

transcript: We have a 54-year-old male, picked up at his residence for a fainting episode. His wife states she heard a thud and found him post-ictal after a likely seizure. On our arrival, he was alert but confused, now he's a GCS 15. He fell during the event and has a small head lac, which is the source of the hemorrhage we noted. He's also complaining of significant neck pain, a 7 out of 10. His vitals are stable: BP 142 over 98, heart rate 90, respirations 15, SpO2 is 96 percent. He has a history of epilepsy and asthma. On auscultation, we noted some wheezing, so we gave 2.45 milligrams of Albuterol IV. We also started a line. For his neck pain, we gave 0.59 milligrams of Hydromorphone IM. He states he has no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a witnessed seizure, fell, and sustained a head laceration and neck injury. He was post-ictal on arrival.

transcript: Hey doc, we have a 52-year-old female teacher from the local high school. Chief complaint is bizarre eye problems, she described it as "everything looking wavy." Staff called because she became acutely confused and then vomited. On arrival, she was alert but definitely not with it, GCS was a 12. She was also complaining of some chest pressure, back pain, and a fever. Initial vitals were blood pressure 121 over 68, heart rate 74, respirations 14, and SpO2 at 96 percent on room air. She denies any pain, a zero out of ten. We did a finger stick, BGL was normal. She has a history of hypertension and Type 2 diabetes, but says she's allergic to sulfa drugs. We didn't administer any medications. She also mentioned some dysuria for the past couple days. It's a very strange presentation. We established a saline lock just in case.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient, a teacher, experienced a sudden onset of visual disturbances, described as 'wavy,' followed by acute confusion and vomiting at work.

transcript: Alright, we've got a 100-year-old male from the institutional residence. The initial call was for a psychiatric problem. On arrival, we found him with a GCS of 8. Staff state he was complaining of chest pain and shortness of breath just before he became less responsive. His vitals are a blood pressure of 149 over 89, heart rate is 63, respirations are 20, and his SpO2 is 100 percent on room air. He's not responding to questions so pain scale is zero. Per facility nursing, they administered two nebulized medications: 1.12 milligrams of Epinephrine and 0.45 milligrams of Nitroglycerin. We initiated a 3-lead and also acquired a 12-lead, which I'll hand you. His past history includes dementia and coronary artery disease. He is allergic to Codeine.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient complained of chest pain and shortness of breath before becoming minimally responsive.

transcript: We have a 47-year-old male, a teacher from the high school. Staff called for eye problems but we found him profoundly weak and in respiratory distress. He's only responding to verbal commands, pretty confused, so we're calling his GCS a 9. He's tachycardic at 128, respirations are 21, BP is 111 over 69, and his SpO2 was 92 percent on room air. He seems to have some neck pain, rates it a 4 out of 10. He's also febrile. He has a history of asthma. According to his medical bracelet, he's allergic to penicillin. We got a 12-lead which just shows sinus tach. Per protocol, we administered 2.8 milligrams of Albuterol sublingually for his wheezing. His breathing seems a little easier now, but he's still very weak.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found profoundly weak, confused, and in respiratory distress at the high school where he works.

transcript: Alright, we're inbound with a 27-year-old male from a vehicle fire on the highway. He was self-extricated prior to our arrival. He's alert, GCS 15. Chief complaints are nausea and significant weakness. He also has some shortness of breath and feels feverish. He says he has a history of COPD. Lungs are a little tight on auscultation. Vitals are blood pressure 171 over 95, heart rate is tachy at 148, respirations 17, and SpO2 is 92 percent. He rates his overall pain at an 8 out of 10. We did a 12-lead which just showed sinus tach, and we also did a 3-lead for monitoring. Blood glucose was normal. Per our Airway protocol, we gave him 2.47 milligrams of Albuterol via IM injection and for his nausea we gave 4.24 milligrams of Ondansetron, that was PO. He says he's allergic to codeine.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient self-extricated from a vehicle fire on the highway and subsequently developed nausea and weakness.

transcript: Morning. We have an 84-year-old male, a volunteer from the school library. Called for a sick person. He's alert and oriented, GCS 15. Complains of generalized weakness, fatigue, and what he calls 'acute pain' all over, a 5 out of 10. Specifically mentioned some abdominal pain, a sore throat, and cervicalgia. Vitals are stable: BP 127 over 82, heart rate 77, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 93 percent on ambient air. We did a 12-lead, shows sinus rhythm. His only allergy is to Codeine. Past history of hypertension and GERD. For his pain, we administered 58.19 milligrams of Fentanyl sublingually. He also has a non-productive cough, so we gave 2.78 milligrams of Albuterol orally. Primary impression is weakness. We used the General-Neonatal Care protocol for transport.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient, a school volunteer, experienced a sudden onset of generalized weakness and non-specific pain while in the library.

transcript: Hey, we have a 51-year-old male, dispatch was for a medical alert alarm. We found him at his single-family residence. Uh, the patient's wife says he felt weak and then fell to the floor. Our primary impression is altered mental status. He's alert but confused, GCS is a 14. He is complaining of some neck pain, about a 3 out of 10, so we applied a cervical collar. Vitals are BP 140 over 91, heart rate is 76, respirations are 22, and his SpO2 is 92 percent. He has a notable cough. We started an IV and got a 12-lead. Per the General-Diabetic Emergency protocol, we administered 1.13 milligrams of EPINEPHrine through the IV. His wife states he has a history of Type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He takes metformin for his diabetes. He has no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt weak and fell to the floor, prompting a medical alert alarm activation by his wife.

transcript: Hey team, we've got a 48-year-old male, picked up from the side of the highway. The initial dispatch was for abdominal pain, but on our arrival, he went into V-fib cardiac arrest. It was witnessed. We started CPR immediately, placed him on the cardiac monitor, and defibrillated him one time. We achieved ROSC about five minutes ago. His GCS is 15, he's alert and talking. His blood pressure is very high at 196 over 136, heart rate is 99, respirations 17, and SpO2 is 95 percent. He's now complaining of severe, generalized abdominal pain, a nine out of ten. He also states he feels feverish and fatigued. We've been doing continuous assessment en route. We're about 3 minutes out. No known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a witnessed V-fib cardiac arrest on the highway after initially complaining of abdominal pain.

transcript:
Hey doc, we're bringing you a 49-year-old female from a local middle school, complaining of near-syncope. She's a teacher who said she felt weak all morning with a really sore throat. While teaching, she developed a sudden, sharp back pain, an 8 out of 10, and then felt like she was going to pass out. Her colleagues helped her to the floor before we arrived.
Initial vitals are blood pressure 105 over 69, heart rate is tachy at 104, respirations 13, and she's saturating 94 percent on room air. She's alert, but a little slow to respond, GCS is 14. We've got her on the cardiac monitor and did a 12-lead, which just shows sinus tach. We're doing continuous assessments en route. She has no known drug allergies. We're about five minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient, a teacher, felt weak with a sore throat all morning. Developed sudden, sharp 8/10 back pain and felt like she was going to pass out while in her classroom.

transcript: Alright, we have a 100-year-old male from the reform school, found unconscious by staff. He's alert for us now, GCS is 15. His primary issue seems to be dehydration secondary to a GI bug. Staff says he’s had diarrhea and nausea for a couple of days, hasn't been eating or drinking well, and felt like he had a fever. He's also complaining of some mild, unspecified abdominal pain, rates it a 2 out of 10. No known drug allergies. He has a history of Afib and takes Eliquis. Vitals are pretty soft: BP is 88 over 49, heart rate of 82, respirations 15, and an SpO2 of 95 percent. We've just been doing continuous physical assessment and have him on a 3-lead, no major changes noted. We're treating under the general headache protocol. No meds were given.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient has had diarrhea, nausea, and fever for two days with poor oral intake. Staff found him unconscious in his room.

transcript: We have an 81-year-old female found wandering on the highway. She is alert with a GCS of 15. She complains of feeling feverish and has some paresthesia in her hands, which she rates as a 2 out of 10 pain. Vitals are blood pressure 125 over 80, heart rate 92, respirations 21, and an SpO2 of 94 percent. Due to her confusion and presentation, we followed our headache protocol. We administered 1.68 milligrams of Naloxone IV with no change in her mental status. We also gave 1.05 milligrams of sublingual EPINEPHrine. After assessing her vital signs, we moved her to our stretcher for transport. She has a history of dementia and is allergic to codeine. We'll be there in about 10 minutes.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found wandering on a highway, experiencing confusion, feverishness, and paresthesia in her hands.

transcript: Yeah, hi, we're coming in with an 86-year-old male from a trade services area. He called us for just feeling sick. He presents with a fever, shortness of breath, and a cough. He also states he had a small amount of hematemesis this morning. He's alert but a bit confused, GCS is a 14. Vitals are BP 125 over 75, heart rate is tachy at 116, respirations 19, and his SpO2 is 93 percent on ambient air. He denies any pain. He has a history of COPD. We did a physical assessment, acquired a 12-lead EKG, and have initiated IV fluids. See you in about five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt progressively sick while at a trade/service area, experiencing fever, SOB, cough, and hematemesis, prompting him to call for an ambulance.

transcript: Alright, we're inbound to your ED, about 10 minutes away. We have a 39-year-old male we picked up on the highway. Bystanders called for a man acting erratically. Our primary impression is generalized abdominal pain, but he's also very short of breath. He's got some aphasia, making history difficult. He's alert but very confused. GCS is a 9. He has a cough and some diarrhea noted on scene. Vitals are concerning: BP is 87 over 56, heart rate 81, respirations are 20, and his SpO2 is only 87 percent on room air. He's not complaining of any pain, so zero out of ten. We couldn't get any history on him. Given the potential for an allergic reaction, we administered 2.06 milligrams of Magnesium Sulfate orally per our protocol for bronchospasm, which is under the General-Chest Pain protocol. We're managing his airway and will see you shortly.

pcr_json.events_leading: Bystanders called for a man acting erratically on the highway.

transcript: We're en route to you with an 80-year-old male. The call came in for an eye problem, but we found him with generalized abdominal pain and in acute respiratory distress. He is unresponsive. No eye opening, no verbal response, and he's extending to painful stimuli, so his GCS is a 4. Family states he was complaining of abdominal pain at a 4 out of 10, shortness of breath, and some nausea with vomiting before he became unresponsive. He also has a cough and had a small nosebleed. Vitals are BP 144 over 93, heart rate 109, and respiratory rate of 21. His SpO2 is 98 percent. Given his history of diabetes, we initiated the diabetic emergency protocol. We've established venous catheterization in his right arm and have him on the cardiac monitor. He's allergic to Codeine. No other meds given.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient became unresponsive after complaining of abdominal pain, shortness of breath, and nausea.

transcript: Alright, so we've got a 66-year-old male, chief complaint of traumatic injury. We found him at a school, in a maintenance closet. Staff said he has a history of epilepsy and they found him on the ground, just really weak and out of it. He's alert but confused, GCS is 11. No signs of trauma, he denies any pain, so zero out of ten. His vitals are a little wonky: BP is 146 over 90, heart rate 104, respirations 21, and he's saturating at 95 percent on room air. He's also warm to the touch, definitely has a fever. We did a 12-lead, no acute changes. Per the General Heat Exhaustion protocol, we started to cool him. Uh, we did inform the doctor of our findings. Meds given were, let's see, 0.98 milligrams of epinephrine PO and 45.49 milligrams of Fentanyl SL for agitation. He has no known allergies.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient with a history of epilepsy was found on the ground in a maintenance closet at a school, presenting as weak and confused.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 56-year-old male, a traumatic injury after a fall at his residence. He's complaining of generalized fatigue and some angina-like pain, which he rates a 2 out of 10. On assessment, we noted he was tachycardic, had generalized hyperhidrosis, and an active epistaxis. His vitals are heart rate 126, BP 143 over 96, respirations 22, and SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. He's alert but a little slow, GCS of 14. He has a history of angina pectoris. We performed an evaluation. For his pain, we administered 41.56 milligrams of Fentanyl IM. He also felt nauseous, so we gave him 3.53 milligrams of Ondansetron via nebulizer. Our impression is other malaise, with a concern for acute respiratory distress given the sats and fall.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient sustained a traumatic injury from a fall at his home.

transcript: We are five minutes out with a 51-year-old female found unconscious. Per family, she was complaining of a fever and some pretty bad abdominal pain all day before she collapsed. We found her unresponsive to all stimuli. Initial vitals were unobtainable, she was in a PEA rhythm for about a minute. We administered 1.12 milligrams of epinephrine IV push and got ROSC. Her blood pressure is now 160 over 90, heart rate is 102, and respirations are 19. We have her on a non-rebreather mask, SpO2 is 100 percent. She remains unresponsive. We're running it as a possible stroke, so we're following that protocol. Family states she is allergic to Codeine.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient complained of fever and abdominal pain before collapsing. She was found unresponsive by family and experienced a brief PEA arrest prior to EMS arrival.

transcript: Hey, we have a 60-year-old male coming in from a residence. Initial call was for eye problems, but that's not the main issue. He developed sudden, severe abdominal pain. He's got a history of COPD and is allergic to Penicillin. He's presenting with general weakness, a bit of a cough, and some referred pain in his shoulder. His vitals are pretty concerning—he's hypotensive at 65 over 38, heart rate is 105. Respirations are 16, SpO2 is 97%. He's alert but confused, GCS is 11. He rates his pain a 3 out of 10. We have him on a 3-lead. We gave him 2.39 milligrams of Albuterol IM and 49.4 milligrams of Fentanyl also IM. Our primary impression is an acute abdomen.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient initially complained of an eye problem but then developed sudden, severe abdominal pain at his residence.

transcript: Hey, we're coming in emergent with an 87-year-old male we picked up from the park. He called for breathing problems. He states he was just sitting on a bench when he suddenly developed a cough, abdominal pain, and weakness. He's also complaining of a 'pins and needles' feeling in his hands, some paresthesia. He rates his abdominal pain a 7 out of 10. He is alert and oriented, GCS 15. Vitals are BP 144 over 97, heart rate is 134, respiratory rate is 19, and his SpO2 was 93 percent, so we have him on O2. He has a history of hypertension. We have him on the cardiac monitor and are trending his pulse oximetry. He was quite agitated, so we gave 2.58 milligrams of Midazolam via nebulizer, and for his complaint of nausea, we gave 3.97 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually. Our ETA is five minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of coughing, abdominal pain, weakness, and paresthesia while sitting on a park bench.

transcript: Alright, we're coming in with a 23-year-old male from the university library. His friends called because he became acutely weak and dizzy. Our primary impression is dizziness and giddiness. He's complaining of severe cervicalgia, a ten out of ten, says it's the worst pain he's ever had. He's also got a fever and experienced some nausea with vomiting just once before our arrival. He seems a little confused, so GCS is 14, but he is alert. Vitals are blood pressure 129 over 85, heart rate 92, respirations 20, and SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. We did a blood glucose measurement, it was normal. He has a history of migraines but takes no daily meds. No known drug allergies. We haven't given any meds. Transport was uneventful. He's on our monitor but that's it.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of weakness, dizziness, and severe neck pain while at the school library.

transcript: Hey doc, we're coming in with a 91-year-old male from a residence, complaining of generalized weakness. Family called because he's been more confused and weak over the last day or so. He's alert, but GCS is a 13. He's complaining of a five out of ten neck pain, a bit of a cough, and says it hurts to urinate. Vitals are a little soft, BP is 91 over 50, heart rate 62, respirations are 14, and he's saturating at 97 percent on room air. History includes dementia and BPH, and he's allergic to sulfa drugs. We're thinking this is likely a UTI. We have him on the cardiac monitor and we're monitoring his end-tidal CO2. His pressure was a bit worrisome so we did start a small push-pressor dose of epinephrine, gave him 1.12 milligrams IV, which brought his pressure up slightly. We'll be there in about five.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Urinary tract infection, site not specified
pcr_json.events_leading: Family called due to patient's increased confusion and weakness over the past day.

transcript: Morning. This is a 77-year-old female we picked up from a private commercial establishment. Chief complaint is generalized weakness. She apparently had a syncopal episode and collapsed. She's also complaining of some unspecified abdominal pain and pain in her shoulder, rates it a 4 out of 10. Our primary impression is an unspecified injury, with a secondary of nausea with vomiting. She is alert now, but on our initial assessment her GCS was a 6. Vitals are BP 145 over 93, heart rate 73, respirations 20, and SpO2 is 98 percent. We established IV access for catheterization of the vein and put her on for cardiac monitoring. Per our standing orders, which fell under the General-Neonatal Care protocol, we gave 344.53 milligrams of Aspirin IM. Her history includes GERD and osteoarthritis. She takes Omeprazole for it. She's allergic to sulfa drugs.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a syncopal episode and collapsed while at a private commercial establishment.

transcript: We have a 44-year-old female, picked up from a sports area. The initial call was for a psychiatric problem. Patient is alert and oriented, GCS of 15. She was watching her kid's game and started feeling a sense of malaise and had some paresthesia in her skin. She also notes some cervicalgia and has had diarrhea and a fever for the past day. She rates her back pain, the dorsalgia, as a 1 out of 10. Vitals are stable: BP 102 over 60, heart rate is 68, respirations 14, SpO2 is 98 percent. She has a history of anxiety and is allergic to Sulfa drugs. We put her on the monitor and did a 12-lead, no acute changes seen. Primary impression is dorsalgia with other malaise. We consulted the Trauma-Eye Injury protocol for some reason, that's what was documented. No meds given.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was watching her child's game when she began experiencing a sense of malaise and paresthesia.

transcript: Good morning, we're en route with a 92-year-old male from his residence, family called for altered mental status. Our primary impression is unspecified illness, but we suspect a UTI. He is alert to voice but confused, GCS is a 13. He's got a heart rate of 110, BP of 141 over 78, respirations are 19, and SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. He's complaining of abdominal pain that he rates a 5 out of 10 and also has a productive cough. He's aphasic, which the family says is worse than baseline. His history includes epilepsy. We've established an IV in his left arm. No medications were administered. We're about 5 minutes from your doors.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Urinary tract infection, site not specified
pcr_json.events_leading: Family called EMS after finding the patient with a more confused and aphasic mental state than his baseline.

transcript: We've got a 72-year-old male we picked up at a department store for a diabetic problem. We found him extremely disoriented, GCS was a 10 on scene. Staff said he was just wandering around looking confused. He has a history of Type 2 diabetes. He's also complaining of some neck pain and abdominal pain but denies any trauma. He's got a cough and mentioned having diarrhea earlier. His vitals are pretty concerning: BP is 189 over 130, heart rate is 90, respirations 22, and SpO2 is 95 percent. Given the presentation, we followed our... uh... protocol and administered 1.0 milligrams of epinephrine PO and 2.18 milligrams of Midazolam via nebulizer for his agitation. We established an IV but haven't pushed anything else. No known allergies. He takes Metformin at home.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient with a history of diabetes was found wandering and disoriented inside a commercial establishment. He was agitated and confused upon EMS arrival.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 70-year-old male for you. Call was for a traumatic injury at a school. He had a syncopal episode while sitting in a lecture and slumped over. He's alert now, GCS is 15. He denies any pain, so zero out of ten. He does complain of some nausea, feeling feverish, and a bit of neck pain. Vitals are stable: BP 147 over 88, heart rate 104, respirations 23, and SpO2 is 96 percent. We have him on two liters of oxygen via nasal cannula and he's on the 3-lead, showing sinus tach. Primary impression is syncope and collapse. His wife mentioned a past history of Afib and BPH. He takes Eliquis and Tamsulosin for that. He is allergic to Sulfa drugs. No medications were given. We're treating this as emergent under the General-Neonatal Care protocol.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a syncopal episode while seated during a lecture, slumping over in his chair.

transcript: Hey, we're about five minutes out with a 97-year-old female from a hair salon. Staff found her unconscious, but she came to just before we got there. Chief complaint is fainting. She's alert now, GCS is 15, but she's showing signs of aphasia and profound weakness. Her daughter, who met us on scene, says she's also had some dysuria for a few days. Vitals are blood pressure 146 over 99, heart rate is 100, respirations 16, and she's 100 percent on room air. She says her pain is a 2 out of 10, but can't describe it. We started an 18 gauge in her right arm. Her past medical history includes hypertension and dementia. She's allergic to penicillin. Our primary impression is acute respiratory distress syndrome.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found unconscious at a hair salon; regained consciousness prior to EMS arrival. Has had dysuria for several days.

transcript: Hey doc, we have a 78-year-old male coming from a local school where he was volunteering. Chief complaint is nausea and dizziness. He says he started feeling weak and dizzy about an hour ago. He's got a bit of a cough and was hyperventilating a little when we got there. He rates his discomfort a 2 out of 10. He is alert and oriented, GCS 15. His vitals are stable: BP 143 over 86, heart rate 88, respirations are 21, and SpO2 is 96 percent on room air. He has a history of hypertension and Afib. He takes warfarin and metoprolol. No known allergies. We placed him on the cardiac monitor. Per protocol, we administered 316.1 milligrams of Aspirin sublingually and also gave 0.32 milligrams of Nitroglycerin via nebulizer. His dizziness has slightly improved since.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of weakness and dizziness while volunteering at a school.

transcript: Hey, we're inbound with a 47-year-old female from the Pines nursing home, chief complaint of a fall. Staff found her on the floor of her room, confused. Her GCS is a 13. She's got a fever and some generalized abdominal pain. Vitals are BP 158 over 110, heart rate 97, respirations are 20, and O2 sat is 95 percent. She has a history of diabetes and COPD. Currently denies pain, so a zero out of ten, but she was complaining of significant pain on our arrival. We're on the Diabetic Emergency protocol. We administered 0.49 milligrams of Ipratropium via nebulizer for some mild wheezing. We also gave 4.05 milligrams of Morphine IM for her earlier pain. We've moved her to our stretcher and we are about 10 minutes out. No known allergies.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the floor of her room at an institutional residence after an unwitnessed fall.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 100-year-old female involved in a minor MVC in a school parking lot. She was a restrained passenger. Our primary impression is nausea with vomiting, and she also has some dorsalgia, or back pain, which she rates a 2 out of 10. She's alert, GCS 14. She's also complaining of a fever, general fatigue, and some paresthesia in her hands. Vitals are: BP 188 over 120, heart rate 76, respirations 21, and SpO2 is 92 percent on room air. We've got her on 3-lead and 12-lead monitoring, and capnography. Under the Neonatal Care protocol for symptom control, we gave 42.98 milligrams of Sodium Bicarbonate via nebulizer for her nausea, and because of her pain and paresthesia, we also gave 42.98 milligrams of Fentanyl IV. Her history includes osteoporosis, and she is allergic to Sulfa drugs. We're about 5 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was a restrained passenger in a minor motor vehicle collision in a school parking lot.

transcript: We are en route with a 1-year-old male, emergent transport. We were dispatched to an unspecified residence for a psychiatric problem, but on scene we found a very sick infant. Primary impression is altered mental status. Parents state he's had a fever, cough, and significant nausea and vomiting for the past two days. Today he became extremely lethargic. He's alert to painful stimuli only, with a GCS of 7. Secondary impression is weakness. His vitals are a blood pressure of 137 over 76, heart rate 82, respirations 17, and an SpO2 of 96 percent. He's not in any apparent pain. No medications were administered. We have him on a 3-lead and we did get a 12-lead EKG. Parents aren't aware of any allergies or medical history.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed extreme lethargy following a two-day history of fever, cough, and vomiting.

transcript: Hi, we have a 27-year-old male, chief complaint of breathing problems. We picked him up at a group home. He's been feeling sick all day with a cough, some ear pain, and now this generalized abdominal pain. He says it hurts to urinate, too. He's a little confused, GCS is a 12. Vitals are blood pressure 139 over 79, heart rate is tachy at 112, respirations 26, and SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. He rates his abdominal pain a 5 out of 10. His only history is asthma. No known allergies. We got a line established and have him on capnography. We gave him 6.65 milligrams of Adenosine orally per our protocol for his breathing. Our primary impression is unspecified abdominal pain with some nausea.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt sick all day with multiple complaints, developing breathing problems and abdominal pain.

transcript: We're coming in with a 36-year-old male found on the side of the highway, initially called in for nausea and vomiting. He is very altered, GCS is a 10, though he is alert to voice. We can't get much of a history but he seems to be complaining of weakness. Bystanders said he was having some diarrhea. He denies pain. His vitals are pretty concerning: BP is 161 over 105, heart rate is 114, respiratory rate is 22, and he's 100 percent on room air. Given his mental status, our primary impression is a mental disorder, not otherwise specified. We have not administered any medications. Our only procedure was taking patient vital signs. We're about 5 minutes out. No history or allergies are known.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found on the side of the highway, altered, with reports of vomiting and diarrhea.

transcript: Hey, we're bringing you a 42-year-old female from the group home on Elm. The initial call was for eye problems, but that was inaccurate. Her actual complaint is a severe headache and a persistent cough. She's breathing at 22 times a minute and her SpO2 is 94 percent. She's alert but slow to respond, so we got a GCS of 13. Her blood pressure is elevated at 172 over 107, with a heart rate of 65. She rates her headache pain as a 4 out of 10. She also mentioned some pain when she urinates. We got her vital signs and placed her on the cardiac monitor. Given her presentation, we administered 368.63 milligrams of sublingual Aspirin. Her past medical history is notable for Type 2 Diabetes and high cholesterol. She has no known drug allergies.

transcript: Hey, we have a 45-year-old male from an unspecified residence, called in for heart problems. He's presenting with weakness, nausea, a cough, and a headache. He also says he has difficulty walking. He's alert but seems confused, GCS is a 13. He's quite hypoxic, his SpO2 was 88 percent so we have him on O2. His blood pressure is low at 98 over 59, heart rate is 85, and respiratory rate is 22. He denies any pain, a zero out of ten. We've just been doing electrocardiographic monitoring en route. His past history is significant for Type 2 Diabetes and he is allergic to penicillin. We suspect hyperglycemia is contributing to his condition. No meds were given. We'll be there in 5.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called from his residence for heart problems, found with weakness, nausea, cough, headache, and difficulty walking.

transcript: We're inbound with a 76-year-old male from a residence, for a traumatic injury. It was a witnessed fall from standing. He's complaining of dizziness, fatigue, and some nausea. He also has some upper abdominal pain, which he rates a 1 out of 10. He's alert but seems confused, GCS is a 10. His vitals are a little concerning: BP is low at 94 over 56, heart rate is 80, respirations are 22, and his SpO2 is 93 percent on room air. We've established an IV and have him on the cardiac monitor. For his pain, we administered 718.87 milligrams of Acetaminophen via IM injection. Our primary impression is dizziness. ETA is 5 minutes.

transcript: Alright, we're bringing you a 62-year-old male we picked up from the side of the road, initial call was just for a sick person. He's complaining of diffuse, unspecified abdominal pain, rates it an 8 out of 10. He also has a very sore throat and says it's difficult to swallow. He seems fatigued and a little confused, GCS is a 14. Vitals are soft, BP is 84 over 56. Heart rate is 73, respirations 14, and his SpO2 is 97 percent on room air. We have him on the cardiac monitor and are monitoring his end-tidal CO2. We gave him 3.78 milligrams of Ondansetron sublingually for nausea. His history includes hypertension and GERD. pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found by a bystander on a public street complaining of feeling sick, abdominal pain, sore throat, and difficulty swallowing.

transcript: Hey team, we have a 59-year-old female, emergent transport. We picked her up on the side of the highway. Chief complaint is chest pain. Uh, she was driving when she felt suddenly weak and developed a cough and what she describes as a seven-out-of-ten abdominal pain, which seems to be her primary issue. She has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril. No known allergies. On exam, she's got a slight fever and some mild aphasia, GCS is thirteen. Her initial vitals were blood pressure 144 over 82, heart rate 81, respirations 15, and an SpO2 of 92 percent on room air. We started a line and did a 12-lead, which shows sinus tach. Per protocol, we gave one sublingual nitroglycerin, that was zero point three seven milligrams, with no change in her pain. We're about five minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was driving when she experienced a sudden onset of weakness, chest pain, and abdominal pain, causing her to pull over on the highway and call for help.

transcript: Hey, we've got an 86-year-old male we picked up from a school. Staff called for seizure-like activity. He's been feeling sick for a couple of days with a sore throat, diarrhea, and some abdominal pain. On arrival, he was pretty confused, GCS was 13 but he is alert. He also had a bit of a nosebleed. His sugar was a little low so we gave 15.9 milligrams of Glucose IV, per our chest pain protocol for AMS. His history includes Type 2 Diabetes and Afib. He's allergic to sulfa drugs. pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced seizure-like activity and confusion at a school after a two-day history of sore throat and gastrointestinal upset.

transcript: We're headed your way with a 65-year-old female from an institutional building, found after a near-syncopal episode. Our primary impression is weakness with a secondary of altered mental status. Patient is alert with a GCS of 15. She's been feeling generally unwell, with a fever and a cough. We initiated the GI Bleed protocol based on family report of dark stools, but her main issue seems respiratory. Vitals are BP 137 over 93, heart rate 96, respirations 16, and O2 sat is 94 percent. Pain is a 3 out of 10. She's allergic to Codeine. We did a 12-lead, which was unremarkable. For the cough, we administered 0.49 milligrams of Ipratropium via nebulizer. pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a near-syncopal episode after several days of feeling generally unwell with a fever, cough, and reported dark stools.

transcript: Alright, we're coming in with a 72-year-old female, driver in a single-vehicle MVC on the highway. We're treating her for an altered mental status. She's got a GCS of 10, she's not really following commands. Complaining of 6 out of 10 abdominal pain and says she has difficulty walking, though that's probably related to the accident. Her vitals are stable: BP 138 over 87, heart rate 88, respirations 14, SpO2 is 95 percent. We've established an IV. For pain, we gave 48.94 milligrams of Fentanyl IV, and for sedation for her agitation we gave 43.66 milligrams of Ketamine IM. Her past history includes essential hypertension. She has no known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was the driver in a single-vehicle motor vehicle collision on the highway.

transcript: We have a 63-year-old female from a mobile home, chief complaint was an eye problem. She called because of a sudden severe headache and blurry vision. transcript: Alright, we have an 81-year-old male, picked up from a reform school. The dispatch was for a traffic incident, but he was just a bystander who felt unwell. He's complaining of some pretty significant cough, diarrhea, and unspecified abdominal pain. He also had one instance of hematemesis. He denies any pain, so pain scale is a zero. Vitals are: BP 151 over 97, heart rate 69, respirations are a bit high at 24, and SpO2 is 92 percent on ambient air. He's alert, GCS 15. Past history of a STEMI two years ago. Per our General-Sepsis protocol for his presentation, we established an IV. We also administered 312.54 milligrams of Aspirin via the IV and 0.34 milligrams of Nitroglycerin intramuscularly. We've been doing continuous physical assessment. Our primary impression is just unspecified pain in limb, maybe from standing around, with a secondary of fatigue.

transcript: We're coming in with a 97-year-old female from a residence. Initial call was for a psychiatric problem, but we found her in acute respiratory distress. She's got a GCS of 13, is alert, and complains of generalized pain, maybe a 2 out of 10. She's had a cough, fever, and some diarrhea for the past day according to her daughter. Her history includes CHF and dementia. Allergies to Penicillin. On scene she had a severe bronchospasm and we were concerned about impending failure, so we administered 0.83 milligrams of EPINEPHrine via IM injection under our neonatal care protocol for dose reference. Her vitals have improved slightly; blood pressure is 105 over 71, heart rate is 86, respiratory rate is 24, and she's satting 96 percent on a non-rebreather. We have her on the cardiac monitor and placed defibrillation pads as a precaution. We'll be there in about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed acute respiratory distress with severe bronchospasm following a day of cough, fever, and diarrhea.

transcript: Morning. We're bringing you a 76-year-old female from the local school's admin office. The call came in for eye problems, but we found her extremely weak and lethargic. Her GCS is 11. pcr_json.events_leading: Patient, a staff member at a school, felt progressively weak and unwell throughout the day, presenting with fever, epistaxis, and dysuria.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 15-year-old male, found wandering on the street. Chief complaint is nausea and some general weakness. He says he just felt really dizzy all of a sudden. transcript: Good morning. We're coming in with a 75-year-old male from a school. He was attending an event for his grandson and had a syncopal episode. Chief complaint is just 'Sick Person'. transcript: Okay, this is a 48-year-old male from a residence. The family called for a seizure. They state he's had a fever and just hasn't felt well for a couple days, just general weakness. transcript: Hey, we're inbound to bay 3 with a 73-year-old male from the reform school. Chief complaint is weakness, possible seizure. transcript: We're en route with a 73-year-old male, picked up from a trade area after a fall. Chief complaint is traumatic injury. He tripped and struck his chest. transcript: Alright, we're coming into Room 3. We have a 42-year-old female teacher from the local middle school with a chief complaint of chest pain. Vitals are blood pressure 103 over 69, heart rate is 115, respirations are 25, and SpO2 is 94 percent. Her GCS is a 13; she's alert but seems a little off. She rates her pain a 7 out of 10. We started a line but have not given any medications. Her history is positive for primary hypertension. She is not aware of any allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient reports a fall followed by the onset of chest pain and fatigue.

transcript: We're en route with a 97-year-old female from her residence, found by her daughter after a near-fainting episode. The patient said she just felt really dizzy. She is complaining of some pain in an unspecified shoulder, rates it a 1 out of 10. She also mentioned having some diarrhea earlier. On exam, she's alert but very slow to respond, GCS is 12. No known drug allergies. She has a history of high cholesterol and osteoporosis. Her vitals are a blood pressure of 132 over 91, heart rate 94, respiratory rate 17, and an SpO2 of 92 percent. We assisted her ventilations with our ventilator and acquired a 12 lead EKG. We didn't administer any medications, following the anaphylaxis protocol. Primary impression is dizziness and giddiness, secondary is other chest pain given the shoulder complaint. We'll be there in about five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt very dizzy, leading to a near-fainting episode where she was found by her daughter.

transcript: Hey team, we're inbound with a 63-year-old male for breathing problems. We picked him up on the highway. He was found by family extremely short of breath. He's unresponsive to verbal, only withdraws to pain. GCS is a 14. He has a history of COPD and hypertension. His current meds are Spiriva and lisinopril. He's hypotensive at 82 over 46, tachycardic at 114, respirations are 24, and he's saturating at 93 percent on two liters via nasal cannula. He's also complaining of some ear pain and abdominal pain when we can get anything out of him. We've got a 12-lead on him, shows sinus tach. He was extremely weak on scene. We're on the Airway protocol. We gave 2.94 milligrams of Albuterol via nebulizer, and also pushed 0.8 milligrams of Epi sublingually. He got pretty agitated so we gave 2.63 milligrams of Midazolam IV. We've already spoken with the doc, he knows we're coming. Should be there in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by family with extreme shortness of breath.

transcript: We are five minutes out with a 47-year-old female, found at her residence after a presumed fall. She's complaining of breathing problems and some weakness. She also mentioned some chest pain, kind of like angina. She is alert but seems disoriented, GCS is 13. Her sats were a little low at 94 percent, respiratory rate is 21. Her BP is stable at 121 over 68, heart rate 77. She denies any pain on the scale, so a zero out of ten. We did a 12-lead, no ST changes noted. Given her disorientation, we administered 56.9 milligrams of Ketamine sublingually. She has a history of anxiety and hypertension, and an allergy to Penicillin. Our primary impression is an unspecified head injury.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found at her residence after a presumed fall, leading to chief complaints of breathing problems and disorientation.

transcript: Good morning. We're bringing in a 27-year-old female from a sports area. Chief complaint was breathing problems. She's been feeling ill and says she has a fever. Today she started experiencing hematemesis. On scene, we found her alert, GCS of 15. Her BP was 118 over 74, heart rate was tachycardic at 108, respirations 18. Her SpO2 was 91 percent on room air, so we have her on a little O2. She rates her pain a 2 out of 10, more just malaise than anything specific. We did a 12-lead which showed sinus tach and a standard evaluation. She has a history of GERD but reports no known drug allergies. The selected protocol was General-Eye Injury, which was a charting error, but care was appropriate for her presentation. We're considering her emergent.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed shortness of breath at a sports area after feeling ill with a fever and then starting to vomit blood.

transcript: We're bringing you a 57-year-old female from a school. She had a syncopal episode, witnessed by students. On arrival, she was alert, GCS 15. She complains of feeling febrile and some chest tightness she calls angina pectoris, but rates her pain a zero out of ten. Her vitals are a bit hypertensive at 171 over 114, heart rate is 95, respirations 14, and SpO2 is 94 percent. We did an evaluation and continuous pulse oximetry. She became agitated during the assessment, requiring brief physical restraint. Primary impression is nausea with vomiting. We gave Albuterol 2.61 milligrams by mouth. Her past medical history is significant for asthma, GERD, and hypertension. She has no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient, a teacher, felt suddenly weak and nauseous during class and had a witnessed syncopal episode.

transcript: Medic 3 to General, we're inbound with a 27-year-old female from a commercial establishment. She reports a sudden onset of severe shortness of breath. She also has some abdominal pain and generalized weakness. She's got an active epistaxis that started around the same time. She is alert and oriented, GCS 15, and denies any pain, so zero out of ten. Vitals are pretty concerning: BP is 169 over 115, heart rate 81, respiratory rate is 25 and labored. Her SpO2 was only 91 percent on room air. We had difficulty establishing peripheral access, so we placed an IO in the right proximal humerus. We're managing her airway and transporting emergently. She has a history of lupus. No known allergies. We'll be there in about 2 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of shortness of breath, weakness, and a nosebleed while at a commercial establishment.

transcript: Okay, so we've got a 10-year-old female, we were called for a person down. We found her at her residence, mom says she just collapsed in the living room. She's alert now but very confused. Initial GCS was 11. She's complaining of a six out of ten chest pain and a headache. Uh, she's also had some shortness of breath, a bloody nose, and some vague abdominal pain. Vitals on scene were blood pressure 152 over 92, heart rate 116, respirations 25, and she was saturating at 89 percent on room air. She's got a history of asthma but no other major medical history. She takes albuterol as needed. No known allergies. We've had her on a 3-lead since we got there. Per protocol General-Neonatal Care, we gave Albuterol slash Ipratropium, two point eight-eight milligrams via IM, and we also administered Nitroglycerin, zero point three-four milligrams, uh, per oral route, with some improvement in her breathing. We're just continuing to assess her en route.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient collapsed in the living room, as witnessed by her mother.

transcript: Medic 3 to Memorial ED. We have a 9-year-old female we picked up from her home, complaining of chest pain. Apparently, she was playing outside, tripped, and hurt her hip. She's been very anxious since the fall, and is now complaining of chest pain, some neck pain, a slight cough, and just generalized abdominal pain. She rates her pain a 3 out of 10. She is alert and oriented, GCS is 15. Her vitals are a bit concerning: BP is 184 over 110, heart rate is 59, respirations are 21. Her SPO2 is 95 percent. We established an IV line and performed airway suction for some minor secretions. We did contact the doctor to inform them of the patient's condition. Per mom, she has an allergy to penicillin. We did not administer any medications. We're following the General-Chest Pain protocol. Our ETA is 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was playing outside, tripped and fell, hurting her hip.

transcript: We have a 45-year-old female, chief complaint is fainting. She was found at her home after a syncopal episode, possibly with a head injury on the way down. Her primary issue seems to be profound weakness. She is alert, GCS is 15. She's complaining of severe dizziness, fatigue, and a ten out of ten pain, though she can't localize it. She also reports a cough and diarrhea for the past two days. Her vitals are... blood pressure 115 over 73, but her heart rate is way up at 138. Respiratory rate is 19, SpO2 is 94 percent. We placed her on a three-lead, which just shows sinus tachycardia. No meds given en route. She takes Lisinopril for high blood pressure. No known allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a syncopal episode at home and may have sustained a head injury during the fall. She has been feeling weak with a cough and diarrhea for two days prior.

transcript: We are en route with a 91-year-old male, picked up from a school for a traumatic injury. It looks like he tripped and fell, with no reported loss of consciousness. His primary complaint is a headache, which he rates as a 4 out of 10 on the pain scale. He's alert with a GCS of 15. Initial vitals are a heart rate of 108, BP 136 over 92, respirations at 22, and an SpO2 of 95 percent on room air. Family reports a history of hypertension and dementia. He is allergic to codeine. We established an IV and performed a 12 lead, which showed sinus tachycardia consistent with his heart rate. No medications were administered. We are about 5 minutes away.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient tripped and fell at a school, resulting in a headache without loss of consciousness.

transcript: Alright, we've got a 24-year-old female, picked up from her residence. Chief complaint was near-syncope. Her roommate called because she was complaining of severe abdominal pain and diarrhea, then got really short of breath and felt like she was going to pass out. She rates the pain a 10 out of 10. She's alert, but her GCS is 14. Her initial BP was pretty high at 171 over 118, heart rate is 135, respirations 18, and her SpO2 is 98 percent. We checked a blood glucose, it was normal. We also put her on the monitor. We're working off the sepsis protocol. For treatment, we gave 49.98 milligrams of Fentanyl via nebulizer for her pain, and we also gave 3.7 milligrams of Ondansetron IM. She has no known drug allergies and no significant past medical history.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced severe abdominal pain and diarrhea at home, followed by dyspnea and a near-syncopal episode, prompting her roommate to call 911.

transcript: Hey, we're about five minutes out with a 28-year-old male, trauma from a traffic incident. He was the restrained driver in a single-vehicle MVC at his residence. He's complaining of unspecified chest pain, which he rates a 3 out of 10, and significant weakness. He also had one episode of hematemesis on our arrival. He is alert but confused; GCS is a 10. Vitals are stable: BP 142 over 79, heart rate 73, respirations 18, and an SpO2 of 97 percent. We've initiated an evaluation procedure and established venous access. Per the General-Headache protocol for some reason, we administered 1.19 milligrams of EPINEPHrine via nebulizer. He has a history of asthma and is allergic to sulfa drugs. We're thinking unspecified chest pain as a primary.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was the restrained driver in a single-vehicle MVC.

transcript: Hey doc, we're coming in with an 88-year-old male, found down on the street. Bystanders weren't sure what happened. Uh, chief complaint is traumatic injury, but we're thinking this is more of a sepsis picture. He's got some altered mental status for sure. He's alert on the AVPU scale 'cause his eyes are open, but his GCS is only a 5. Really bizarre presentation. He's febrile and clammy, complaining of difficulty swallowing and has had diarrhea. Vitals are BP 144 over 86, heart rate is 85, respirations 14, and he's saturating at 100 percent on room air. He's got a pain score of 1 out of 10. We established an IV and have him on a 3-lead. Per his wife on the phone, he has a history of dementia and recurrent UTIs. He takes Donepezil, but she couldn't recall any allergies. We're following the influenza-like illness protocol. We should be there in about five minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found down on the street by bystanders with an unknown mechanism or downtime.

transcript: Alright, we're here with a 43-year-old male from a private residence, complaining of severe nausea and vomiting for the past twelve hours. He also reports diarrhea, some unspecified abdominal pain, and a fever. Patient feels a lot of general malaise and is very worried about his condition. He rates his pain a 7 out of 10. We placed him on the monitor, got a 12-lead, and administered 4.08 milligrams of Ondansetron via nebulizer for the nausea. He has a history of asthma and generalized anxiety disorder. He's allergic to Penicillin.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a twelve-hour onset of severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, and fever at his private residence.

transcript: Alright, this is a 69-year-old female from a reform school. The initial dispatch was for a psychiatric problem. On our arrival, staff said she had a syncopal episode. transcript: Alright, we have a 17-year-old male, chief complaint of a head injury. transcript: We're bringing you a 28-year-old male from the university science building. He had a sudden onset of severe eye pain while working in the lab, though he denies any chemical splash. transcript: Hey Doc, we have a 100-year-old female for you from the nursing home. Staff called for a well-person check; they said she just seemed weaker than usual. pcr_json.events_leading: Patient's nursing home staff called for a check because she seemed weaker than her baseline.

transcript: Hey doc, we're inbound with an 83-year-old male, victim of a residential fire. He was extricated by the fire department just before our arrival. transcript: We're en route with a 47-year-old male from his house. His wife called for a seizure. He's postictal and confused, GCS 14. He's got some aphasia, having trouble finding words. pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a seizure at home witnessed by his wife. Found postictal and confused upon EMS arrival.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 39-year-old male coming from the elementary school. Chief complaint was an animal bite, but he seems to have other issues. pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was bitten by an animal at a school. Upon arrival, he was found to be confused with a GCS of 9, complaining of chest and abdominal pain.

transcript: Hey, we've got an 89-year-old female for you. We were called out to her residence for a post-MVC evaluation. Apparently, she had a minor traffic incident earlier today and refused transport, but her daughter called because she developed a cough, some abdominal pain, and a pretty significant nosebleed. pcr_json.events_leading: Patient had a minor traffic incident earlier in the day and refused transport. Family called later due to development of a cough, abdominal pain, nosebleed, and nausea.

transcript: Hi, we're bringing in a 33-year-old female from a local school. Staff called for a sick person. She's complaining of general malaise, weakness, and some pretty significant back pain. We did a 12-lead, which suggests some kind of arrhythmia, but she's stable. Uh, let's see... she has a history of recurrent UTIs and is allergic to sulfa drugs. We just monitored her resps and informed the charge nurse we were on our way.

We did a 12-lead which shows sinus bradycardia and we've established a saline lock. No meds given by us. She is allergic to codeine. We're treating her as emergent.

transcript: Hey Doc, we're bringing you a 15-year-old female from the local high school. Chief complaint is just sick person. Per protocol, we gave her 2.93 milligrams of Albuterol via nebulizer and 0.43 milligrams of Atrovent, uh, PO. We're about 5 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was in her school class when she began to feel profoundly weak, which was followed by the onset of a cough, fever, and headache.

transcript: Hi, we have a 58-year-old female coming from her residence. The chief complaint is weakness. We found her in bed, very lethargic. She's alert but confused, GCS is 12. She complains of nausea with vomiting and generalized weakness. She rates her pain at a 3 out of 10, just a dull ache all over. She's hypotensive and tachycardic—BP is 104 over 58, heart rate is 120. transcript: Hey there, we have an 83-year-old male. The call came from a local college for eye problems, but on arrival we found the patient had a syncopal episode during a lecture. He was alert and oriented on our arrival, GCS 15. He denies any eye issues but does complain of an acute pharyngitis and feeling feverish. His unspecified pain is a 3 out of 10. Based on his history of coronary artery disease, we're treating this as potential cardiac syncope under the General-Chest Pain protocol. We gave 328.77 milligrams of Aspirin sublingually, and also 0.33 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IM. He states he has no known allergies. We believe he had a syncope and collapse.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a syncopal episode while attending a lecture. Upon regaining consciousness, he complained of a sore throat and feeling feverish.

transcript: Hey, we're coming in hot with a pediatric trauma. We have a 1-year-old male from a residence. Chief complaint is traumatic injury. pcr_json.events_leading: Patient fell from a high chair, had a lucid interval, then became unresponsive.

Vitals are a bit concerning: BP is 182 over 123, heart rate is tachycardic at 141, respirations are 13, and SpO2 is 98 percent. We established IV access and have him on the cardiac monitor. His history includes dementia and congestive heart failure. He has no known allergies. Per our Airway protocol for sedation, we administered 297.82 milligrams of Aspirin via IM injection and also gave 3.91 milligrams of Ondansetron via nebulizer. We're about 10 minutes away.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found with severe chest pain and fatigue at his institutional residence after staff called for a psychiatric problem.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 73-year-old female we picked up from a salon. Chief complaint is just "sick person." We found her feeling generally weak with some unspecified abdominal pain. She denies any pain, so zero out of ten. We did some continuous physical assessments en route. Per our General-Neonatal Care protocol, we administered, uh, 106.82 milligrams of Methylprednisolone sublingually, 14.03 milligrams of Diltiazem via nebulizer, and 45.94 milligrams of Fentanyl IV.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was at a salon when she began feeling weak with abdominal pain, leading to an episode of emesis just prior to EMS arrival.

transcript: We're en route with a 59-year-old female from a school. She's a teacher who experienced a sudden onset of generalized weakness and dizziness. transcript: We have a 68-year-old male coming from a vocational school. Staff called for a behavioral issue, said he was having a severe anxiety attack. He's complaining of pain, rates it a 7 out of 10, but can't specify where. We're seeing some tachycardia. His vitals are concerning: BP is low at 98 over 59, heart rate is 79, respirations are 17, and his SpO2 is 98 percent. Due to his poor respiratory effort alongside the altered mental status, we initiated CPAP. Our primary impression is an unspecified anxiety disorder with decompensation. We haven't administered any medications.

procedures: Continuous positive airway pressure ventilation treatment

transcript: We're coming in with a 39-year-old female from a local school. Initial call was for an eye injury, but she also has significant back pain and nausea. transcript: Alright, we're here with a 66-year-old female, chief complaint is just "sick person." transcript: Hey, we're coming in emergent from a local school with a 56-year-old female. Initial chief complaint was chest pain. It seems to be more of an allergic reaction.
