pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of weakness and feverish feelings, prompting him to call for an ambulance from a service area.

transcript: Hi, we have a 41-year-old female we picked up on the highway. Driver called because she had a sudden onset of severe nausea, shortness of breath, and some chest pain with breathing. She states she has a history of panic attacks. She rates her pain a 2 out of 10. She also says she feels feverish. On exam, she's alert and oriented, GCS of 15. Vitals are a bit elevated: BP is 169 over 102, heart rate is 60, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 99 percent. We have her on the cardiac monitor, just sinus bradycardia. For her nausea, we administered 3.68 milligrams of Ondansetron IM in the left deltoid. We're just providing transport and monitoring. Transfer of care should be straightforward. See you in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of severe nausea, shortness of breath, and chest pain while on the highway, which she believed was a panic attack.

transcript: We have a 74-year-old male we picked up from his residence. The call was for a sick person; a neighbor found him exhibiting some strange and inexplicable behavior. He complains of general fatigue and some dysuria. He rates his pain at a 3 out of 10, but it's pretty vague. On exam, he's not tracking well, GCS is an 8. Vitals are BP 120 over 72, heart rate is 90, respirations are 22, and SpO2 is 96 percent. He has a history of BPH and anxiety. He has no known drug allergies. We established a line and his blood glucose was normal. He was getting pretty agitated, so we administered 2.28 milligrams of Midazolam sublingually. Our primary impression is chest pain, unspecified, given his risk factors despite the vague complaints. We're about five minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by a neighbor exhibiting strange behavior, complaining of fatigue and dysuria.

transcript: Alright, we're inbound to your ED with a 66-year-old male, picked up from a local school where he works. Staff witnessed a seizure. He's postictal but back to a GCS of 15. He feels warm and complains of some neck pain, a 2 out of 10. He also had a brief epistaxis during the event. Vitals are: blood pressure 140 over 79, heart rate is 124, respirations 18, and SpO2 98 percent. We have him on the monitor, shows sinus tach. He says he has no known allergies. We gave 3.68 milligrams of Ondansetron and 327.14 milligrams of Aspirin, both orally. His medical history is significant for a prior stroke. We're thinking this is acute pain, but secondary to the seizure event.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a witnessed seizure at his place of work, followed by a postictal state with neck pain and a brief nosebleed.

transcript: Alright, we're bringing in a 79-year-old female from a residence, chief complaint is weakness. Family states she's been getting weaker over the last two days. On exam, she is not fully alert. Her GCS is a 10... she opens her eyes to voice but is confused and withdraws to touch. She's complaining of some unspecified abdominal pain and also some back pain, which she rates a 3 out of 10. Vitals are BP 150 over 89, heart rate is 78, respiratory rate is 21, and her SpO2 was 91 percent on room air. We've placed her on oxygen and have been doing continuous electrocardiographic monitoring. Per family, she has a history of atrial fibrillation and high cholesterol. She takes warfarin and atorvastatin. No known allergies. Our primary is a cardiac arrhythmia.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced progressive weakness over two days, leading to a decreased level of consciousness.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 98-year-old female from the skilled nursing facility. Staff called for chest pain and altered mental status. They found her very lethargic about an hour ago. She’s currently alert but confused, GCS is a 12. Her initial vitals were blood pressure 127 over 87, heart rate 78, respirations 18, and she was saturating at 92 percent on room air. She’s complaining of some vague abdominal pain and fatigue, rates her chest pain a 3 out of 10. We placed her on a 3-lead. IV access was impossible so we established an IO in the right tibia. We followed the sepsis protocol due to her mental status change. We administered 2.32 milligrams of Naloxone via the IV route, with no change in her condition. Past medical history includes dementia and she is bed-bound. She has a known allergy to Penicillin. We moved her to our stretcher for transport. Primary impression is a mental disorder, but we're concerned about something else given the hypoxia.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found lethargic by nursing facility staff with new onset altered mental status and chest pain.

transcript:
Alright, we're headed your way with a 56-year-old male from a warehouse, complaining of back pain. His coworkers called us. He says he was working when he suddenly felt weak, vomited once, and then the back pain started. He's also reporting some painful urination. Our primary impression is fever, possibly sepsis. We're on the sepsis protocol.

His vitals are blood pressure 124 over 69, heart rate is 96, respirations 17, and he's saturating 94 percent on room air. He's alert and oriented, GCS of 15. He rates his pain a 2 out of 10. We heard that bystanders may have initiated CPR before we arrived, but he was conscious and breathing when we got there so we had them stop. He has a history of type two diabetes and hypertension. He's allergic to penicillin. No meds given en route. See you in about 10.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was at work when he suddenly felt weak, vomited, and developed back pain and painful urination.

transcript: Okay, so we have a one-year-old male, found by his parents to be extremely lethargic. They say he's had a fever, diarrhea, and what they think is pain on urination for the last two days, but he got much worse this afternoon. On our arrival, he was minimally responsive. Vitals are concerning: heart rate is bradycardic at 58, blood pressure is 77 over 50, respirations 17, and he's saturating 95 percent on room air. His GCS is a 10. He does open his eyes to voice, so we're calling him Alert on the AVPU scale. He’s not crying, so we've got a pain scale of zero. Given the poor perfusion and bradycardia, we established an IO in the right tibia and have him on the cardiac monitor. We did a 3-lead as well. We are on the general diabetic emergency protocol, which is what printed out, but this looks more like sepsis to me. We have not administered any medications. Wait, my partner just reminded me, we did give Albuterol / Ipratropium 3.14 milligrams sublingually per a misread protocol, just be aware of that.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient found extremely lethargic by parents after a two-day history of fever, diarrhea, and painful urination.

transcript: Alright, we're bringing you a 33-year-old female from the university campus. The initial call was for a syncopal episode. Staff states she felt dizzy and feverish before she nearly fainted while teaching a class. On our arrival, she was alert but complaining of 7-out-of-10 generalized abdominal pain. Her vitals are concerning: BP is 181 over 125, heart rate 107, respiratory rate is 19, and her SpO2 was 91 percent on room air. Her GCS is an 11; she's alert but seems confused about the events. We're working her up under the sepsis protocol. Our primary impression is generalized abdominal pain, with a secondary of unspecified hypotension, likely causing the initial faint. We did inform the receiving doctor of our findings. For the pressure, we administered 0.42 milligrams of Nitroglycerin PO. She has a history of lupus but reports no known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt dizzy and feverish while teaching a class, which was followed by a near-syncopal episode.

transcript: Alright, we've got a 66-year-old female, coming from the vocational school dorms. Chief complaint is weakness. She's alert and oriented, GCS 15. She also complains of generalized abdominal pain and some pain in her left shoulder, rating it a 6 out of 10. She's tachycardic and hypotensive—heart rate is 130, BP is 91 over 56. Respirations are 18, and SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. We established an IV and have her on the monitor. She vomited once, so we did have to perform some brief airway suction. We administered zero-point-four milligrams of Nitroglycerin via the IV line. No known drug allergies. Her history includes Afib and GERD. We're about 10 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of weakness, prompting the call for EMS.

transcript: We're bringing you a 62-year-old male from an institutional setting. The initial call was for an eye problem, but on arrival, he was complaining of severe, 10 out of 10 generalized abdominal pain. He's also febrile and has an active nosebleed. He is alert but confused, GCS is a 10. Vitals are BP 146 over 87, heart rate 83, respirations 12, and SpO2 is 94 percent. We started an IV of normal saline, drew blood for you, placed him on a 3-lead, and did a 12-lead. Per protocol, we gave 2.14 milligrams of Magnesium Sulfate via nebulizer and 43.43 milligrams of Fentanyl orally for his pain. He has a history of high blood pressure and is allergic to codeine. We'll be there in two minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Initial call was for an eye problem, but patient's primary complaint on scene was severe 10/10 generalized abdominal pain, accompanied by fever, epistaxis, and confusion.

transcript: We're coming in with a 52-year-old female from a school, call was for a traumatic injury. On arrival, we found her exhibiting some strange and inexplicable behavior, she was alert but her GCS was only a 10. Staff said she fell. She's complaining of significant fatigue, but she denies any pain, so a zero out of ten. We did notice some hematemesis on her shirt. Vitals are BP 125 over 70, heart rate 98, respirations 22, and SpO2 is 100 percent. Given the presentation, we've just been doing continuous physical assessments. We administered a few medications: Aspirin, 370.81 milligrams PO, Sodium Bicarbonate, 42.42 milligrams IM, and Albuterol, 2.31 milligrams via IV. Her history includes asthma and peptic ulcer disease. No known drug allergies.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient fell at a school. On arrival, she was found with altered behavior and signs of hematemesis.

transcript: Alright, we're bringing you a 29-year-old male from his house, called for a near-syncopal episode. His roommate states the patient developed sudden, severe generalized abdominal pain, which he rated 10 out of 10, then became extremely lightheaded and nearly fainted. On arrival, he was alert, GCS of 15, but was hyperventilating. He just looks generally unwell. Vitals are stable: BP is 130 over 75, heart rate 69, respirations 13, and his SpO2 is 96 percent. He has a past medical history of GERD and says he takes omeprazole for it. He denies any allergies. We initiated an IV line and performed an evaluation. We're treating this as generalized abdominal pain under the General-Chest Pain protocol. Given some of the other findings on scene, we administered 1.61 milligrams of Naloxone via nebulizer, though with no significant change in his condition. We'll be there in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of severe, 10/10 generalized abdominal pain, followed by a near-syncopal episode.

transcript:
Hey doc, this is Medic 23 with a 90-year-old male coming from the nursing home on First Street. Chief complaint is severe abdominal pain. He was found by staff clutching his abdomen and moaning about an hour ago. He's got a history of diverticulitis and diabetes. No known drug allergies.

On exam, his abdomen is rigid and exquisitely tender. He rates his pain a ten out of ten. Vitals are: blood pressure 114 over 64, heart rate is tachy at 102, respirations 16, and SpO2 is 98 percent on room air. He's alert but very confused, GCS is a 9. He's also febrile and has been nauseous with some vomiting. We started a line, put him on the cardiac monitor and capnography. We're on the General-Diabetic Emergency protocol. Per that protocol, we administered 42.81 milligrams of Fentanyl sublingually for his pain. No change so far. ETA is about 5 minutes.

pcr_json.primary_impression: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found by nursing home staff clutching his abdomen and moaning about an hour prior to EMS arrival.

transcript:
Morning. We have a 37-year-old male, chief complaint of breathing problems. We picked him up at a local school. The patient was teaching when he felt his heart start racing and became dizzy and short of breath. He's got some associated cervicalgia and states he's had diarrhea for about a day. Our primary impression is a cardiac arrhythmia. He also has a headache.

He's alert, but GCS is 14. Vitals are BP 108 over 72, heart rate is 107, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 91 percent on room air. He rates his pain, mostly the neck pain, as a 2 out of 10. He has no known allergies or significant past medical history.

We did an initial assessment and have had him on continuous monitoring. We had a really hard time getting a line, so we did have to establish an IO in his right humerus. No meds given. We're following the General-Neonatal Care protocol per dispatch instructions, which seems incorrect, but we're rolling with it. Any questions?

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was teaching a class when he experienced a sudden onset of a racing heart, dizziness, and shortness of breath.

transcript: Hey, we have a 36-year-old female teacher from the middle school. Dispatched for a possible overdose, but it looks more like syncope. She collapsed in the teacher's lounge. On arrival, she was alert but very weak. Her blood pressure is 114 over 77, heart rate is tachy at 117, respirations 18, and O2 sat is 96 percent on room air. GCS is a 14. She denies any pain, so a 1 out of 10. She's also had a nosebleed and complains of some abdominal pain and a fever. We started an IV, checked her sugar, and put her on the cardiac monitor. Per protocol, we gave 18.4 milligrams of Heparin via nebulizer. She has a history of depression and takes Zoloft. She's allergic to codeine. She admits to drinking last night but denies any drug use today.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient felt weak and collapsed in the teacher's lounge at the school where she works.

transcript: We're en route with an 82-year-old male involved in a traffic incident in a school parking lot. He was the restrained driver in a minor collision. On arrival, he was confused with a GCS of 9. He's alert but not oriented. He's complaining of some other chest pain and is generally diaphoretic, with a pain scale of 5 out of 10. His vitals are a blood pressure of 114 over 66, heart rate is 132, and respirations are 20. His SpO2 is 96 percent. We initiated Sepsis protocol due to the tachycardia and altered mental status. We have him on the cardiac monitor, placed a cervical collar, and established IV access. We administered 347.58 milligrams of Aspirin IV for the chest pain and 4.79 milligrams of Ondansetron IM for nausea. His only listed allergy is to Ibuprofen.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was the driver in a minor motor vehicle collision; found confused, diaphoretic, and complaining of chest pain on EMS arrival.

transcript: We were called to the nursing home for a 100-year-old female with an altered mental status. Staff said she was fine this morning but became acutely confused and started complaining of dyspnea and generalized abdominal pain about an hour ago. She’s alert to voice but doesn't know where she is. Her GCS is a 9. Vitals are blood pressure 171 over 118, heart rate is tachycardic at 122, respirations 17, and SpO2 is 100 percent on room air. She denies any specific pain right now, so pain scale is a zero. We just performed a basic evaluation. Per the facility's chart, she has a history of hypertension and dementia. She’s allergic to penicillin. We have not administered any medications. We initiated transport under the neonatal care protocol which was a misclick on the tablet. Primary complaint is sick person.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient became acutely confused and started complaining of dyspnea and abdominal pain at her nursing home.

transcript: Good morning. We're bringing you a 20-year-old female from a school campus. The call came in as a well person check. We found her febrile and weak with unspecified abdominal pain. Our primary impression is unspecified angina pectoris, with a secondary of sepsis. She's tachycardic at 126, blood pressure 139 over 82, respirations are 18, and her SpO2 is 91 percent. So, here's the odd thing: she is alert, but her GCS is a 4. Her pain is an 8 out of 10. We initiated continuous physical assessment under the diabetic emergency protocol, given her history. She has no known allergies. We haven't administered any medications. We're about 10 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found febrile and weak with abdominal pain during a well person check at a school.

transcript: Hi, we're en route with a 91-year-old female found on the street after a near-fainting episode. Chief complaint was unconsciousness. She's extremely weak and has some dyspnea. She's not responsive to verbal commands, just moaning, so her GCS is a 7. She is, however, alert on the AVPU scale. We're giving her an 8 out of 10 for pain based on her grimacing. Her blood pressure is extremely high at 230 over 143. Heart rate is 75, respiratory rate is 15, and SpO2 is 100 percent. She experienced some nausea with vomiting, so we gave 4.05 milligrams of Ondansetron IV. No known allergies. She has a history of weakness. We're just doing continuous physical assessment. Based on the initial call, we started the General-Neonatal Care protocol but obviously this is a geriatric patient. Our primary impression is weakness.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a near-fainting episode and was found on the street.

transcript: Hey, we're coming in emergent with an 84-year-old female from her home. Original call was for chest pain, but she's also describing generalized abdominal pain. Her pain radiates to her left shoulder and she has some cervicalgia, rates it a 4 out of 10. She's fully alert, GCS 15. Family notes she had some diarrhea and a brief epistaxis before we got there. Vitals are blood pressure 148 over 94, heart rate 109, respirations 18, and SpO2 is 92 percent. We started an IV and checked a blood glucose, which was normal. For her nausea, we gave 4.73 milligrams of Ondansetron IM. We also gave 1.2 milligrams of EPINEPHrine via nebulizer per our neonatal care protocol. We have her on the stretcher. Her past history includes atrial fibrillation and GERD. No known drug allergies.

procedures: Moving a patient to a stretcher
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of chest pain at home, with radiation to the shoulder and neck, accompanied by nausea and a brief nosebleed.

transcript: Okay, we're bringing you a 62-year-old female from a residence, chief complaint is just 'sick person.' We found her pale and diaphoretic with generalized abdominal pain. She rates the pain a 6 out of 10. She's alert but confused, GCS is an 11. She has a history of diabetes and hypertension. She takes Metformin and Lisinopril. She is allergic to codeine. Her vitals are a bit sporty: blood pressure 169 over 93, heart rate is tachycardic at 120, respiratory rate is 23, and her SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. We did a 12-lead which shows sinus tach. Based on our chest pain protocol, we administered zero point three three milligrams of Nitroglycerin via nebulizer. Primary impression is abdominal pain, secondary is weakness. See you in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called 911 for feeling sick and was found pale and diaphoretic with generalized abdominal pain.

transcript: Hey, we've got a 66-year-old male, chief complaint of abdominal pain. He says it's been getting worse and now he feels it in his chest. Rates the pain a 7 out of 10. He's also feeling very fatigued and says he has a fever. Patient is alert and oriented, GCS is 15. We took his vitals: blood pressure 123 over 78, heart rate is 79, respiratory rate is 24, and his SpO2 is 93 percent on room air. We have started an IV and have him on the EKG monitor for continuous monitoring. Given the presentation, our primary impression is unspecified chest pain. We haven't given any meds. We're about 10 minutes away.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called due to several hours of worsening abdominal pain that began radiating to his chest, accompanied by fatigue and fever.

transcript: Okay, so we have a 5-year-old female, chief complaint of just being a sick person according to her mom. We found her at her residence. The parents state she was playing in her room and then suddenly became disoriented and couldn't speak clearly, which tracks with our finding of aphasia. She seems to have some pain in her arm but can't specify. Her vitals are a bit concerning—blood pressure is 181 over 124, heart rate 90, respirations are 20, and she's saturating at 96 percent on room air. She’s alert, GCS is 15, and denies pain, so a zero out of ten. We established an IV and ran a 12-lead which showed sinus tach. For her presumed nausea, we gave 3.26 milligrams of Ondansetron IM. She has no known allergies. Our primary impression is pain in limb, but obviously the neuro changes are the main concern.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was playing in her room and suddenly became disoriented and was unable to speak clearly.

transcript: Hi, we're inbound with a 66-year-old female from a single-family residence. Chief complaint was generalized weakness. She states this started after a sudden onset of severe abdominal pain, which she rates a 9 out of 10. She's also feeling very nauseous and did have some nausea with vomiting before we got there. She mentioned having a sore throat, consistent with acute pharyngitis, for a couple days. On exam, she's alert but confused, GCS is 11. She's tachycardic and tachypneic. Heart rate is 125, respirations are 22, BP is 142 over 84, and she's 96 percent on room air. We just did a basic evaluation procedure. For her symptoms, we administered 0.4 milligrams of Nitroglycerin IV and also 0.83 milligrams of EPINEPHrine 0.1 MG/ML orally. Our primary impression is an acute abdomen. We'll be there in five.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced a sudden onset of severe abdominal pain leading to weakness, nausea, and vomiting.

transcript: Morning. We're bringing you a 78-year-old female from a school, it's an adult day program. The initial call was for chest pain, but on scene her main issue is altered mental status. She seems weak, is complaining of nausea and vomiting, and some upper abdominal pain. She denies any actual chest pain, so pain scale is zero. Her past history is notable for a prior CVA and hypertension. She is allergic to iodine. Her vitals are 155 over 95 for blood pressure, heart rate is 90, respirations 18, SpO2 is 94 percent on room air. She's alert but confused, GCS is a 13. We performed a 12-lead, no ST elevation. We did continuous assessment en route. Based on our General-Neonatal Care protocol, we gave Aspirin 329.97 milligrams sublingually, Ipratropium 0.49 milligrams via nebulizer, and Fentanyl 51.37 milligrams IV for her abdominal pain.

pcr_json.events_leading: Staff at an adult day program called for chest pain, but upon EMS arrival the patient's primary complaint was altered mental status accompanied by nausea and abdominal pain.

transcript: Hey Doc, we're bringing in a 56-year-old female from the local high school. We were dispatched for a medical alert alarm activation. Staff found her; she said she suddenly felt very dizzy and short of breath with some angina-like chest pressure. Her initial vitals were a blood pressure of 163 over 97, heart rate 106, respirations 24, and her SpO2 was low at 90 percent on room air. She was alert but a little confused, GCS of 14. We placed her on the cardiac monitor and established an IV. Per our anaphylaxis protocol for her shortness of breath, we gave 340.78 milligrams of Aspirin via nebulizer. We also administered 51.28 milligrams of Fentanyl IV for her discomfort. She says she has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril for it. No known allergies. We're about 2 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient activated her medical alert at work after a sudden onset of dizziness, shortness of breath, and chest pressure.

transcript: Hi, we are transporting a 99-year-old male from the nursing home on Elm Street. The call was for a well person check, staff was just worried about him. We found him with an altered mental status and shortness of breath. He has a known history of COPD. He's alert but confused, GCS is a 14. He also has had some diarrhea today. He denies any pain. His vitals show a heart rate of 109, blood pressure 123 over 83, respirations are 18, and his SpO2 is 92 percent on room air. He's on our cardiac monitor and we're performing continuous assessment. He was extremely anxious and agitated, so we gave 51.94 milligrams of Fentanyl via nebulizer, 0.52 milligrams of Hydromorphone IV, and 2.76 milligrams of Midazolam PO to calm him down. That seems to have helped. We'll be there in about ten.

pcr_json.events_leading: Called for a well person check at a nursing home due to staff worries. Patient was found to have an altered mental status and shortness of breath.

transcript: Alright, we have a 54-year-old female complaining of nausea and vomiting. She called because she felt extremely weak and couldn't get up. She has a history of end-stage renal disease and epilepsy. She's alert and oriented, GCS 15. She’s complaining of diffuse, unspecified pain which she rates a 5 out of 10. She also has a cough and feels like her heart is racing. Vitals are stable: blood pressure is 129 over 80, heart rate is 93, respirations are 19, and her SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. She’s allergic to Codeine. We performed a 12-lead EKG, which showed a normal sinus rhythm with some tachycardia. No medications were given en route. We're just bringing her in for evaluation. Seems like she missed her dialysis appointment yesterday, which is likely the cause.

pcr_json.secondary_impression: Epilepsy, unspecified, not intractable, without status epilepticus
pcr_json.events_leading: Patient called EMS due to feeling extremely weak after missing her dialysis appointment yesterday.

transcript: Good morning. We have a 94-year-old female from a residence, dispatched for a possible carbon monoxide inhalation. On scene, we found the patient alert, GCS 15, but she was definitely aphasic and complaining of nausea. Family witnessed a seizure-like event just before our arrival. She denies any pain. Vitals are: blood pressure 166 over 113, heart rate 94, respirations 19, and SpO2 is 96 percent. We performed a blood glucose check, which was normal. We also placed her on a 3-lead monitor. We did give 14.56 milligrams of Glucose sublingually. Her history includes a past CVA. Based on the family's report, our primary impression is 'other seizures.'

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was found with aphasia and nausea after family witnessed a seizure-like event; initial call was for possible carbon monoxide inhalation.

transcript: Morning, we're bringing in a 43-year-old male from his apartment for a psych evaluation. His chief complaint was listed as a behavioral problem. We found him alert and oriented, GCS of 15. He's complaining of some non-specific chest pain, rates it a 1 out of 10, and feels some weakness and shortness of breath. He also mentioned feeling nauseous and had an episode of hematemesis, looked like coffee grounds, before our arrival. He has a history of anxiety and GERD, for which he takes Omeprazole. He reports an allergy to Sulfa drugs. Vitals are pretty stable: BP is 125 over 80, heart rate is 58, respirations 17, and his SpO2 is 95 percent on room air. We just performed an evaluation, no medications were given. He needs checking out for the GI bleed and the behavioral component.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient experienced anxiety along with chest pain, weakness, nausea, and an episode of hematemesis at his apartment.

transcript: Hey doc, we're coming in with a 62-year-old male from the reform school. Staff called for a sick person. He's alert, GCS 15, but appears very weak and anxious. His main complaints are weakness, some mild chest pain he rates a 1 out of 10, and dysuria. He's febrile and hyperventilating. Vitals are BP 127 over 85, heart rate is 69, but his respiratory rate is 24 and he's hypoxic at 87 percent on room air. We started a line in his left AC. No meds given en route. His history is significant for type 2 diabetes and BPH. He is allergic to Sulfa drugs. Given the presentation, we're concerned for sepsis, possibly urosepsis. We've informed you as the receiving doctor.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient became progressively weak and febrile at his residence, prompting staff to call EMS.

transcript: Hey, ED staff. We're bringing you a 100-year-old male from an assisted living facility. The chief complaint was abdominal pain, but he's also pointing to his chest now, so we're going with other chest pain. He also had an episode of epistaxis just before we arrived. He's alert with a GCS of 15, denies any specific pain right now so a zero out of ten, but he also has some cervicalgia. His vitals are a little concerning: BP is 158 over 91, heart rate is 90, but his respiratory rate is only 9. SpO2 is good at 100 percent on room air. He has a history of hypertension and GERD, with no known drug allergies. We just got an IV established and have him on the 3-lead monitor. We followed the sepsis protocol but haven't given any medications. We are about 10 minutes out.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient developed abdominal pain, chest pain, and a nosebleed at his assisted living facility.

transcript: Hey team, this is a 28-year-old male from a construction site. He fell approximately 10 feet off a ladder. Chief complaint is traumatic injury, but he's really focused on his shortness of breath. He's alert, GCS of 15, denies any pain, so pain scale is a zero. Lungs are clear but diminished bilaterally. His vitals are pretty impressive—BP is 196 over 113, heart rate is 97, respirations 16, and his SpO2 is 92 percent on room air. We've had him on the cardiac monitor, showing sinus tach. He says he feels some malaise and a bit of chest tightness, like angina. Past medical history is significant for anxiety, for which he takes Zoloft. He has no known allergies. Our primary impression is shortness of breath, possible secondary to the fall. We've just done a physical assessment and are monitoring him closely.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient fell approximately 10 feet from a ladder at a construction site.

transcript: We're bringing you a 69-year-old male involved in a minor traffic incident at a school. He's complaining of acute pain, which is our primary impression. He says the pain is a 2 out of 10. He also has a cough and feels like he has a fever. He's alert, but his GCS is a 12. Vitals are BP 161 over 99, heart rate of 97, respirations 11, and SpO2 is 96 percent. His past medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes. He's allergic to sulfa drugs. We did a 12-lead EKG and checked his blood glucose. No medications were administered en route. We'll be there in about 10 minutes.

pcr_json.events_leading: Patient was involved in a low-speed motor vehicle collision in a school parking lot and began experiencing pain shortly thereafter.

transcript: Hey, we're here with a 77-year-old female from the reform school on 5th street. Staff called for a behavioral issue. We found her pretty agitated, complaining of a fever and some unspecified pain in her shoulder, though she rates it a zero out of ten. She's alert but seems confused, GCS is a 12. Her initial vitals were: BP 138 over 86, heart rate 108, respirations 13, and she was saturating 94 percent on room air. We just did a physical assessment. She has a history of dementia and hypertension. She's allergic to penicillin. On the way over she started feeling nauseous, so we gave her 4.23 milligrams of Ondansetron IV. We're bringing her in for evaluation under the sepsis protocol due to the fever and altered mental status.

transcript: We have a 99-year-old male we picked up from a bus stop on the highway. Bystanders called because he looked unwell. Chief complaint is non-traumatic back pain, but we're thinking more of a fever. He's got a cough, nausea with vomiting, and some abdominal pain. He also had a bit of a nosebleed. Initial vitals: heart rate is 137, BP 111 over 76, respirations 17, and his SpO2 was 90 percent. GCS is 14. He rates his pain a 3 out of 10. Given his symptoms, we followed the Diabetic Emergency protocol. We performed a 12-lead and have had him on continuous pulse oximetry. For his nausea, we gave Zofran 3.93 milligrams sublingually. We also gave Albuterol 2.22 milligrams sublingually. For his pain, we administered Fentanyl, 50.16 milligrams IV. He has a history of dementia and is allergic to Codeine.

transcript: Hi, we're coming in with a 23-year-old male from the college campus. He had a syncopal episode during a lecture. His friends state he was complaining of a fever and some back pain before he passed out. On our assessment, he's definitely hypotensive and bradycardic. BP is 85 over 48, heart rate is 49. Respirations are 17, SpO2 is 98 percent. He's a little out of it, GCS is 12. He denies any pain right now. We have him on digital capnography. He has no past medical history to speak of and is only allergic to sulfa drugs. We're thinking this is syncope and collapse, but the vitals are concerning. No medications were administered.

transcript: We're bringing you a 43-year-old female from a local school, initial call was for a stroke. On arrival she was alert but confused, GCS is a 9. She's complaining of significant angina, an eight out of ten pain, and she's warm to the touch with a fever. She also has difficulty walking. We found some minor hemorrhage from a scalp lac, probably from when she fell. Vitals are 137 over 88, heart rate is 89, respirations are 22, and SpO2 is 99 percent. We started a line and got a 12-lead. Per online med control, we administered 4.34 milligrams of Morphine PO for the pain, and also 2.24 milligrams of Albuterol PO. We're thinking this is a cardiac arrest of some kind. Per dispatch, we were on the General-Neonatal Care protocol. Her allergies include sulfa drugs.

transcript: Hey team, we've got a 40-year-old male, found down at the local high school. Dispatch was for a cardiac arrest, but he was breathing on our arrival. He’s pretty out of it, GCS is an 8. Uh, events leading to this… he’s a teacher, was giving a lecture when he just collapsed. No apparent trauma. Vitals are blood pressure 179 over 100, heart rate is 96, respirations 19, and he’s saturating at 97 percent on room air. He's alert but confused, so AVPU is 'A'. He denies any pain. He is complaining of some generalized muscle weakness and some abdominal pain. We did place a C-collar as a precaution and started an IV in his right AC. Our primary impression is syncope and collapse, maybe secondary to that generalized muscle weakness. We didn't give much... just, uh, four point five-two milligrams of Ondansetron PO for nausea. Also gave DuoNeb, two point oh-four milligrams, sublingually per a new protocol we're trialing. It's weird, I know.
